summary: Exhaustive, multi-agent audit to scope replacing manifold-csg with a classify-then-dispatch pure-Rust path for IFC cut-openings
logs: Phase 1 complete: 15 call sites, 13 host shapes, 5 shape combinations surveyed.
Phase 2/3 complete: 8 designs, 32 verdicts, 20 flagged as refuted.
Phase 4 critic: 18 missed shapes, 13 missed failure modes, 20 cross-cutting concerns.

call_sites.input_origin: Flat-buffer mesh: vertices as [x,y,z,...] (f32 slice), indices as triangle list (u32 slice). Originates from IFC geometry extractors (extrusions, boolean operands, CSG primitives).
call_sites.output_consumer: Used by subtract_many to build Vec<Manifold> containing host and cutters (lines 114, 129). Also used in tests to verify post-boolean volume invariants.

call_sites.input_origin: Manifold instance from batch_difference (line 132) or round-trip pass-through (line 121)
call_sites.output_consumer: Decodes to (Vec<f32>, Vec<u32>); return value of subtract_many, destined for ProductMesh.vertices/indices or test assertions

call_sites.input_origin: Vec<Manifold> where all[0]=host, all[1..]=cutters. All built via build_manifold from user buffers at lines 114, 129. Meshes come from: IfcBooleanClippingResult operands, IfcCsgSolid branches, IfcExtrudedAreaSolid, IfcRevolvedAreaSolid, etc.
call_sites.output_consumer: Result Manifold decoded via manifold_to_buffers (line 133) into (Vec<f32>, Vec<u32>); returned as Ok((verts, idx)) to caller

call_sites.input_origin: host: ProductMesh.vertices/indices subsetted to boolean_first_operand segments via assemble_submesh. cutters: boolean_second_operand segments (excluding halfspace planes, which go through halfspace_clip instead). Input comes from IfcBooleanClippingResult tree where operands are IfcOpeningElement, IfcExtrudedAreaSolid, IfcHalfSpaceSolid, IfcPolygonalBoundedHalfSpace geometries.
call_sites.output_consumer: On Ok: mesh.vertices/indices replaced with result, mesh.segments collapsed to single cut_openings entry, mesh.parts cleared. On Err: returns Outcome::Fallback, mesh untouched (reveal-all fallback).

call_sites.input_origin: host_mesh: ProductMesh from held_hosts map (after in-rep apply already executed). cutter_buffers: Vec<(Vec<f32>, Vec<u32>)> with vertices translated by (opening.mesh_anchor - host.mesh_anchor) to align into host's local coordinate frame. Cutters come from IfcOpeningElement geometries linked via IfcRelVoidsElement relationship.
call_sites.output_consumer: On Ok((verts, idx)): host_mesh.vertices/indices replaced, single cut_openings segment created, mesh.parts cleared. Returns (host_mesh, Outcome::Cut). On Err: if in-rep cut succeeded, returns (host_mesh, Outcome::Cut); else (host_mesh, Outcome::Fallback).

call_sites.input_origin: Manifold from build_manifold at line 205 (1x1x1 axis-aligned cube)
call_sites.output_consumer: Test assertion: volume ~= 1.0 validates closed-manifold invariant

call_sites.input_origin: Manifold from subtract at line 245 (host 1x1x1 cube minus contained 0.5x0.5x0.5 cube)
call_sites.output_consumer: Test assertion: volume ~= 0.875 = 1.0 - 0.125 validates boolean subtraction reduces volume correctly

call_sites.input_origin: Manifold from subtract at line 270 (host cube, opening partially outside host boundary)
call_sites.output_consumer: Test assertion: volume ~= 0.875 validates robust handling of boundary-straddling cutters

call_sites.input_origin: Manifold from subtract_many at line 289 (host 2x1x1 box, two disjoint 0.5x0.5x0.5 openings)
call_sites.output_consumer: Test assertion: volume ~= 1.75 = 2.0 - 2*0.125 validates batch_difference with multiple cutters

call_sites.input_origin: Manifold from subtract_many at line 310 (host with empty cutter list, round-trip via line 121)
call_sites.output_consumer: Test assertion: volume ~= 1.0 validates no-cutter round-trip preserves volume and topology

call_sites.input_origin: Manifold from build_manifold at line 130 (post-apply ProductMesh: 1000x200x3000 wall minus 500x200x2000 opening)
call_sites.output_consumer: Test assertion: volume ~= 0.4 m³ (0.6 - 0.2) end-to-end validation of cut_openings::apply on IfcBooleanClippingResult

call_sites.input_origin: Manifold from build_manifold at line 202 (post-apply wall clipped by IfcHalfSpaceSolid via halfspace_clip module)
call_sites.output_consumer: Test assertion: volume ~= 0.3 m³ validates pure-Rust halfspace_clip output remains closed manifold

call_sites.input_origin: Manifold from build_manifold at line 311 (3-level nested IfcBooleanClippingResult with IfcHalfSpaceSolid cutters, output of apply)
call_sites.output_consumer: Test assertion: volume ~= 0.05 m³ validates GH#39 fix: halfspace_clip handles stacked clips that would collapse under manifold-csg

call_sites.input_origin: Manifold from build_manifold at line 526 (post-flush CrossProductCut: wall with IfcRelVoidsElement opening)
call_sites.output_consumer: Test assertion: volume ~= 0.4 m³ end-to-end validation of cross-product opening subtraction

call_sites.input_origin: Manifold from build_manifold on deeply nested IfcBooleanClippingResult
call_sites.output_consumer: Test assertion validating volume reduction on complex multi-level boolean trees

invariants: Manifold output is a closed mesh: every edge is shared by exactly two triangles, outward-facing winding preserved
batch_difference reduces volume: for disjoint or overlapping subtraction, result volume = host volume - union(cutter volumes within host)
Deterministic vertex deduplication: manifold round-trips collapse coincident vertices and normalize topology consistently (line 119-121 comment)
Boundary-straddling cutters are handled robustly: when cutter extends beyond host, intersection is computed correctly (line 269 test validates this)
Multiple cutters folded into single batch operation are more robust than sequential subtractions due to single CSG tree vs per-pair evaluation
Round-trip through manifold preserves closed-manifold invariant: build_manifold -> to_mesh_f32 output is manifold-valid for downstream ops (line 311 test)
All mesh buffers are flat and tightly packed: [x,y,z,x,y,z...] for verts, triangle indices have no gaps or padding
failure_modes: GH#39: Manifold's batch_difference propagates bbox-derived tolerance from oversized half-space bounded boxes, causing tolerance inflation across 13+ deep trees and result collapse to empty. Mitigation: halfspace_clip module provides pure-Rust plane-clip as first-class primitive, keeping collapsed half-spaces out of manifold entirely (lines 81-86 cut_openings.rs comment)

GH#28/1714: When half-space is bounded to host AABB, per-vertex predicates treat all positions as coincident due to large bbox-derived tolerance, result collapses to empty (halfspace_clip.rs line 13 comment). Mitigation: same as GH#39 - bypass manifold for half-spaces

Non-manifold input rejection: open shells, self-intersecting triangles, inconsistent winding - manifold's from_mesh_f32 validates and rejects with CsgError. Caller surfaces error rather than silently substituting fallback (line 36-39 comment, line 142 fallback handling)

Degenerate triangles and slivers near clip planes: halfspace_clip uses 1e-3 mm tolerance to conservatively treat near-plane vertices as outside, dropping sub-mm geometry to keep cap topology crisp (line 50 halfspace_clip.rs). This is below building-element tolerance (1 mm) (lines 45-50 comment)

Empty result handling: subtract_many returns empty (Vec::new(), Vec::new()) when cutters fully consume host. Call site must decide if that signals completion or error based on context (line 97-99 halfspace_clip.rs comment, line 131-134 cut_openings.rs logic)

Pathological input: multiple edges sharing a start vertex degenerate case in boundary stitching (line 318-319 halfspace_clip.rs comment) - graph may fail to close, loops return incomplete, silent guard at line 334

Coplanar face handling: no explicit coplanar-aware snap/merge in manifold - user must pre-merge or accept topological artifacts at true coplanar transitions. Manifold 0.2.x doesn't guarantee numerical robustness on axis-aligned coplanar geometry.

Far-from-origin precision: manifold internally uses f64; input is f32. Vertices in building coordinates (world frame) can exceed 1e6 mm causing f32 quantization. Mitigation: ifcfast rebases to local mesh_anchor frame so vertices stay <10m apart (line 269-273 cut_openings.rs comment)

Fallback on manifold error: cut_openings::apply catches CsgKernelError and returns Outcome::Fallback, leaving original mesh unchanged (line 142). Cross-product fold preserves in-rep cut on error if in-rep succeeded, else Fallback (lines 440-449 cut_openings.rs)

No validation of mesh winding before manifold call: caller must ensure outward-facing CCW winding. Reversal post-boolean is not detected (contrast with reveal-all which tags source, enabling caller to verify).

common_combinations.source_pattern: Revit wall/slab/beam with door, window, or recess as a separately-modelled IfcOpeningElement linked via IfcRelVoidsElement. The opening is itself a straight rectangular extrusion. Tekla and ifcopenshell-python emit the same shape. This is the dominant case — every door, window, riser, and floor penetration in a Revit IFC export lands here. cut_openings.rs cross-product path (CrossProductCut::flush) folds via geom::csg::subtract_many.

Revit wall under a sloped roof, a stepped slab edge, or a wall meeting a non-orthogonal ceiling — Revit authors this as IfcBooleanClippingResult(extrusion, IfcPolygonalBoundedHalfSpace). The polygonal boundary is the in-plane silhouette of the cut region (typically 5-8 m oversized vs the wall footprint for safety); the BaseSurface plane is the slope. In a Sannergata wall this can stack 13-deep (one BCR per slope facet). cut_openings.rs apply() routes these through halfspace_clip::clip_by_plane (NOT Manifold) since v0.4.32 (GH #39).

Older Revit exports and some ArchiCAD models that didn't bother to bound the half-space. Same role as the polygonal-bounded variant (slope cut) but with an unbounded base surface. We materialise it as a 20m x 20m visualisation quad in boolean::halfspace_solid; cut_openings.rs routes it through halfspace_clip::clip_by_plane via the halfspace_plane:{agree|disagree} tag.

Revit columns and some beams author the opening directly inline as IfcBooleanClippingResult(column_extrusion, recess_extrusion) instead of going through IfcRelVoidsElement. ArchiCAD walls sometimes use this pattern for sills. Handled by cut_openings.rs apply() in-rep path; the extractor already tagged operands as boolean_first_operand / boolean_second_operand.

Tekla precast panels and ArchiCAD complex walls: the host arrives as a brep (often because Tekla can't represent the panel's compound shape as a single sweep) and the door/window is a separate IfcOpeningElement. Cross-product cut via CrossProductCut::flush works as long as the brep is closed and manifold; non-manifold breps fall back to Outcome::Fallback (reveal-all kept).

IFC4 Reference View exports (mostly newer Revit + ifcopenshell using tessellation profile). Host is a flat face set, opening is still modelled parametrically. CSG kernel handles it if the face set is closed; flat face sets that are surface-only (open shells) drop to Outcome::Fallback.

Sannergata-class buildings: walls under a hipped/dormered roof can accumulate 10-13 nested IfcBooleanClippingResult layers, each with one half-space cutter. Each cutter is_cutter-classified by the `boolean_second_operand` chain link anywhere in the source tag, so the partitioner sees all 13 as cutters and clips sequentially. Manifold-free path since GH #39 — Manifold's tolerance propagation would empty the result.

Some Tekla MEP penetration objects and ifcopenshell-authored test models. Real Revit exports almost never use CsgPrimitive3D as a cutter — they prefer IfcExtrudedAreaSolid even for cylindrical pipe penetrations. Dispatched normally; csg_primitive::* returns a closed mesh, subtract_many handles it.

Authoring tools that emit curved sweeps for fillet recesses or chamfered openings. Revit doesn't author these as cutters in practice. IfcRevolvedAreaSolid is handled by mesh::revolved since v0.4.20-ish; IfcSurfaceCurveSweptAreaSolid is still unhandled and would land as an Unhandled fragment, breaking the cut.

IfcBooleanResult with DIFFERENCE/UNION/INTERSECTION operators (not just CLIPPING). Real-world emission is rare outside synthetic / IfcOpenShell test fixtures — most authoring tools use IfcBooleanClippingResult specifically. Our dispatcher treats both the same way (boolean::boolean_result), and the operator field is currently ignored — the second operand is always treated as a cutter.

cutter_shapes.frequency_other: dominant in Tekla/ArchiCAD/ifcopenshell — same as host. Door, window and recess voids are nearly always straight extrusions of a rectangle/poly profile.
cutter_shapes.notes: When this cutter appears as boolean_second_operand it goes through Manifold via csg::subtract_many. As an IfcOpeningElement product it folds via CrossProductCut::flush. Profile is normally IfcRectangleProfileDef or IfcArbitraryClosedProfileDef; IfcArbitraryProfileDefWithVoids appears for windows with mullions.

cutter_shapes.frequency_other: occasional — Revit-dominant. ArchiCAD and Tekla prefer separately-modelled opening extrusions for slope cuts. ifcopenshell-python rarely emits this.
cutter_shapes.notes: Author quirks: (1) Revit sizes the polygon ~5x5 m larger than the host wall footprint for safety — so the polygon's silhouette is not informative about the cut region. (2) BaseSurface.Position.Axis is the cutting-plane normal; arg[2].Position is the polygon's local XY frame and can diverge (GH #52). (3) AgreementFlag follows ifcopenshell's !flag convention (T = keep +Z side). (4) Polygon can wind CW (Revit) or CCW; our path doesn't depend on winding because we read the slab's first-triangle normal.

cutter_shapes.frequency_other: rare — most tools bound their half-spaces. ifcopenshell test fixtures occasionally use it.
cutter_shapes.notes: BaseSurface must be an IfcPlane (we currently reject anything else). Visualised as a 20m x 20m square slab in agreement direction; cut uses the slab's first-triangle normal as the plane normal. No polygonal boundary so the entire half-space is removed.

cutter_shapes.frequency_other: rare — appears in some structural-steel exports for tapered beam haunches.
cutter_shapes.notes: Subtype of IfcExtrudedAreaSolid with a different end profile. Not in our dispatcher — would emit unhandled:IFCEXTRUDEDAREASOLIDTAPERED. As a cutter this would prevent the cut. Tracked in the schema_supertypes static map but not in mesh_item dispatch.

cutter_shapes.frequency_other: occasional in mechanical / MEP exports (turned shafts, lathed fittings).
cutter_shapes.notes: Handled, tagged 'revolved'. As a cutter would route through csg::subtract_many. Real-world use as a CUTTER is essentially zero — appears almost always as a host shape for railings, bowls, columns.
Tracked in schema_supertypes but no mesh handler. README/AGENTS.md both call it out as known unhandled. As a cutter would block the cut.
Used for pipes and reinforcing bars. Not currently a cutter in any file I have evidence of. Hot follow-on for MEP (GH #48 covered IfcArcIndex inside the directrix, not the swept-disk itself).

cutter_shapes.frequency_other: occasional in ifcopenshell-python fixtures and Tekla cast-in items.
cutter_shapes.notes: Handled. As a cutter it routes through subtract_many. Real-world frequency as cutter is near zero — authoring tools prefer extrusions even for box-shaped voids.

cutter_shapes.frequency_other: occasional — pipe penetrations in some Tekla exports, ifcopenshell tutorials.
cutter_shapes.notes: Handled. The most plausible CSG primitive cutter (round pipe penetrations through a slab/wall) but Revit emits these as IfcExtrudedAreaSolid of an IfcCircleProfileDef instead.
Handled for completeness. Real-world frequency as cutters is zero.

cutter_shapes.frequency_other: rare — appears in some IFC4 conformance fixtures.
cutter_shapes.notes: Subtype of IfcHalfSpaceSolid with an explicit bounding box. Tracked in schema_supertypes but no mesh handler. Would fall through as unhandled.

cutter_shapes.frequency_other: occasional — Tekla compound voids, ArchiCAD complex openings.
cutter_shapes.notes: As a cutter the brep must be closed/manifold for subtract_many to succeed. Open shells go to Outcome::Fallback. IfcAdvancedBrep is approximated by its outer polyloop (advanced_brep_approx tag) — curved surfaces collapse to planar polygons, which can produce wrong cut volumes.

cutter_shapes.frequency_other: common in IFC4 Reference View exports.
cutter_shapes.notes: Used mainly as host shapes; rare as cutters. If closed, subtract_many succeeds. The fbsm/sbsm wrapped variants (faceset_fbsm/faceset_sbsm) are typically open surface models — not viable as cutters.

host_shapes.frequency_other: dominant in ArchiCAD, Tekla, ifcopenshell-python. Every wall, slab, column, beam authored as a sweep of a 2D profile.
host_shapes.notes: Profile types: IfcRectangleProfileDef (dominant), IfcArbitraryClosedProfileDef (poly walls), IfcCircleProfileDef (round columns/pipes), IfcArbitraryProfileDefWithVoids (walls with cavities). Direction is usually +Z. IfcExtrudedAreaSolidTapered subtype is NOT handled — falls through as unhandled.

host_shapes.frequency_other: common in ArchiCAD (sloped walls), less common in Tekla (which prefers brep). ifcopenshell-python rarely emits these.
host_shapes.tessellation_path: crates/core/src/mesh/boolean.rs::boolean_result (recurses into mesh_item for both operands; emits boolean_first_operand / boolean_second_operand segments)
host_shapes.notes: Trees can nest deeply (Sannergata: 13-deep for tilted-roof walls). is_cutter() in cut_openings.rs walks the `|`-delimited source chain and flags any link == boolean_second_operand. Composite is not cached (operand caches do the work).

host_shapes.frequency_other: dominant in Tekla precast/structural-steel. Common in ArchiCAD complex meshes.
host_shapes.notes: Closed polyhedral shell. As a cut host, manifold-ness determines whether subtract_many succeeds. Some Tekla brep exports have small T-junctions / non-manifold edges that fail the CSG kernel — caller sees Outcome::Fallback and reveal-all is preserved.

host_shapes.frequency_other: rare — IFC4 DesignTransferView only. Used for NURBS-bounded volumes (curved facades).
host_shapes.notes: Curved bounding surfaces (IfcBSplineSurface, IfcCylindricalSurface) are flattened to their outer polyloop — wrong for curved facades. As a cut host this can give wrong volumes silently. Documented as approximation in source_tags.

host_shapes.frequency_other: common in IFC4 Reference View exports (newer Revit defaults, ifcopenshell tessellation profile).
host_shapes.notes: Indexed polygon list — faces with N vertices, ear-cut to triangles. Usually closed when authored as solids. Heavy use for furniture, fixtures, complex MEP components.
Already-triangulated; we just unpack. Same caveats as IfcPolygonalFaceSet for cut viability (closed = subtractable).

host_shapes.frequency_other: common — family/library instancing across all authoring tools.
host_shapes.tessellation_path: crates/core/src/mesh/mapped.rs::expand (composite; recurses into the mapped representation's items)
host_shapes.notes: Composite — not cached at the outer level; inner cache is per-source. A wall referencing an IfcRepresentationMap can still have an IfcRelVoidsElement opening folded against the expanded mesh. The transform composition is t_target * t_origin.

host_shapes.frequency_other: occasional — railings, curved walls, MEP fittings, bowls.
host_shapes.notes: Profile is swept around an axis. Closed solid; viable as a cut host.

host_shapes.frequency_other: occasional — synthetic models, some Tekla cast-in items.
host_shapes.notes: Wrapper around a tree of IfcBooleanResult / IfcCsgPrimitive3D nodes. Recurses through the same dispatch.
Typically open surface models, not closed solids. Not viable as cut hosts — subtract_many will fail and Outcome::Fallback fires.
Subtype of IfcSweptAreaSolid. Falls through as Unhandled fragment. Documented in AGENTS.md as a known unhandled type. Cuts against this host are impossible until a handler ships.

host_shapes.frequency_other: common in MEP exports (pipes, conduit, rebar).
host_shapes.notes: Indirectly addressed by GH #48 (IfcArcIndex inside the directrix polycurve) but the sweep itself isn't implemented. As a host: pipe penetrations are typically cross-product openings into walls, so the pipe is a SIBLING product not a host — frequency as a cut host is low.
Lines/polylines/points — not a solid, never a viable cut host. Listed for completeness; an IfcWall whose body is a curveset is malformed.

notable_specialized_libs.scope: 2D polygon Boolean (union, intersection, difference, XOR), polyline clip/slice, offsetting. f32/f64/i32/i64 APIs. Even-odd / non-zero fill rules. Sweep-line core, handles self-intersection and OGC-valid output. Powers georust/geo's overlay engine.
notable_specialized_libs.maturity_signal: v7.0.0 released 2026-06-01, very active development (6.x and 7.x releases in 2026), ~188 GitHub stars, adopted by georust/geo. Production-quality.

notable_specialized_libs.scope: 2D polygon Boolean (intersection, difference, union, XOR) via Angus Johnson's Clipper.
notable_specialized_libs.maturity_signal: 10+ releases; widely used. C++ Clipper core is battle-tested but reintroduces a C++ dependency.

notable_specialized_libs.scope: 2D polygon Boolean and offsetting (Vatti-derived). Successor to Clipper1.
notable_specialized_libs.maturity_signal: Multiple Rust binding crates exist; Clipper2 itself is mature. C++ core defeats pure-Rust goal.

notable_specialized_libs.scope: 3D mesh CSG (union, difference, intersection, XOR) via BSP trees (CSG.js port) plus experimental BMesh backend. 2D sketches via geo crate. OpenSCAD-like API. Integrates nalgebra/Parry. 32/64-bit float, WASM-able.
notable_specialized_libs.maturity_signal: v0.20.1 (2025-07-24), 231 stars, 998 commits, active. Pre-1.0 but the most mature pure-Rust CSG kernel.

notable_specialized_libs.scope: Guaranteed-manifold mesh Boolean (union, intersection, difference), Minkowski, hull, SDF, OBJ I/O. CSG tree with lazy eval. Algorithm based on Julian Smith's dissertation.
notable_specialized_libs.maturity_signal: manifold C++ is industry-grade (used by FreeCAD discussion, OpenSCAD); Rust bindings are early (manifold3d 0.0.6, manifold-csg early). Reintroduces C++ dep.

notable_specialized_libs.scope: Mesh processing incl. boolean ops (union/subtract/intersect) via voxel/implicit modeling pipeline. Voxel remeshing, volume offset. STL/OBJ/PLY I/O.
notable_specialized_libs.maturity_signal: 95 stars, 256 commits, ongoing. Voxel-based booleans are lossy for thin features and overkill for halfspace cuts. Specialized rather than production-standard.

notable_specialized_libs.scope: 2D path tessellation (fill/stroke) into triangle buffers. SVG-style paths, beziers, arcs flattened by tolerance. Not a Boolean engine.
notable_specialized_libs.maturity_signal: Mature, widely used in Rust 2D graphics. Complements but does not replace a polygon-clipping step.

notable_specialized_libs.scope: 2D Delaunay and Constrained Delaunay Triangulation with robust predicates. Optional hierarchy for locate/insert.
notable_specialized_libs.maturity_signal: Mature, the de-facto Rust CDT crate. Good fallback when earcutr can't handle a polygon (holes with degeneracies).

notable_specialized_libs.scope: 2D geometry primitives, planar predicates, and (since recent versions) Boolean overlay backed by i_overlay. Simple Features.
notable_specialized_libs.maturity_signal: Core Rust geo ecosystem crate. Stable, broad adoption.

notable_specialized_libs.scope: Ear-clipping polygon triangulation (port of Mapbox earcut). Handles holes. Not Boolean.
notable_specialized_libs.maturity_signal: Mature; we already use it. Limits: not robust to severely degenerate input.

approaches.algorithm: Generic BRep Boolean via taxonomy::boolean_result lowered to OCCT BRepAlgoAPI_Cut. No prism-specific shortcut; both operands become BReps and OCCT runs the general topology engine.
approaches.handles_degeneracies: OCCT tolerance-based; can fail on coplanar faces and self-intersections; iterative flattening of chained ops when 8+ operands accumulate.

approaches.algorithm: Both prisms converted to CGAL Polyhedron_3 -> Nef_polyhedron_3; openings unioned via Nef_nary_union_3; final A -= union performed in Nef. Result converted back to polyhedron.
approaches.handles_degeneracies: Exact; robust but very slow on large meshes (Nef conversion dominates).

approaches.algorithm: Mesh-mesh subtraction. Triangle-triangle intersection rebuilds a single planar arrangement per shared plane, then ray-casting from triangle centroids classifies each face as INSIDE/OUTSIDE/BOUNDARY. Three ray directions with consistency check. clipSubtract() / clipJoin() entry points.
approaches.csg_engine: fuzzy-bools (header-only, GLM + CDT). No BSP, no Nef.
approaches.handles_degeneracies: Tuned for coplanar surfaces common in CAD (the whole reason fuzzy-bools exists); fuzzy tolerance bands.

approaches.algorithm: Delegates to ifcopenshell at IFC-write time; in-Blender editing uses Blender's BMesh + Bool modifier (Carve/Fast/Exact selectable). Authoring uses IFC opening elements rather than direct mesh Booleans.
approaches.csg_engine: Blender BMesh boolean (Carve/Exact) for live edit; ifcopenshell on save
approaches.handles_degeneracies: Exact backend (CGAL-based MPR) is robust; Fast can fail on coplanar.

approaches.algorithm: No runtime CSG. Consumes pre-tessellated meshes from an upstream converter (ifcopenshell, web-ifc, IFC2gltf). Pure visualization.
approaches.handles_degeneracies: n/a (delegated upstream)

per_shape.consensus: No prism-specific specialization in mature tools. Everyone runs the generic 3D mesh Boolean (OCCT BRep, CGAL Nef, or fuzzy-bools mesh-arrangement). The expensive path is the default; that is the dominant pattern.

approaches.algorithm: SPECIALIZED PATH. In IfcBooleanResult.cpp it detects SecondOperand IfcHalfSpaceSolid and, when 8+ halfspaces chain, iteratively flattens them rather than handing OCCT a single multi-operand cut. Comment: 'There is something peculiar about many half space subtraction operands that OCCT does not like... excessive edge-edge interference checks'. IfcPolygonalBoundedHalfSpace gets its OWN mapping function that subclasses the half_space taxonomy and substitutes the polygonal loop for the default infinite face boundary - it is not generalized to BRep Boolean of a thick prism.
approaches.handles_degeneracies: Bounded polygonal face avoids OCCT instability with infinite half-spaces; chain flattening avoids n-way interference blowup.

approaches.algorithm: Has an experimental halfspace_tree decomposition (nef_to_halfspace_tree.h) representing booleans as a tree of halfspace_tree_plane leaves + halfspace_tree_nary_branch (OP_UNION/OP_SUBTRACTION/OP_INTERSECTION) nodes. Builds a Boost graph of Nef facets, classifies edges as reflex, isolates connected components, BFS-extracts convex subsets, recursively decomposes the remainder. BUT the production convert_openings path has this commented out and falls back to generic Nef union/subtract. The author intended a specialized halfspace path; it was deferred.
approaches.csg_engine: CGAL Nef (production); halfspace tree exists but is dormant
approaches.handles_degeneracies: Exact arithmetic

approaches.algorithm: When two IfcPolygonalBoundedHalfSpace cuts are stacked, the cutter prisms are merged into a single mesh and applied in one fuzzy-bools subtract. Halfspace is not treated as a true infinite plane - it is materialized as a fat prism large enough to exit the host on every side, then run through the generic mesh path.
approaches.csg_engine: fuzzy-bools mesh boolean (no dedicated halfspace path)
approaches.handles_degeneracies: Coplanar-tolerant by construction; multiple stacked halfspaces are pre-merged to amortize

approaches.algorithm: IfcPolygonalBoundedHalfSpace is a recognized 'Clipping' representation type. Authoring creates an IfcOpeningElement; at save, ifcopenshell handles the boolean. Live preview uses Blender's modifier stack.
approaches.csg_engine: Blender modifier (Exact = CGAL) at preview; ifcopenshell on save
approaches.handles_degeneracies: Same as ifcopenshell

per_shape.consensus: Halfspace IS specialized in mature tools: ifcopenshell-OCCT detects halfspace at the mapping layer, swaps the boundary loop on IfcPolygonalBoundedHalfSpace, and rate-limits batched halfspace subtractions; ifcopenshell-CGAL has a halfspace_tree decomposition built (but currently dormant); web-ifc merges and amortizes. NOBODY hands a pile of halfspaces to a black-box mesh boolean and hopes. This validates the ifcfast halfspace_clip approach as in-line with prior art - in fact ifcfast is closer to the principled outcome than ifcopenshell-CGAL, which still has the cleanup commented out.

per_shape.shape_combination: prism minus convex non-extrusion (cylinder, sphere, IfcCsgPrimitive3D)

approaches.algorithm: Primitives become OCCT solids via the dedicated MakeCylinder/MakeSphere primitives, then BRepAlgoAPI_Cut. OCCT has native parametric primitives so this is the cheapest path it offers, but still goes through general BRep Boolean.
approaches.handles_degeneracies: OCCT cylindrical surfaces are exact; tolerance based at the cut faces.

approaches.algorithm: Cylinder/sphere tessellated to Polyhedron_3 -> Nef. No analytic primitive support in CGAL Nef. Most expensive path.
approaches.handles_degeneracies: Exact on tessellated input; tessellation faceting visible.

approaches.algorithm: Primitive tessellated to triangle mesh, then fuzzy-bools subtract. No analytic special-case.
approaches.handles_degeneracies: Generic mesh boolean

per_shape.consensus: No analytic specialization in the IFC tool ecosystem. All three lower primitives to either BRep (OCCT) or triangles (everyone else) and run the generic engine.

per_shape.shape_combination: brep minus prism (faceted host with a sweep cutter)

approaches.algorithm: Generic OCCT BRepAlgoAPI_Cut between two BReps. Cutter (sweep) is built via BRepPrimAPI_MakePrism; host loaded as faceted BRep.
approaches.handles_degeneracies: Tolerance based; faceted BReps with near-coplanar faces often fail and ifcopenshell logs warnings.

approaches.algorithm: Both operands -> Polyhedron_3 -> Nef -> subtract. Robust but slow.
approaches.handles_degeneracies: Exact

approaches.algorithm: Both as triangle meshes through fuzzy-bools.
approaches.handles_degeneracies: Coplanar-tolerant mesh boolean

per_shape.consensus: Treated as generic 3D Boolean. No tool exploits 'cutter is a prism' as a fast path.

approaches.algorithm: BRepAlgoAPI_Cut/Common/Fuse on whatever BRep IfcOpenShell's mapping layer produced. Iterative flattening + retry on failure.
approaches.handles_degeneracies: Tolerance based; documented failure mode on coplanar faces and pathological IFC authoring.

approaches.algorithm: Polyhedron_3 -> Nef_polyhedron_3 -> Boolean -> back to Polyhedron_3. nef_to_halfspace_tree.h exists as a future optimization but is not in the active code path.
approaches.handles_degeneracies: Most robust available; slow.

approaches.algorithm: Triangle-soup Boolean. Build planar arrangements at intersections; CDT-retriangulate; classify each fragment by centroid ray-casting against the opposite mesh with three rays for consistency.
approaches.handles_degeneracies: Designed specifically for CAD coplanar surfaces and tolerances; pre-alpha label still on the repo but production in web-ifc.

approaches.algorithm: Blender 'Exact' boolean = MPR-based mesh arrangement (CGAL lineage); 'Fast' = float-precision interval BSP-like. User-selectable.
approaches.handles_degeneracies: Exact: robust; Fast: fails on coplanars

approaches.algorithm: Guaranteed-manifold mesh Boolean per Julian Smith's algorithm. Lazy CSG tree of CsgOpNodes; manifold-by-construction output.
approaches.handles_degeneracies: Guarantees manifold output without caveats; the most modern serious alternative.

per_shape.consensus: The general case is dominated by three production engines: OpenCASCADE BRep (industry default, brittle on bad input), CGAL Nef (slow but exact), and increasingly elalish/manifold for guaranteed-manifold output. The pure mesh-arrangement path (fuzzy-bools) is the WASM/browser choice and is what we would most resemble if we did the general case in Rust today.

tools_surveyed: ifcopenshell v0.8.0 (C++ kernels: OpenCASCADE + CGAL, with halfspace_tree decomposition module)
web-ifc / engine_web-ifc (C++ to WASM via fuzzy-bools)
fuzzy-bools (tomvandig, the actual CSG core behind web-ifc)
BlenderBIM / Bonsai (uses ifcopenshell at save, Blender BMesh booleans live)
xeokit-bim-viewer / xeokit-sdk (no runtime CSG; consumes pre-meshed input)
elalish/manifold (referenced as the modern alternative; FreeCAD discussion)
clipper2 / clipper2-rust (Rust bindings to Clipper2 C++)
earcutr (pure-Rust ear-clipping triangulation; already used by ifcfast)

category.label: prism − prism (the ~95% case)
category.description: Host = IfcExtrudedAreaSolid (rectangular or polygon profile), cutter = IfcExtrudedAreaSolid. Both extrudes along arbitrary axes, in arbitrary placements. Most window/door/MEP openings.

design.primary_algorithm:
**Sweep-aligned 2D Boolean + extrusion rebuild (the "prism algebra" approach).**

Core observation: a prism = (2D profile polygon P, extrusion direction d, depth h, local-to-world 4x4 X). The 3D set is X * { (u, v, w) : (u,v) in P, w in [0, h] } projected along d. Two prisms (H = host, C = cutter) admit a closed-form 3D Boolean difference iff their extrusion directions are **parallel in world frame** (i.e. `X_H * d_H` colinear with `X_C * d_C`, with tolerance ~1e-4 on dot product). In that case the result is itself a generalised prism (possibly with holes), and we can compute it entirely with 2D polygon Boolean + extrusion. When extrusion directions are NOT parallel, fall back to a general 3D mesh CSG path (Phase B below).

The pipeline has two cooperating layers:

---

**Layer A — Parametric fast path (prism algebra, ~85% of all prism-minus-prism cases in Revit)**

A new module `crates/core/src/mesh/prism_csg.rs` exposes:

```
pub struct PrismParams {
    pub profile: Polygon2D,         // outer + holes, in profile-local 2D
    pub dir: Vec3,                  // unit, in solid-local 3D
    pub depth: f32,                 // in solid-local units
    pub local_xform: Mat4,          // solid-local to world (Position * ObjectPlacement)
}

pub enum PrismCsgOutcome {
    Cut { profile: Polygon2D, dir: Vec3, depth: f32, local_xform: Mat4 },
    Empty,
    Unchanged,
    NotParametric,   // caller falls back to mesh CSG (Phase B)
}

pub fn subtract_prism(host: &PrismParams, cutter: &PrismParams) -> PrismCsgOutcome;
pub fn subtract_many_prisms(host: &PrismParams, cutters: &[PrismParams]) -> PrismCsgOutcome;
```

Algorithm step-by-step for the single-cutter case:

1. **Axis alignment check.** Compute `n_h = (X_H_rot * d_H).normalize()` and `n_c = (X_C_rot * d_C).normalize()` in world. If `n_h.dot(n_c).abs() < 1 - AXIS_EPS` (AXIS_EPS = 1e-4), return `NotParametric`. The cutter sweeps a non-parallel axis — no closed-form prism algebra; route to Layer B.

2. **Choose host sweep frame as canonical 3D frame.** Build orthonormal basis `(e1, e2, n_h)` where `n_h` is the host's world-space sweep axis. `e1` and `e2` span the profile plane (chosen deterministically: pick the world axis least parallel to `n_h` and Gram-Schmidt).

3. **Project both profiles into the host's profile plane.**
   - Host: walk each outer / hole vertex, transform `(u, v, 0)` by `X_H`, then project onto `(e1, e2)`: `world = X_H * (u, v, 0, 1)`; `u' = (world - host_origin).dot(e1)`; `v' = (world - host_origin).dot(e2)`. Yields `P_H_world` (outer + holes), 2D in the host plane.
   - Cutter: same. The cutter profile is swept along `n_c` ≈ ±`n_h`, so its bottom-cap polygon, transformed by `X_C` and projected onto `(e1, e2)`, is the cutter's footprint in the host plane. Yields `P_C_world`.
   - Force CCW orientation on outer loops and CW on holes via shoelace (the IFC convention is loose; matches the existing convention used by `mesh::extrusion`).

4. **Compute the sweep-axis overlap interval.**
   - Host sweep interval (along `n_h`): `[s_h, s_h + h_H * sign(d_H_world . n_h)]`, where `s_h = host_origin.dot(n_h)`. Normalise so `s_h_lo < s_h_hi`.
   - Cutter sweep interval (project both end-caps of the cutter onto `n_h`): `s_c_lo = cutter_origin.dot(n_h)`, `s_c_hi = (cutter_origin + n_c * h_C * sign).dot(n_h)`. Normalise.
   - Intersect: `lo = max(s_h_lo, s_c_lo)`, `hi = min(s_h_hi, s_c_hi)`. If `hi <= lo + 1e-6`, the cutter does not overlap host in the sweep direction → return `Unchanged`. This is the closed-form answer for "opening extends beyond wall thickness on both sides" — the cutter footprint is just clipped to the wall's sweep range, then subtracted.

5. **2D polygon Boolean difference: `P_H_world − P_C_world`.**
   - Library choice: **i_overlay** (pure Rust, v7.x, the polygon Boolean engine that powers `georust/geo` and the only mature pure-Rust option that handles self-intersection, even-odd / non-zero fill, and multi-hole input). Use `f32` API to avoid precision-loss conversions; vertices are already in the host's local-rebased frame and well within ±1e5 mm.
   - Call: `Overlay::with_subj_and_clip(p_h_outer + p_h_holes, p_c_outer + p_c_holes).overlay(OverlayRule::Difference, FillRule::NonZero)`.
   - Result: `Vec<Shape>`, each shape having one outer + 0..N holes. May be empty (cutter consumed host) → return `Empty`. May be a single shape (typical: wall - door = wall with a rectangular hole). May be multiple disconnected components (cutter splits an arbitrary profile into pieces — rare but valid).

6. **Sweep range adjustment.** Three cases for the 1D component:
   - **Through-cut**: `[s_c_lo, s_c_hi]` covers `[s_h_lo, s_h_hi]` → no sweep-axis change; result is the 2D difference extruded over the host range (the standard door/window case). This is the 95% subcase.
   - **Partial side recess**: cutter touches one face only, e.g. `s_c_lo <= s_h_lo < s_h_hi < s_c_hi` is not satisfied — the cutter ends inside the wall. We cannot represent that as a single prism with the host's profile. **Decompose the result into two prisms** sharing a horizontal seam at the cutter's end z: (a) the "clear" prism using the original host profile over `[s_h_lo, s_c_lo]` (and/or `[s_c_hi, s_h_hi]`), and (b) the "carved" prism using the 2D difference over the overlapping `[max(s_h_lo, s_c_lo), min(s_h_hi, s_c_hi)]` range. Both extruded independently and concatenated.
   - **Pocket** (cutter strictly inside, both faces uncut): three prisms stacked: original profile below cutter; carved profile through cutter range; original profile above. Same decomposition pattern.

7. **Rebuild the mesh.** For each output sub-prism, build a `Polygon2D` in `(e1, e2)` coordinates and call `mesh::extrusion::extrude_polygon(&poly, n_h, sub_depth, sub_xform)`. The local transform places the bottom cap at the right sweep-axis offset. Concatenate all sub-prism meshes (just append vertices + shift indices) — they share seam vertices ideally, but since they have collinear edges on different faces the simple concatenation produces a valid (though potentially non-watertight at the seam) closed mesh. Stitch the seam via `weld_coincident_vertices` (a small pre-existing-style utility, ~30 LOC) to guarantee manifoldness.

For multi-cutter (`subtract_many_prisms`):
- Pre-check: all cutters must share the host's sweep axis (axis alignment check per cutter). Cutters that fail are diverted to Layer B.
- Compute the union of cutter footprints first via `Overlay::union`, then a single `Overlay::difference` against the host. This is a structural improvement over Manifold's batch_difference because the 2D union is much cheaper than 3D Boolean.
- Sweep-axis decomposition: bucket cutters by their `[s_c_lo, s_c_hi]` intervals. If all cutters share the same `[lo, hi]` (typical: wall with multiple doors all at full height), single-pass extrusion of the carved profile. If intervals diverge (rare: wall with a door at floor level + a clerestory window above), partition the host's sweep range at all cutter interval endpoints into slabs, compute the union of cutter footprints active in each slab, and emit one sub-prism per slab.

---

**Layer B — General mesh fallback (the "extrusion not parallel" case + brep host)**

Used when Layer A returns `NotParametric` (axis misalignment, profile not extractable, or operands not both `IfcExtrudedAreaSolid`) OR when a non-prism host shape (brep, faceset) is involved. Two viable pure-Rust paths:

**B.1 — `csgrs` BSP-tree mesh boolean.** Pure-Rust port of CSG.js, the most mature pure-Rust 3D mesh CSG kernel (v0.20.1, 231 stars, actively developed). Accepts triangle meshes, returns mesh. Algorithm: build a BSP tree from host's polygons, clip cutter polygons against it (classify INSIDE/OUTSIDE/COPLANAR), invert, clip host polygons against cutter's BSP, union results. Same algorithmic family as Manifold's predecessor (Carve, libigl-cork). Known weaknesses: coplanar face handling at exact axis-aligned alignment (e.g. cutter face exactly on wall face) can produce slivers or T-junctions. Mitigation: pre-perturb the cutter by a small (1e-5 mm) inward translation along its sweep axis — well below building tolerance, eliminates exact coplanar degeneracies. This is the same trick OCCT uses internally with its tolerance band.

**B.2 — Hand-rolled triangle-soup CSG via repeated halfspace clip.** Decompose the cutter into halfspaces (a convex prism = intersection of N halfspaces, one per side face + top/bottom cap). For each cutter halfspace, clip the host. The result is `H ∩ (¬C) = H \ C` only when C is convex; non-convex profiles need an additional union step. This generalises the existing `halfspace_clip` module and reuses its robust per-triangle classification + cap triangulation. Suitable for convex cutters only (rectangle, circle, regular shapes); falls back to B.1 for arbitrary cutters with concave profiles.

**Recommended Layer B**: B.1 (`csgrs`) for the broad fallback; B.2 (halfspace iteration) only if `csgrs` benchmarks unfavorably on the deep-nested case. Either way, the fallback path is opt-in behind a Rust-only crate dep, no C++.

---

**Dispatcher**: `cut_openings::apply` becomes:

```
for each cutter segment:
    if halfspace_plane tag → halfspace_clip (already pure-Rust, unchanged)
    elif both host AND cutter are IfcExtrudedAreaSolid AND we have parametric metadata:
        try Layer A (prism_csg::subtract_many_prisms)
        on Cut/Empty/Unchanged: use result
        on NotParametric: fall through to Layer B
    else:
        Layer B (csgrs)
```

To make Layer A possible we need the **prism parameters preserved alongside the mesh** — see Prerequisites.
prerequisites: Parametric prism metadata propagation: extend `LocalMesh` (or add parallel `LocalMeshSource`) with optional `Option<PrismParams>` carrying the source profile + direction + depth + local transform. Populated in `mesh::extrusion::extrude`, threaded through `mesh::boolean::boolean_result`'s retag/append loop so segments stamped `boolean_first_operand|extrusion` and `boolean_second_operand|extrusion` retain a pointer back to their parametric origin. ~200 LOC + audit of all `LocalMesh`-producing call sites.
`PrismParams` type + constructor from `IfcExtrudedAreaSolid` step id (essentially refactor `mesh::extrusion::extrude` to return both a `LocalMesh` AND the params it built from). ~80 LOC.

World-frame axis projection utility: take a `PrismParams` plus the product's `ObjectPlacement` matrix, return `(world_origin, world_sweep_axis, world_depth, profile_in_world_plane)`. Handles non-uniform scale defensively (reject if det(rotation) < 0.99 — IFC placements should be rigid, but guard). ~80 LOC.
i_overlay crate dependency in `crates/core/Cargo.toml` (~v7.0). Verify wheel-build is clean on all 4 platforms; i_overlay is pure Rust no_std-compatible so this should be free.
2D polygon CCW/CW canonicalisation helper (shoelace area sign). ~20 LOC; exists in spirit inside `mesh::profile` but not exposed.
Vertex welding pass to seal sub-prism seams at sweep-axis decomposition boundaries — grid-hashed coincident-vertex merge within 1e-4 m tolerance. ~60 LOC.

csgrs crate dependency for Layer B fallback (~v0.20). Audit license (MIT, fine). Verify it links cleanly into our crate; csgrs depends on nalgebra which we don't currently use — adds ~15 MB to compile time. Acceptable.
Cutter perturbation utility: translate a cutter mesh by epsilon along its inferred sweep axis (or the host face normal if known) to break exact coplanar degeneracies before handing to csgrs. ~30 LOC.
New top-level dispatcher in `cut_openings::apply` that inspects segment metadata + parametric availability to pick Layer A vs Layer B vs the unchanged halfspace_clip path. ~80 LOC restructure.

Tests: parametric-path determinism (same input → byte-identical output), axis-misalignment correctness (Layer B fires), all 6 existing csg.rs integration tests must pass against the new dispatcher without feature flag manipulation. ~300 LOC test.
edge_cases: Wall tilted 30 degrees from vertical (sloped wall with vertical-extrusion door): host's world sweep axis is along the tilted normal; door's world sweep axis is along world +Z. Their dot product ≈ cos(30) = 0.866 → fails axis-alignment check (threshold 0.9999) → routes to Layer B (csgrs). Correct behaviour — these are genuinely 3D CSG.

Wall and door both vertical, sloped IFC site placement (whole project rotated): both prisms' world sweep axes are the same tilted vertical → Layer A still applies, projection plane is tilted accordingly. The math is frame-invariant; this should just work and we add a test.

Opening exactly coplanar with wall face (door whose backplane is the wall's inside face): in Layer A this is fine — the cutter's 2D footprint touches the host outer polygon edge, i_overlay handles edge-on-edge difference robustly with NonZero fill. In Layer B (csgrs) this is the canonical degeneracy; mitigated by the cutter perturbation step. We never give a perfectly coplanar pair to csgrs.

Opening box extends beyond wall thickness on both sides (oversized cutter — Revit's safety pad pattern): Layer A step 4 clips the cutter's sweep interval to the host's, so the cutter footprint just gets clipped to the wall's sweep range before 2D subtraction. Closed-form correct, no through-cut artefacts.

Nested halfspace clip inside the host extrusion before the opening cut (Sannergata-class sloped wall + door): host comes in as `IfcBooleanClippingResult(extrusion, halfspace × N)`. The halfspaces are clipped FIRST via `halfspace_clip` (unchanged path) — this destroys the prism algebra invariant (the result is no longer an `IfcExtrudedAreaSolid` shape, it's an extrusion-shaped mesh with a sloped top cap). So when the door cutter comes along, the host is no longer parametric → Layer A's parametric path can't apply → routes to Layer B. Correct. Optimisation note: we could preserve a `prism+capped-by-planes` typed intermediate to keep Layer A viable through halfspace clips, but that doubles algorithm complexity; defer.
Cutter with concave profile (L-shaped recess): Layer A 2D Boolean handles it natively — i_overlay supports arbitrary polygons. Layer B halfspace iteration (B.2) would not handle it; csgrs (B.1) does.

Two cutters whose footprints overlap (overlapping openings — pathological but seen in messy Revit files): Layer A's pre-union of cutter footprints via i_overlay's union folds overlapping regions correctly, producing a single subtraction. Better than Manifold's batch_difference which can produce slivers at the overlap boundary.

Cutter exactly equal to host (cutter consumes host entirely): 2D Boolean returns empty shape list → return `Empty` → cut_openings.rs emits an empty mesh (current Manifold path does the same). Caller / substrate writer can flag this.
Cutter outside host entirely (mis-authored relationship): 2D Boolean returns the host unchanged → `Unchanged` → mesh untouched. No spurious geometry.

Profile with curve sampling artefacts (32-sample circle subtracted from rect wall): the 32 sampled points become 32 polygon vertices, i_overlay handles them. Visible faceting on the cut face is inherent and unavoidable — same as current Manifold behaviour. Could be raised to 64+ samples for openings authored with `IfcCircleProfileDef` if quality demands it.

Non-rigid local transform (rare; some Tekla scaled exports): `world_xform` from `axis_placement_3d` should be rigid, but defensive check on `det` < 0.99 routes to Layer B. Layer A's axis math assumes rigid transforms (otherwise sweep depth in world frame is anisotropic).

Far-from-origin precision: vertices arrive in local-rebased frame per `mesh_anchor` contract. i_overlay's f32 API is then well within precision range. No special handling needed; covered by existing rebase invariants.

Cross-product opening (IfcRelVoidsElement) — host extrusion + opening element extrusion: handled by `CrossProductCut::flush` which currently calls `csg::subtract_many`. We re-route through the new dispatcher, which means the parametric path needs the cutter (opening) to also surface its `PrismParams`. The opening product mesh is an `IfcOpeningElement` whose representation is a plain `IfcExtrudedAreaSolid` → params extractable. The anchor translation in `flush` becomes a translation of `PrismParams.local_xform` instead of vertex munging. Cleaner than the current vertex-buffer translate.

Multiple cutters with diverging sweep intervals (door at base, window above): Layer A's slab decomposition handles this — sweep range partitioned at endpoints, per-slab cutter footprint union. Produces 2-3 sub-prisms stacked; vertex weld pass seals seams.

Cutter is `IfcExtrudedAreaSolidTapered` (unhandled today): would fail Layer A's prism-extraction (not a plain `IfcExtrudedAreaSolid`); fall through to Layer B. Layer B mesh path handles it like any other closed mesh provided the tessellation handler is added. Independent prerequisite.

Host is brep (Tekla precast), cutter is extrusion: Layer A inapplicable (host not parametric prism); Layer B fires. csgrs handles it provided the brep is closed-manifold; same Outcome::Fallback rule as today applies if csgrs rejects.

Sub-mm sliver between cutter face and host face (Revit imprecise authoring): Layer A 2D Boolean's NonZero fill collapses sub-tolerance slivers naturally. Layer B is sensitive; mitigated by perturbation.

Opening with profile holes (window with internal mullion): cutter profile has its own holes (`IfcArbitraryProfileDefWithVoids`). The opening's 2D footprint is the outer minus holes. Subtracting that from the host means the mullion area is NOT cut from the host — the host retains the mullion region. Layer A handles this correctly because i_overlay reads holes as part of the polygon. Verify with a synthetic test.
design.loc_estimate: ~1800 LOC (Layer A: ~700 prism_csg + ~250 prism-params plumbing + ~200 tests; Layer B integration: ~150 csgrs bridge + ~100 perturbation + dispatcher; tests + welding + axis projection utilities: ~400)
open_questions: Should `LocalMesh` grow `PrismParams` or should we maintain a parallel `HashMap<step_id, PrismParams>` keyed by IFC step id alongside the mesh cache? Parallel map is less invasive but adds a lookup. Lean toward in-mesh struct for cache locality and to survive `boolean::retag` cleanly.

i_overlay v7.x API: confirm that `Overlay::with_subj_and_clip` accepts polygons with holes as a single shape, not as separate subjects. (Reading docs suggests yes via `add_shape`; verify before committing.)
csgrs handling of f32 vs f64: csgrs is generic over a numeric type. Default f64 is fine but doubles memory for the fallback path. Probably acceptable since fallback is the cold path.

Should we keep manifold-csg as a third escape hatch behind a feature flag for users who have known-good files and want speed parity? Argues against because it reintroduces the C++ dep that this whole exercise is meant to retire. Recommendation: no, retire manifold-csg entirely once Layer A + Layer B cover the matrix.

When Layer A's slab decomposition produces a non-watertight seam between sub-prisms (numerical noise after weld), should we re-route to Layer B? Lean toward no — the weld pass with 1e-4 m tolerance should always succeed for shared vertices that came from the same source extrusion endpoints. Add an assertion in debug builds.

What is the right `AXIS_EPS` for the parallel-axis check? 1e-4 means we accept axes within ~0.5 degrees. Tighter (1e-6) routes more cases to Layer B; looser (1e-3) accepts visibly skew cuts as parametric (~2.5 degrees off). Building authoring rarely produces axes that are not bit-identical when they're meant to be parallel — propose 1e-5 (0.25 degrees) as a default.

Performance target: today's Manifold path on the 13-deep Sannergata wall fold takes ~50 ms. Layer A 2D path should be O(N log N) on profile vertex count and ~10x faster in the common single-cutter case. Need a benchmark to confirm before committing to retirement of Manifold.
Should `PrismParams` carry the source step_id back for diagnostics (when Layer A diverts to Layer B, log which prism failed which check)? Cheap; do it.

concerns: Slab-decomposition seam produces non-manifold interior triangles in the partial-recess and pocket sub-cases. Step 7's `weld_coincident_vertices` welds *vertices* but does nothing about the *triangles* on the seam plane. Concretely, for the 1m×0.25m×3m wall with 0.9m×0.3m×2.1m door (partial recess), the algorithm emits: (a) lower 'carved' prism over z∈[0, 2.1] whose top cap is triangulated as (wall_outer − door_outer) — i.e. has a door-shaped hole; (b) upper 'clear' prism over z∈[2.1, 3.0] whose bottom cap is triangulated as the full wall rectangle (covers the door-shaped region). After welding, the door-shaped patch appears as an interior face inside the closed solid at z=2.1, with two oppositely-oriented copies. Volume integral happens to still compute correctly (cancels), but the mesh is non-manifold: ray-inside tests, mesh_quality=closed_shell classification, and any topology-sensitive consumer downstream are broken. This is not equivalent to a true 3D Boolean.

The proposal does NOT describe coplanar-face cancellation at sub-prism interfaces. The fix is straightforward (emit the seam as a single oriented patch equal to the symmetric difference of the upper and lower cap regions, or detect and cancel opposite-facing coplanar triangle pairs after welding) but it is load-bearing for correctness and entirely missing.

Sign convention for cutter sweep interval is underspecified: step 4 references `sign` without defining it for the cutter (only host has `sign(d_H_world . n_h)` stated). When `n_c = -n_h` (cutter extrudes opposite to host), the unstated sign rule must flip, then `Normalise` swaps. Probably right in implementation but the spec leaves a correctness-critical detail to chance.

Non-watertight assertion at slab seams is described as 'add an assertion in debug builds' in open questions, not as a failure-routing fallback. If welding misses a vertex due to floating-point drift (e.g. transcendental profile sample point that doesn't appear identically in both caps because the upper cap doesn't sample that vertex at all), the mesh ships non-watertight in release builds with no diversion to Layer B. For an arc-sampled profile (32-point circle in cutter), the upper slab's solid wall cap does NOT contain those 32 vertices on its boundary at all — there's nothing to weld to. The seam is structurally non-coincident in vertex set, not merely numerically.

The i_overlay holes-in-single-shape API question (open question 2) is admitted unresolved. If the API takes outer+holes as separate subjects with even-odd fill instead of one shape with non-zero fill, the difference operation against a host with internal voids (e.g. wall with existing service penetration) returns wrong topology. This is checkable but flagged as a risk by the author themselves and unresolved at design time.
verdicts.blocker: Wall 1m×0.25m×3m with 0.9m×0.3m×2.1m door (door cuts only the lower 2.1m, leaving a 0.9m lintel): the slab decomposition produces a non-manifold result because the carved sub-prism's top cap (door-shaped hole) and the lintel sub-prism's bottom cap (solid wall rectangle) overlap on the door-shaped region at z=2.1, creating an interior face inside the closed solid. Volume happens to integrate correctly due to sign cancellation, but `mesh_quality=closed_shell` invariant is violated, ray-inside tests fail inside the lintel, and any topology-aware downstream consumer (clash narrow-phase point-in-solid, mesh repair, BIM viewer back-face culling diagnostics) sees a degenerate mesh. The described `weld_coincident_vertices` step welds vertices but does not cancel the redundant coplanar triangle pair.
verdicts.suggested_mitigation:
Replace step 7's vertex-weld-only stitching with explicit seam-face reconstruction: at each slab interface plane, compute the 2D difference between adjacent caps (upper cap region MINUS lower cap region), triangulate that as a single oriented seam patch, and emit ONLY that patch at the interface — neither sub-prism contributes its own cap polygon at the shared plane. For the worked door example this means: lower carved prism is open-topped at z=2.1, upper lintel prism is open-bottomed at z=2.1, and a single door-shaped patch (normal +Z) fills the gap. Result is manifold and closed by construction. Also: explicitly route to Layer B (csgrs) when sub-prisms have profile vertices that don't coincide on the seam boundary (arc-sampled cutter into rectangular host = non-coincident vertex sets that welding cannot reconcile). Tighten the open-question on welding into a hard fall-through to Layer B on non-watertight detection rather than a debug-only assertion.</suggested_mitigation>
</invoke>

concerns: Far-from-origin precision is asserted to be 'covered by existing rebase invariants', but the design crucially relies on i_overlay's f32 API. The mesh_anchor rebase is applied to vertex buffers, NOT to the parametric PrismParams. Step 3 of Layer A computes `world = X_H * (u, v, 0, 1)` and `u' = (world - host_origin).dot(e1)` — but host_origin and cutter_origin come from the raw IFC placements which, on a UTM-scale project (~6e6 m northing), live at 1e6+ metres. Computing `world - host_origin` in f32 yields catastrophic cancellation on the dot product into e1/e2 if host_origin and cutter_origin are themselves f32 — the difference loses 5-6 significant digits before the dot product begins. The design does not specify which precision the placement math uses, nor does it specify rebasing PrismParams to the local anchor before invoking Layer A. This is a silent corruptness bug on every Norwegian/UTM project, which is the dominant ifcfast workload (Sannergata is exactly this).

AXIS_EPS = 1e-4 (later 1e-5 in open_questions) defines a hard cliff between Layer A and Layer B. Real-world Revit exports routinely produce 'parallel' axes that disagree at the 1e-3 to 1e-5 level due to Revit's internal trig (e.g. a wall at 45° azimuth combined with a door that inherits the wall transform but goes through one additional matrix decomposition). This creates a robustness landmine: on one project two near-identical doors take different code paths (Layer A vs Layer B), producing visibly different cut geometry on the SAME wall. The proposal acknowledges the tuning question but does not specify a snap-to-parallel pre-step, which would be the actual robust answer.

Coplanar handling in Layer A claims 'i_overlay handles edge-on-edge difference robustly with NonZero fill'. This is overconfident. When the door's outer profile edge is colinear with (and partially overlaps) the wall's outer profile edge, i_overlay can emit zero-thickness edges or duplicate vertices at the overlap interval endpoints. The result is a 2D polygon that extrudes into a 3D mesh with degenerate triangles on the side wall — passes mesh validity nominally, but fails downstream clash/QTO and looks wrong in viewers. The design lacks a post-Boolean polygon cleanup pass (collapse near-duplicate vertices, remove sub-tolerance edges). i_overlay is good but not magic.

Degenerate cutter (zero-area profile or zero-extrude depth) is not enumerated as an edge case at all. The proposal lists 16 edge cases but skips this one. Step 4 has `if hi <= lo + 1e-6 → Unchanged`, but does not check h_C == 0 (zero-depth cutter) or area(P_C) == 0 (zero-area profile from a malformed IfcArbitraryClosedProfileDef with collinear points). i_overlay's behaviour on a degenerate polygon subject is undefined territory — likely returns the host unchanged, but may also panic or return garbage. No early-reject is specified.

Self-intersecting input: the proposal asserts i_overlay 'handles self-intersection' but does not gate this behind an explicit validation step. Revit DOES emit self-intersecting profiles (figure-8 wall sweeps from bad model edits, profiles with order-reversed inner loops where the author intended a hole). i_overlay with NonZero fill on a figure-8 will produce a topologically different result than EvenOdd, and the choice in step 5 is hardcoded to NonZero. The design does not analyse which fill rule matches IFC's stated polygon semantics (IFC is silent; common practice is EvenOdd for IfcArbitraryProfileDefWithVoids). This is a correctness-on-malformed-input issue masquerading as a settled choice.

The vertex weld pass (`weld_coincident_vertices`, ~30 LOC, 1e-4 m tolerance) is positioned as a guaranteed seam-sealer for slab decomposition. But the tolerance is in WORLD units after the local transform; if the project's unit_scale is metres but vertices are in millimetres post-rebase (the substrate convention is source units), 1e-4 m = 0.1 mm — fine for mm. If the project is in feet (US Revit), 1e-4 m ≈ 0.0004 ft which is below the noise floor and the weld will fail to coalesce seam vertices. The design does not parameterise tolerance by unit_scale, so US Revit imperial models silently produce non-watertight cuts at slab boundaries. ifcfast supports imperial via unit_scale; this is a real regression vector.

Cross-product cut (IfcRelVoidsElement) routing: the proposal claims the opening's PrismParams 'translates' via `local_xform` instead of vertex munging. But the current `CrossProductCut::flush` operates on already-rebased meshes with a `mesh_anchor` offset. Translating the cutter's local_xform by the anchor offset is correct in principle, but the cutter PrismParams was constructed BEFORE the rebase (during extrusion). Threading the rebase through the parametric path requires every PrismParams-producing site to also be told about mesh_anchor — which is decided much later in the pipeline, after the host has been built. The dependency inversion is non-trivial and the proposal does not spell out the data-flow. This is a correctness hazard at the parametric path's most-used entry point.

csgrs fallback perturbation (1e-5 mm inward translation) is described as 'eliminates exact coplanar degeneracies'. But coplanar degeneracy in csgrs (BSP CSG) is not just about exact equality — it's about the BSP splitter heuristic. A 1e-5 mm shift can still leave triangle faces with normals that disagree at machine epsilon when transformed through the placement chain, leading to BSP misclassification (triangle classified INSIDE that should be COPLANAR). The standard mitigation is much larger (~1e-3 mm) for f32 stability, but that's now visible on close inspection in a viewer. The proposed value is in the worst zone: too small to fix the real precision issue, large enough to violate the building-tolerance promise on stacked operations.
verdicts.blocker: Far-from-origin f32 precision: Layer A's projection step (world = X_H * vertex, then (world - host_origin).dot(e1)) is computed without specifying rebase of PrismParams to the local anchor. On UTM-projected Norwegian projects (Sannergata is the canonical example, with ~6e6 m northing), the placement origins live at 1e6+ metres, so the subtraction loses 5-6 significant f32 digits before i_overlay ever sees the polygons. The mesh_anchor rebase exists in the vertex pipeline but is not threaded through the parametric metadata path. This silently corrupts the dominant ifcfast workload while presenting clean meshes. The proposal handwaves "covered by existing rebase invariants" without specifying the rebase point for PrismParams — but the entire mesh_anchor convention is mesh-buffer-local, not placement-local, so this isn't actually covered.
verdicts.suggested_mitigation: Make the precision contract explicit: (1) Define a `PrismParams::rebase(anchor: DVec3)` that converts placement origins + sweep depths into local-anchored f64 BEFORE Layer A's projection math, mirroring `mesh_anchor` for vertices — and assert this in debug builds. Do the projection arithmetic in f64, only narrow to f32 at the i_overlay boundary. (2) Add a pre-pass that snaps near-parallel axes (dot > 1 - 1e-3) to exact parallel via Gram-Schmidt against the host axis, eliminating the AXIS_EPS cliff. (3) Add explicit degenerate-input gates: reject cutters with `h_C * area(P_C) < volume_eps` (parameterised by unit_scale) before entering Boolean, returning `Unchanged`. (4) Add a post-Boolean 2D cleanup pass (collapse near-coincident vertices, drop sub-tolerance edges) keyed off unit_scale. (5) Parameterise the weld tolerance and csgrs perturbation by unit_scale so imperial models behave. (6) Document the fill-rule choice (NonZero vs EvenOdd) with reference test cases drawn from real Revit malformed inputs, and pick the rule that produces the correct hole semantics for IfcArbitraryProfileDefWithVoids. Until these are spelled out, the design is not robust against the actual degeneracies ifcfast encounters.

concerns: Big-O claim ('10x faster, O(N log N) on profile vertices') is plausible but unbenchmarked. The dominant cost in Manifold's batch_difference is not the boolean per se — it's BVH build + triangle intersection across O(T_host * T_cutter) potential pairs. Layer A reduces that to a 2D i_overlay call over ~4-32 profile vertices (door = 4 vertices, sampled circle = 32). 2D Boolean on tiny polygons is sub-microsecond; the extrusion rebuild dominates. So the asymptotic story is fine, but the per-call overhead — extracting PrismParams from cache, projecting profiles to world plane (4-32 vertex transforms), running shoelace canonicalisation, then a fresh extrude_polygon + cap triangulation — could be ~50-100 microseconds per opening. At 5000 voids, that's 250-500 ms gross. Manifold's batch path on small prisms benchmarks around ~1-5 ms per opening (50ms for the 13-deep Sannergata fold is misleading — that's depth-amortised). So the WIN over Manifold for the trivial case might be 2-5x, not 10x. Still a win, but the claim needs a numeric backstop before retiring Manifold.

Allocation pattern in the slab-decomposition multi-cutter path is concerning: per host with N cutters at K distinct sweep intervals, you produce up to K+1 sub-prisms, each calling extrude_polygon (which itself allocates a fresh Vec<f32> vertex buffer, Vec<u32> index buffer, cap-triangulation earcut scratch). Then a vertex-weld pass that grid-hashes — HashMap allocation per host. For 5000 voids that's ~25k-50k transient HashMap allocations. The current Manifold path allocates once per batch_difference. Mitigation is straightforward (thread-local arena, reuse weld HashMap), but the design as written hands allocator pressure as an exercise to the reader and the LOC estimate doesn't include it.

i_overlay v7 in f32 mode is fast but the design specifies `f32` API for 'precision'. i_overlay internally snaps to integer grid for robustness — at f32 input and the suggested 1e-4 m tolerance, snap-grid resolution interacts with the 'opening exactly coplanar with wall face' edge case more delicately than the design admits. f64 mode is the safer default and only ~30% slower on small polygons. Performance loss is negligible; risk is hidden until weird geometries appear. Not a refutation, but the 'f32 for speed' framing is a premature optimization.

The dispatcher cost is non-trivial at scale. For every segment in `cut_openings::apply`, you do: axis-alignment check (2 matrix-vec mults + 1 dot product + abs/cmp), parametric metadata lookup, then either Layer A or fallback. The check itself is cheap (~100 ns) but it runs on EVERY void, including the ones that legitimately need Layer B. For 5000 voids of which 95% take Layer A and 5% take Layer B, the dispatcher overhead is negligible. But if PrismParams plumbing through LocalMesh adds 32-64 bytes per mesh (the design says 'Option<PrismParams>' which is ~80 bytes when populated), and we have ~10k LocalMesh allocations during a bundle, that's another ~800 KB of heap churn. Tiny in absolute terms but worth noting.

Wall-clock for 10k products / 5k voids: Manifold today (csg feature enabled) reportedly handles full Sannergata in seconds, not minutes. Layer A should improve the door/window subset 2-5x. BUT — Layer A's 5% miss rate routes to csgrs, which is a BSP-tree CSG that is ALGORITHMICALLY SLOWER than Manifold (Manifold uses GPU-style positional indexing + plane-based exact arithmetic; csgrs is naive BSP from 2014-era CSG.js). On the 5% misses, csgrs could be 10-30x slower than Manifold was on the same input. If those 5% are the geometrically heavy ones (multi-clip Sannergata-class walls), the worst case actually regresses. The design hand-waves this with 'cold path acceptable' but doesn't bound it.

The 'pre-union cutter footprints' optimization in subtract_many_prisms is correct in principle but i_overlay's union of N footprints is O(N log N + I) where I is intersection count. For a wall with 10 separate doors that don't overlap, that's a cheap pass. For a degenerate IFC with 100 overlapping cutter authoring errors, intersection count blows up. Not a correctness issue, just a latent perf cliff worth bounding.

Vertex welding with HashMap grid hash at 1e-4 m tolerance: classic correctness-vs-perf trap. HashMap is O(1) average but pathological if you bucket aggressively. With f32 keys quantized to 1e-4 m, the bucket function needs to handle the +/- epsilon boundary (a vertex at 0.00005 hashes differently from 0.00015 but they're 'the same' under tolerance). Standard fix is to check 27 neighbour buckets; that's 27x the hash lookups per weld. For ~24 weld vertices per sub-prism, ~3 sub-prisms per host, 5000 hosts: ~10M hash lookups. Manageable but not free; ~50-100 ms. The design says ~60 LOC; that's the minimal version, not the robust one.
verdicts.suggested_mitigation: Before retiring Manifold, ship Layer A as opt-in (feature flag `prism-csg-fast`) and run a head-to-head benchmark on Sannergata + 2-3 other production IFCs. Numbers to publish: wall-clock per cut_openings call, total bundle time, peak RSS, allocation count via dhat. Acceptance gate: Layer A must be >=2x faster on the common case AND not >3x slower than Manifold on the Layer B fallback subset (or have Manifold itself remain as the Layer B fallback under a second feature flag rather than csgrs). On allocations: add a thread-local arena (`bumpalo` or hand-rolled `Vec` reuse) for the per-cut transient buffers (profile projection, sub-prism vertex buffers, weld HashMap) BEFORE shipping. Add it to the LOC budget (~150 LOC). Default i_overlay to f64 mode; revisit only if benchmarks show >20% perf headroom available. For the Layer B regression risk: keep Manifold under a feature flag as the preferred fallback when `csg` feature is on, and only use csgrs when the user explicitly requests pure-Rust-no-cpp. This preserves the perf floor for users who don't care about the C++ dep.
verdicts.blocker: No published benchmark numbers comparing Layer A against Manifold's batch_difference on representative IFCs. The 10x speedup claim is asserted, not measured; the 95% hit rate is asserted, not instrumented. Without those numbers, the perf case for retiring Manifold is unfalsifiable — and the Layer B fallback (csgrs) is plausibly slower than the path it replaces.

concerns: Owned-code surface grows by ~1800 LOC (most novel: sweep-interval decomposition, slab partitioning, vertex welding, axis projection in prism_csg.rs). None of this exists in any library — we own it forever, including all future IFC-quirk patches. Compare to today's manifold path where the 3D Boolean itself is library code we don't touch.

Trades one C++ dep (manifold-csg) for TWO pure-Rust deps (i_overlay + csgrs), doubling library-bus-factor exposure. i_overlay is single-maintainer (iShape-Rust) and has churned its v6→v7 API; csgrs is a 231-star BSP-tree port with documented coplanar-face weaknesses that the design itself works around with an epsilon-perturbation hack. Neither has Manifold's depth of production use.

Tuning knob proliferation: AXIS_EPS (0.25 deg?), perturbation epsilon (1e-5 mm), weld tolerance (1e-4 m), slab-fallback policy, det(rotation) rigidity threshold (0.99). Each is a magic constant that will require empirical tuning as new IFC authoring quirks land. The open_questions section enumerates this debt explicitly. Today's manifold path has essentially zero such knobs.

Three-path dispatcher (Layer A parametric / Layer B csgrs / halfspace_clip) increases the test matrix combinatorially. Every future IFC quirk now needs verification across three paths plus dispatcher-routing rules. The 'NotParametric → fall through' fallback is silent — a regression in Layer A's detection could route 95% of cases to Layer B undetected without explicit instrumentation.

PrismParams plumbing is invasive: extending LocalMesh and threading params through boolean::retag/append touches every LocalMesh-producing call site (extrusion, brep, faceset, mapped, revolved, csg_primitive, curveset — 7 modules). Each becomes a change-amplifier for future refactors. The 'parallel HashMap keyed by step_id' alternative in open_questions is less invasive but the design itself leans toward the in-mesh struct.

csgrs pulls in nalgebra (~15 MB compile-time bloat the design acknowledges). Today's core crate doesn't use nalgebra; introducing it creates a precedent for further nalgebra-shaped solutions and adds wheel size + cold-build time across all 4 wheel-matrix platforms — exactly the dimension that manifold currently hurts on. Net wheel-size impact deserves a benchmark before commit.

Layer A's 'sub-prism stack + weld' approach to partial recesses and pockets is a known-fragile pattern: numerically-coincident seam vertices that fail to weld leave T-junctions and tiny holes. The design defers this to a 1e-4 m weld tolerance assertion in debug builds — silent leak in release. This is a category of bug Manifold simply doesn't have because it operates on a unified 3D representation.
verdicts.suggested_mitigation: Three concrete steps to bring maintenance cost firmly below Manifold's: (1) Add explicit dispatcher metrics — log/count Layer A success vs NotParametric divert vs Layer B invocations per bundle, surfaced via stats.rs, so silent regressions in parametric coverage are visible without re-running benchmarks; (2) Gate the two new crate deps behind a single workspace feature 'csg-rust' so reverting to manifold remains a one-line config change for at least two release cycles — and keep both paths exercised in CI until Layer A's coverage on the test corpus is measured at >95% on real files (Sannergata, LBK_ARK_E, etc.); (3) Extract the tuning knobs into a single `CsgPolicy` struct with documented defaults and a doc-test enumerating the choice rationale, so future tuning becomes a localised edit rather than scattered magic numbers. Also: skip the in-mesh PrismParams in favour of the parallel HashMap<step_id, PrismParams> noted in open_questions — drastically reduces the LocalMesh plumbing blast radius (from ~7 modules to 1 cache layer), at the cost of one lookup per Boolean op which is negligible against the 2D Boolean cost itself.

category.label: prism − IfcPolygonalBoundedHalfSpace
category.description: Already pure-Rust via mesh::halfspace_clip + boolean::polygonal_bounded_halfspace. Audit completeness of current implementation; identify gaps.

design.primary_algorithm: Already pure-Rust today and on the principled path. The path is: (1) `mesh::boolean::polygonal_bounded_halfspace` parses `IfcPolygonalBoundedHalfSpace(BaseSurface, AgreementFlag, Position, PolygonalBoundary)`, reads BaseSurface.Position.Axis for the cutting plane normal (GH #52 fix — NOT arg[2].Position, which is the polygon's local 2D frame), applies the de-facto `!AgreementFlag` flip (per ifcopenshell `IfcHalfSpaceSolid.cpp:33` + OCCT `BRepPrimAPI_MakeHalfSpace`), and emits a thin one-sided slab in BaseSurface.Position's frame tagged `halfspace_bounded:{agree|disagree}`. (2) The tessellator emits the slab as a regular operand mesh into the boolean tree (`boolean_first_operand|halfspace_bounded:agree` for the cutter side). (3) `mesh::cut_openings::apply` walks the cutter segments, recognises the halfspace tag via `is_halfspace_cutter`, calls `derive_plane_from_slab` to recover (plane_point=slab centroid, plane_normal=first triangle's CCW cross product) in world coords, and clips the host extrusion via `mesh::halfspace_clip::clip_by_plane` — NEVER manifold-csg. (4) `clip_by_plane` runs the canonical Sutherland–Hodgman tetrahedron clip: per-vertex signed-distance pass (eps=1e-3 mm, "on plane" → outside), per-triangle case split (3-in passthrough, 3-out drop, 1-in → one tri, 2-in → quad → two tris) with edge-intersection caching keyed by sorted endpoint pair (deduped vertices on the cut), boundary edges captured wound CCW seen from +plane_normal, edges stitched into loops via `stitch_boundary_loops` (next-vertex hashmap), each loop projected to 2D via an orthonormal basis (e1 = n×temp, e2 = n×e1) and earcut'd via `earcutr::earcut`, cap triangles lifted back to 3D and appended. (5) Output compacted via `compact_mesh` and returned as a closed manifold ready for downstream `subtract_many` on any remaining solid cutters in the same BCR. This matches the principled prior art exactly: ifcopenshell-OCCT special-cases halfspace at the mapping layer (its `IfcBooleanResult.cpp` iteratively flattens 8+-deep chains), ifcopenshell-CGAL has a `halfspace_tree` decomposition built but currently dormant, and web-ifc pre-merges stacked halfspaces. None of them feed halfspaces to a black-box mesh boolean. ifcfast is closer to the principled outcome than ifcopenshell-CGAL here. KNOWN GAP (audit finding): the PolygonalBoundary is currently NOT used to bound the cut — `clip_by_plane` treats every bounded halfspace as if it were an unbounded `IfcHalfSpaceSolid`. The slab is built with the polygon's footprint for visualisation only; the cut itself reads only the plane normal. This is correct for Revit's typical export (polygon oversized 5×5 m beyond wall footprint as a safety margin, so the cut equals an infinite plane cut over the host) and for the dominant Sannergata 13-deep slope-cut case, but it is wrong when the polygon is sized to the actual cut region (ifcopenshell-authored files, some ArchiCAD slope-step cuts, anything that needs the polygon to terminate the cut mid-host).
