The principled fix is a polygon-bounded variant: (a) clip host against the BaseSurface plane → get the cut polygon; (b) project the cut polygon and the IfcPolygonalBoundary onto a shared 2D frame (BaseSurface.Position's XY); (c) compute the 2D intersection of cut-cross-section ∩ polygon via `i_overlay::Overlay`/`geo::BooleanOps` (Boolean DIFFERENCE between the host volume and the prism `polygon × halfspace`); (d) for parts of the host that fall outside the polygon's footprint, KEEP them un-cut. Equivalently: the polygonal-bounded halfspace IS a prism (polygon extruded along the plane normal to infinity on the agreement side), and the cut becomes "host minus polygonal prism" — which collapses cleanly to a 2D Boolean once we slice the host on the plane. This is the same algorithm class as prism-minus-prism with a free top/bottom face for the cutter (handled by plane-clipping the host first, then 2D-Booleaning the resulting silhouette against the polygon). Until that gap is closed, ifcfast silently treats `IfcPolygonalBoundedHalfSpace` as `IfcHalfSpaceSolid` for cut purposes. Sannergata 97.4% match is the data point that says this is good enough for the dominant case.
prerequisites: Optional (to close the polygon-bounded gap): polygon-bounded halfspace algorithm — 2D Boolean DIFFERENCE of host-cross-section ∩ polygon-footprint in BaseSurface.Position's XY frame. Either (a) add `i_overlay` (v7, pure Rust, Apache-2/MIT, georust/geo adopted it) as a dependency and call `Overlay::with_subj_and_clip(...).overlay(OverlayRule::Difference)` (~150 LOC integration) OR (b) build a custom prism cutter: extract the polygon, build a closed prism mesh (polygon × HALFSPACE_PRISM_DEPTH along the plane normal, sized to ≥ host AABB diagonal on the agree side), pass through `csg::subtract_many` like any other solid cutter (~80 LOC) — but this re-introduces manifold-csg dependency for this case, which is exactly the failure mode GH #39 ran away from for deep stacks. (a) is the principled choice.

Optional: a polygonal-bounded vs unbounded discriminator on the cutter segment tag so `apply` can route bounded-with-tight-polygon cases to the 2D-Boolean path and oversized/unbounded cases to the existing `clip_by_plane` fast path. ~30 LOC tag inspection + heuristic (polygon AABB diagonal vs host AABB diagonal ratio).

Optional hardening: explicit IfcPlane validation in `polygonal_bounded_halfspace` — currently parses BaseSurface as `IfcPlane` but if BaseSurface is `IfcCylindricalSurface` / `IfcSphericalSurface` / `IfcBSplineSurface` (legal per IFC4 but rare in halfspace context) we'd silently fall through to identity. ~20 LOC typed Unsupported error + worklog entry.

Optional: `stitch_boundary_loops` graph-robustness — current implementation has a documented 'silent guard' for the pathological multiple-edges-sharing-a-start-vertex case. Replace with a proper edge-disjoint walk (consume edges from a `HashMap<u32, Vec<u32>>` of outgoing edges) so a self-intersecting cap polygon still produces all closed sub-loops. ~50 LOC.

Optional: `clip_by_plane` `ON_PLANE_EPS` made input-aware — currently fixed at 1e-3 mm; for files in metres or with wildly far-from-origin geometry the conservative 'drop sub-mm slivers' assumption may bite. Plumb a tolerance argument from the mesh's unit_scale. ~15 LOC.
edge_cases: Wall tilted 30° from vertical (slope cut, Sannergata-class): WORKS. `halfspace_clip` is fully plane-agnostic — it reads BaseSurface.Position.Axis (GH #52 fix routes this correctly), classifies vertices by signed distance, and clips along the actual world-space plane. Covered by `deep_bcr_with_three_halfspaces_cuts_correctly` integration test (3 stacked axis-aligned planes) and Sannergata 97.4% match (379/389 walls including 13-deep stacks).

Opening cutting-plane exactly coplanar with wall face: `ON_PLANE_EPS = 1e-3 mm` treats coplanar vertices as 'outside' (conservative). Result: the wall face is dropped, the cap polygon is the wall face itself. Volume-correct; topology has a degenerate sliver of zero thickness that earcut handles but is geometrically pointless. Acceptable.

Polygonal boundary sized TIGHT to actual cut region (not Revit's 5×5 m oversize convention): CURRENT GAP — boundary is ignored, cut behaves as unbounded halfspace, host is over-cut. Affects ifcopenshell-authored files, some ArchiCAD. Mitigation: would need the 2D-Boolean polygon-clipping prerequisite. Frequency in Revit-dominated real-world data: low (every Revit slope-cut authoring oversizes by safety margin per Revit's IFC exporter convention).

Polygonal boundary entirely outside host footprint in BaseSurface.Position's XY: CURRENT GAP — `clip_by_plane` still cuts along the infinite plane. Real-world frequency: near zero (a polygon outside the host has no semantic reason to exist), but Tekla / synthetic files occasionally emit malformed BCR trees with this shape. Result: host gets over-cut. Mitigation: same as above (2D-Boolean check).

Polygon winding (CW Revit vs CCW ifcopenshell): IRRELEVANT to current path — `derive_plane_from_slab` reads the first SLAB triangle's normal, which is determined by `extrude_polygon`'s consistent CCW-after-orientation policy, not by the input polygon's winding. The cut direction is therefore robust to authoring-tool polygon convention.

Polygon non-convex with holes (multi-component polygonal boundary): IfcPolygonalBoundedHalfSpace's PolygonalBoundary is by IFC4 spec an `IfcBoundedCurve` (single closed curve, no holes). Spec-compliant input cannot have holes. If a non-convex polygon is supplied, `polygonal_bounded_halfspace` extrudes it via `extrude_polygon` which routes through earcut — works for the slab visualisation. Cut path doesn't care (boundary is ignored, see above).

Polygon self-intersecting (synthetic / malformed input): `bounded_curve_points` returns the raw points; earcut on a self-intersecting polygon may emit garbage triangles for the slab visualisation but `derive_plane_from_slab` reads only the first triangle's normal — robust to slab degeneracy. Cut still uses the (correct) plane.

Opening box extends beyond wall thickness on both sides: trivially handled — `clip_by_plane` is a half-space cut, not a containment check; it just removes everything on the +normal side of the plane. The polygon's depth in the agreement direction is mathematically infinite, so 'extends beyond' is not a meaningful concept for this shape. Distinct from prism-minus-prism's boundary-straddling case.

Multiple nested halfspaces in a deep BCR (Sannergata: 13-deep): `apply` partitions ALL halfspace segments out of the cutter list and runs `clip_by_plane` sequentially on the host (cut_openings.rs:115-126). This is the GH #39 win: each clip stays a pure-Rust plane operation, no Manifold tolerance propagation, no result collapse. Covered by `deep_bcr_with_three_halfspaces_cuts_correctly`.

Nested halfspace clip COMBINED with cross-product opening cut (IfcRelVoidsElement opening on a sloped wall): `apply` runs first (in-rep: extrusion minus its inline halfspaces → reduced wall mesh), THEN `CrossProductCut::flush` folds the opening via `csg::subtract_many` on the already-clipped host. Composes correctly — half-space output is closed-manifold-valid so Manifold accepts it. Documented at cut_openings.rs:368-385 ('per-host sequence').

BaseSurface is not IfcPlane (IfcCylindricalSurface, IfcSphericalSurface, IfcBSplineSurface): CURRENT BEHAVIOUR — `polygonal_bounded_halfspace` returns `None` (drops the cutter entirely), so the host passes through un-cut → Outcome::Passthrough. IFC4 spec permits non-planar BaseSurface but real-world frequency is essentially zero. Improvement opportunity: emit a typed Unsupported warning so the symptom is visible.

Slab degenerate (zero-area first triangle from a collinear polygon): `derive_plane_from_slab` returns None; `is_halfspace_cutter` segment is silently dropped; the host passes through. Visible as Outcome::Passthrough rather than a hard error — acceptable.

Far-from-origin host (wall at world coordinates > 1e6 mm): `clip_by_plane` operates on f32 vertices in the LOCAL-rebased frame (the streaming pipeline rebases to `LocalMesh.rep_origin` / `mesh_anchor` per GH-far-origin-precision fix). 1e-3 mm tolerance is safe in the local frame. WOULD break if the rebase were skipped — but it never is on the production path.

Polygon Position's XY frame diverges from BaseSurface.Position's XY frame (GH #52 wall #50724): the slab visualisation polygon footprint will look 'sheared' in world but the CUT is still correct because `derive_plane_from_slab` reads the first triangle's normal in world coords, which lives in BaseSurface.Position.Axis. Documented at boolean.rs:215-220.

AgreementFlag default missing (some IFC2x3 exports): `parse_agreement_flag` defaults to `true` (boolean.rs:113-119) — matches Revit/ArchiCAD/Tekla majority convention. Safe default; rare misfire on synthetic / IFC2x3 files.
design.loc_estimate: ~600 LOC pure-Rust today (halfspace_clip.rs 524 + polygonal_bounded_halfspace ~100 in boolean.rs + derive_plane_from_slab ~50 in cut_openings.rs). Optional gap-closing additions: 2D-Boolean polygon-bounded variant ~150 LOC (with i_overlay) + boundary-tightness discriminator ~30 LOC + non-planar BaseSurface typed warning ~20 LOC + stitch_boundary_loops robustness ~50 LOC. Total if all optional work shipped: ~850 LOC.
open_questions: Is the polygonal-boundary-ignored gap actually a problem on real ifcopenshell-authored files? We have 97.4% Sannergata match (Revit-authored) without enforcing the boundary. Need a counter-example file (ifcopenshell-test-files / IFC4 conformance suite) where the polygon is sized tight enough that ignoring it produces a visibly wrong cut. Until we see one in the test suite, the principled-but-unneeded i_overlay integration is busywork.

Should the bounded-vs-unbounded heuristic be ratio-based (polygon AABB diagonal ÷ host AABB diagonal) or tag-based (caller declares 'tight boundary')? Heuristic is fragile (Revit's 5×5 m oversize on a 50 m wall is ratio < 1 but still semantically unbounded); tag requires per-cutter author classification which we don't get from IFC.

Should `clip_by_plane` accept a tolerance argument? Currently fixed `ON_PLANE_EPS = 1e-3 mm`. For files in metres, vertices in the local frame still have similar magnitudes after rebase, so the absolute eps still maps to sub-millimetre precision. The likely answer: no, but document the invariant in the function doc rather than relying on the inline comment.

Is the `stitch_boundary_loops` pathological-graph silent guard ever hit in practice? No evidence in worklogs / GH issues. A test fixture that intentionally provokes it would let us decide whether to harden or leave the documented limitation.

The slab visualisation uses the polygon's footprint in BaseSurface.Position's frame (GH #52 fix) instead of arg[2].Position's frame, which is a visualisation regression vs IFC spec — the polygon is supposed to live in arg[2].Position's XY. Reverting that requires re-deriving the cut plane from somewhere else than the slab. Worth re-opening when GH #52 follow-ups land, but not a correctness issue for the cut itself.

concerns: The PolygonalBoundary is silently ignored — this is what makes the shape category 'bounded' in the first place. The design itself flags this as a 'KNOWN GAP' but argues it's acceptable because Revit's exporter convention happens to oversize the polygon. That is a Revit-specific statistical observation, not correctness. For a worked example with a tight-bounded polygon (an ifcopenshell-authored door opening polygon sized exactly to the 0.9×2.1 m door footprint on a wall face), `clip_by_plane` cuts the entire infinite plane through the host — removing roughly half the wall (~1.5 m³ on a 0.75 m³ host clamped to volume) instead of the expected 0.4725 m³ door volume. The result is silently and catastrophically wrong, with no diagnostic surfaced. The category description says 'audit completeness'; the audit verdict against the *category's own semantics* is incomplete.

Worked-example trace fails: host wall 1×0.25×3 m (vol 0.75 m³) with a polygonal-bounded halfspace door opening (0.9×2.1 m polygon, depth-into-wall agreement) should yield 0.75 − 0.4725 = 0.2775 m³. Current implementation: agreement flag drives plane orientation, `clip_by_plane` removes ALL of one half (likely 0.375 m³ if plane is at mid-thickness, or 0 m³ if at one face, or 0.75 m³ if at the other face). The expected output is 0.2775 m³; the achievable output set under the current algorithm is {0, 0.375, 0.75} — none of them right. The design fails its own worked example.

`derive_plane_from_slab` reads the *first triangle* of the slab's index buffer and assumes it is a top-cap CCW triangle. That's a contract on `extrude_polygon`'s output order, not a property derivable from the slab alone. If a future refactor of `extrude_polygon` (or a different code path that happens to share the tag) emits a side-wall triangle first, the derived 'plane normal' would be the polygon-edge direction crossed with the extrusion direction — perpendicular to the actual plane. The downstream clip would then cut along an entirely wrong plane, silently. There is no assertion that the first triangle's normal is parallel to the slab's extrusion direction.

`stitch_boundary_loops` uses `HashMap<u32, u32>` for `next`, which means *only one outgoing edge per start vertex* is ever recorded. The 'later one overrides' branch silently drops edges. When the clip plane intersects a host in a way that produces a non-simple boundary (e.g. the plane grazes a vertex where two surface loops meet, or the host has internal voids the plane intersects), edges are dropped and the cap is triangulated against an incomplete loop. The output is then a non-manifold mesh that downstream `csg::subtract_many` may or may not survive. The design dismisses this as a 'pathological case'; the function name `stitch_boundary_loops` (plural) suggests the author already knew about it but never closed it.

Cap triangulation never uses holes. `triangulate_cap_loop` is called once per loop with `earcutr::earcut(&flat_2d, &[], 2)` — empty hole list. If a single clip produces TWO boundary loops where one should be a hole inside the other (a doughnut host clipped by a plane through both sides of the hole), both loops are filled solid. Result: the doughnut hole is silently capped over. The design assumes 'walls with cavities produce one loop per connected component' — this is true for the simple case but wrong as a general invariant of half-space clipping.

`ON_PLANE_EPS = 1e-3` is in source units, with no `unit_scale` plumbing. The comment claims '1 mm' but the function takes raw f32 vertices. For a file authored in millimetres, 1e-3 source-units = 1 µm, two orders of magnitude tighter than building tolerance — fine. For a file in metres, 1e-3 = 1 mm — what the comment says. For a file in feet or inches, 1e-3 source-units ≠ 1 mm. The clipping behaviour (which vertices are treated as 'on plane → outside') therefore changes silently per source unit. The design's open_questions concedes this and says 'the likely answer: no [we don't fix it]', but a tolerance that changes behaviour with file metadata is a correctness bug, not a documentation gap.

Sequential half-space clips accumulate the 'on-plane → outside' bias. Over a 13-deep stack (Sannergata-class), any vertex that lands within ON_PLANE_EPS of any of the 13 planes gets dropped. With 13 conservative cuts in series, a vertex 1.3e-2 mm from a face can be classified as outside even though no individual plane should remove it. The 97.4% Sannergata match is presented as evidence that this is fine, but the 2.6% gap (10 of 389 walls) is exactly the kind of cumulative-tolerance failure this design is most exposed to — and the design does not investigate those 10.

The `is_halfspace_cutter` check matches *any* link in the source chain containing 'halfspace_plane' or 'halfspace_bounded'. If a halfspace cutter is itself the second operand of an outer boolean (e.g. a polygonal-bounded halfspace nested inside another BCR), the tag survives but the geometry has been transformed by the outer boolean's frame. `derive_plane_from_slab` then derives a plane in the wrong frame. Documented at cut_openings.rs:641 (the tag chain test covers it lexically) but the geometric implication isn't verified.

AgreementFlag defaulting to `true` on missing input (boolean.rs:113-119) is presented as 'matches Revit/ArchiCAD/Tekla majority convention'. For an IFC2x3 file where the flag was explicitly *intended* to be `false` but parsed as missing, the cut runs in the opposite direction — keeping what should be removed. This is correctness-by-popularity, not correctness.
verdicts.blocker: For the worked example in the prompt (host wall 1×0.25×3 m, opening 0.9×0.3×2.1 m at one face with a tightly-bounded polygon), the implementation cannot produce the expected 0.2775 m³ result. Because the polygonal boundary is ignored, the clip cuts along an infinite plane through the wall, yielding one of {0, 0.375, 0.75} m³ depending on plane position. The design's own admitted KNOWN GAP — bounded vs unbounded — is the defining property of this shape category. The Sannergata 97.4% statistic is for a Revit-authored corpus where the bound is conventionally oversized, which is the case in which a polygonal-bounded halfspace is *indistinguishable from an unbounded one*; it does not generalise. Until either (a) a 2D-Boolean polygon-bounded variant is implemented or (b) a bounded-vs-unbounded discriminator triages tight polygons to a different code path, the prism-minus-bounded-halfspace category is correctness-defective for the specific case it is named after.
verdicts.suggested_mitigation: Two-track fix. (1) Implement the polygon-bounded variant the design itself describes in open_questions: slice the host on the BaseSurface plane to get the cut polygon, project it and the IfcPolygonalBoundary onto a shared 2D frame (BaseSurface.Position.XY), compute their intersection via `i_overlay` Boolean DIFFERENCE, and only remove the host volume where the cut polygon ∩ boundary polygon is non-empty. Pure-Rust, ~150 LOC. This is the principled fix; the design names it but defers it. (2) For the interim, add a tag-based discriminator at parse time in `polygonal_bounded_halfspace`: compute polygon AABB diagonal vs host AABB diagonal (host AABB available from indexer). If polygon < 0.7 × host AABB diagonal, emit a typed `Unsupported("tight polygonal-bounded halfspace")` warning and fall back to Outcome::Passthrough rather than silently over-cutting. The current behaviour — silent over-cut — is the worst correctness outcome because consumers cannot detect it. Separately: harden `derive_plane_from_slab` to verify the first triangle's normal is parallel to the slab's longest axis (the extrusion direction) before trusting it, and replace `stitch_boundary_loops`' `HashMap<u32, u32>` with a `HashMap<u32, Vec<u32>>` so multi-outgoing-edge graphs produce all sub-loops. Make `ON_PLANE_EPS` input-aware via the LocalMesh unit_scale so the tolerance is a consistent fraction of a millimetre regardless of source units.

concerns: Cap-triangulation cannot handle host with internal cavities. `triangulate_cap_loop` calls `earcutr::earcut(&flat_2d, &[], 2)` with the holes argument fixed to `&[]`. When the host already contains an opening (e.g., a wall with a previously-cut IfcRelVoidsElement door) and the halfspace plane intersects both the outer surface AND the cavity surface, `stitch_boundary_loops` returns two disjoint loops — one outer perimeter and one inner hole. The code currently triangulates each loop as an independent solid outer polygon and fills the void on the cut plane, producing a topologically-wrong result (cap is no longer manifold against the host's interior void). This case is not in the edge_cases list and not gated by any check. It WILL fire on the documented composition pattern at cut_openings.rs:368-385 where halfspaces run BEFORE cross-product openings — but it'll also fire when halfspaces run AFTER in-rep openings within the same extrusion.

`stitch_boundary_loops` silently corrupts on the multi-edge-per-start case. The `next: HashMap<u32, u32>` keyed on start vertex means `next.insert(s, e)` for a repeated `s` overrides the earlier edge. This isn't a synthetic concern: it fires whenever the boundary graph has a T-junction or pinch point on the cap, which happens when the cutter plane passes through a host vertex that's shared by 4+ triangles (extrusion seam intersected at a profile vertex — common for prismatic walls). The 'silent guard' comment at line 318-319 explicitly acknowledges this as a known limitation, but it's classified 'Optional hardening' rather than treated as a robustness gate. Concrete failure: a wall whose extrusion seam runs vertically and a halfspace plane that grazes the seam produces a boundary graph with the seam vertex as a shared start, half the cap edges get dropped, earcut emits non-closed cap, downstream `subtract_many` rejects the mesh as non-manifold.

Polygonal boundary ignored — design's own audit flags this. The current `apply` path treats every `IfcPolygonalBoundedHalfSpace` as if the polygon were infinite, so a tight-bounded polygon over-cuts the host. Sannergata 97.4% match (10 of 389 walls visibly wrong) is presented as evidence this is fine; on a robustness lens 2.6% failure rate on the DOMINANT Revit-authored case is not robust, it's a documented bug. ifcopenshell-authored test fixtures (where polygons are typically sized to actual cut region, not 5×5 m oversized) will fail visibly. The mitigation (i_overlay 2D Boolean) is listed as 'Optional' in prerequisites.

ON_PLANE_EPS = 1e-3 mm is a hard-coded scale-dependent constant with an unenforced invariant. Function contract says vertices arrive 'in the wall's local-rebased frame, so values rarely exceed 1e5 mm' — that invariant lives entirely outside this module. If a caller ever invokes `clip_by_plane` with world-space UTM coordinates (e.g., a future code path that skips the rebase, or a regression that breaks `LocalMesh.rep_origin`), f32 precision at 6.6e6 mm yields ~0.5 mm absolute error, comparable to the eps. Vertices that should classify as 'on plane' instead classify randomly as inside/outside per round-off, and the cap topology becomes non-deterministic. Listed as 'Optional' hardening.

Coplanar host-face / cutter-plane case produces an open mesh. The design claims 'wall face is dropped, the cap polygon is the wall face itself, volume-correct.' But if the host face IS the boundary set (every edge of a host face lies on the plane), `stitch_boundary_loops` walks the perimeter of that face as a single loop, the cap triangulation re-creates the same face (CCW from +n), and the original face was already DROPPED in Phase 2 (all-outside triangles). Net result: the cap replaces the face, but with potentially-reversed winding (the cap is wound CCW from +plane_normal; the original host face was wound CCW from outside-the-host). If those directions disagree (host normal = -plane_normal, which is the agreement-side coplanar case), the cap is inside-out and the mesh is non-manifold. No test fixture covers this.

`derive_plane_from_slab` reads the FIRST slab triangle's normal — if the polygon was extruded with a non-convex outline and earcut emits a degenerate or reversed first triangle (which can happen for self-intersecting or near-collinear polygons), the cut plane normal is wrong by sign or direction. Design states 'derive_plane_from_slab reads only the first triangle's normal — robust to slab degeneracy', but a degenerate first triangle (zero-area) makes the cross product zero, `polygonal_bounded_halfspace` is supposed to handle this by returning None, but I didn't see explicit guard in the algorithm description — combined with the 'silent passthrough' fallback this means malformed cutters cause hosts to silently un-cut.

Cap loops with shared interior vertices (figure-8 from a non-simple cut polygon) are silently truncated. The `visited` map in `stitch_boundary_loops` is keyed by vertex index — once a vertex is visited in one loop, the second loop sharing that vertex is broken at that point. Real-world cause: a host with a thin web (I-beam profile) clipped through both flanges and the web at once. The boundary graph is two disjoint cycles connected at the web's two endpoints; the walker breaks at the shared vertex and emits one truncated loop instead of two.
verdicts.blocker: Host with a pre-existing internal cavity (a wall already cut by an IfcRelVoidsElement opening) clipped by an IfcPolygonalBoundedHalfSpace whose plane passes through both the outer surface and the cavity surface. `stitch_boundary_loops` returns two loops (outer perimeter + inner hole), `triangulate_cap_loop` is hard-coded to `earcutr::earcut(&flat_2d, &[], 2)` with no holes argument, so each loop is triangulated as an independent solid outer polygon and the void gets filled on the cap plane. The result is topologically wrong (cap is non-manifold against the host's interior void) and downstream `csg::subtract_many` will reject it. This composition (halfspace + in-rep opening on the same host) is documented as supported at cut_openings.rs:368-385 and is the exact Sannergata-class scenario — a sloped wall with a window opening — that the GH #39 work was supposed to make robust.
verdicts.suggested_mitigation: Three changes, in order of robustness impact. (1) Detect and handle cap loops with holes: classify the loops returned by `stitch_boundary_loops` into nested groups via a 2D point-in-polygon test in the (e1, e2) basis (outer loop contains inner loops); pass nested children to `earcutr::earcut` as holes via the second argument. ~80 LOC. This closes the host-with-cavity blocker. (2) Replace `stitch_boundary_loops`'s start-vertex HashMap with an edge-disjoint walk over `HashMap<u32, Vec<u32>>` of outgoing edges, consuming edges as they're walked. ~50 LOC (already in 'optional hardening'). This closes the T-junction / shared-vertex silent-edge-drop. (3) Add a typed Unsupported error path for the polygonal-boundary-tight case — at minimum, detect when polygon AABB diagonal is < (some fraction of) host AABB diagonal and emit a logged warning so the over-cut is visible to consumers. ~30 LOC. Then either ship i_overlay-based 2D-Boolean polygon clipping as the principled fix (~150 LOC) or accept the loud-warning state as good enough until a counter-example appears in the test suite.

concerns: Per-clip `out_v = vertices.to_vec()` (halfspace_clip.rs:110) clones the entire vertex buffer at the start of every clip. For a 13-deep BCR stack on a wall with N vertices, that's 13×N floats moved — pure waste, since most clips touch <5% of vertices. Could be in-place: maintain a single `out_v` across the stack and only append cut-edge intersection points.

`compact_mesh` runs as the final phase of EVERY `clip_by_plane` call (line 248). For a deep stack, only the final compact matters; intermediate compactions allocate a fresh HashMap<u32,u32> + two Vecs and remap every surviving index, then immediately get fed back into the next clip as the new full-input buffer. Net cost: D × O(I) HashMap-based remap work where D-1 of them are thrown away.

Four HashMap allocations per clip (`edge_cache`, `next`, `visited`, `remap`) with std-default SipHash. Replace with `FxHashMap` (1-2 ns/lookup vs ~8 ns) or — since vertex indices are dense u32 — flat Vec<u32> sentinel arrays sized to nv. For a deep stack × 10k walls this is measurable allocator churn.

No AABB-vs-plane early reject. A clip whose plane misses the host AABB (very common in Revit's oversized polygon-bounded halfspaces — the polygon is 5×5 m but the wall might be 0.5×3 m and entirely on the keep side) still walks every vertex via the signed-distance pass before hitting the `in_count == nv` fast-path on line 99. Cheap fix (~8 dot products on AABB corners) saves O(V) per missed clip.

Earcut on long cap loops is O(L²) worst case in `earcutr`. A clip plane that slices a wall lengthwise produces a long, thin cap polygon (hundreds of vertices on a tessellated curved wall). Not pathological per the Sannergata data, but worth flagging vs the design's claimed O(triangles) framing.

Sequential half-space clips within a deep stack (cut_openings.rs:115-126) — no batching opportunity. Each clip's output is the next clip's full input. For a 13-deep stack this is intrinsically serial; can't be parallelised the way per-product tessellation is. Product-level parallelism (already in place) is the only available axis, so deep-stack walls are throughput bottlenecks unless they're rare.

Optional polygon-bounded variant via `i_overlay` (suggested in prerequisites) WOULD be materially slower per cut than the current 'ignore polygon' path. 2D Boolean DIFFERENCE on a non-trivial polygon is typically 10-100× the cost of a single plane clip. The current pragmatic choice (skip the 2D Boolean for the 97.4% Revit case) is the right perf call; only switch on a detected tight-boundary signal.

Wall-clock vs manifold for common case (10k products, shallow halfspace cuts): plane-clip path is materially FASTER — no BVH build, no constructor overhead, no tolerance-propagation step. Likely 5-50× faster per shallow halfspace cut. For the 5k IfcRelVoidsElement (cross-product openings), the path is unchanged (still `csg::subtract_many` per line 140), so no regression there. For deep-stack walls, manifold isn't a comparator (it returned wrong answers per GH #39), so 'vs manifold' is undefined.
verdicts.suggested_mitigation:
Three high-ROI perf improvements, all <100 LOC each: (1) AABB-vs-plane early reject before the signed-distance pass — saves O(V) on every clip whose plane misses the host (likely the common case for Revit's oversized polygon-bounded halfspaces). (2) Defer `compact_mesh` until the END of the halfspace stack in `cut_openings::apply`, not inside each `clip_by_plane` — saves D-1 HashMap-based remap passes per deep-stack wall. Pass a `compact: bool` parameter or split `clip_by_plane` into `clip_by_plane_no_compact` + a final `compact_mesh` call after the loop. (3) Replace std HashMap with `FxHashMap` or — since indices are dense u32 — flat `Vec<i32>` sentinel arrays (init to -1) for `edge_cache`, `next`, `visited`, `remap`. Expected combined speedup on deep-stack walls: 2-3×. None of this is required for correctness; defer until a profile shows cut_openings is on the critical path.</suggested_mitigation>
<parameter name="blocker">

concerns: Three documented 'tuning hooks' (ON_PLANE_EPS=1e-3 mm magic number, stitch_boundary_loops silent guard, non-IfcPlane BaseSurface silent passthrough) are each individually small but collectively form a 'long tail' of issues that will trickle in as users hit edge cases — each one will look like a mystery bug to the reporter ('my wall doesn't cut') because the failure modes are silent (Outcome::Passthrough or over-cut) rather than typed warnings. Maintenance cost = N debugging round-trips, where N grows with user base.

The polygon-bounded gap is honestly flagged but the 'is this a problem?' question stays open. If a real ifcopenshell-authored counter-example surfaces in the wild, the i_overlay integration becomes urgent and is ~150 LOC + a new dep that georust/geo also depends on (so transitively healthy) but still adds a build-graph node. Cost is bounded and the design correctly defers it — but the cost IS real and will eventually be paid.

earcutr at v0.4 is on crates.io but the upstream Rust port has had thin maintenance compared to mapbox/earcut.js; if it goes unmaintained we'd own ~600 LOC of cap-triangulation eventually. Mitigation is trivial (vendor it, ~1 day work; alternatives exist e.g. spade/lyon_tessellation), so this is a Tier-2 risk, not Tier-1.

The four 'Optional' prerequisites in the design are honestly labelled as optional but the file mentions five separate places where future work might land (2D-Boolean polygon path, tag-vs-ratio discriminator, non-planar BaseSurface typed warning, stitch_boundary_loops graph robustness, input-aware tolerance). Each is ~20-150 LOC of pure-Rust work. The maintenance debt to FIX all of them is bounded (~265 LOC), but the cognitive debt of 'is this one of the gaps?' will recur on every halfspace-shaped bug report for the lifetime of the project until they're closed.
verdicts.suggested_mitigation: Two cheap wins to neutralise the maintenance-cost concerns: (1) Convert the three 'silent guards' (ON_PLANE_EPS, stitch_boundary_loops cap, non-IfcPlane BaseSurface) to typed Warning events surfaced on a debug channel — turns mystery bug reports into self-diagnosing logs. ~40 LOC total. (2) Add a single integration fixture per known-gap edge case (polygon-tight-to-cut, polygon-outside-host, non-IfcPlane BaseSurface) that asserts current behaviour (Outcome::Passthrough or over-cut) so any future code change that touches this path either preserves the documented behaviour or intentionally fixes it. Locks in the design contract. ~80 LOC of tests. Do NOT add i_overlay until a real-world counter-example surfaces — that's busywork per the open_questions correctly notes.

category.label: prism − IfcCsgPrimitive3D (cylinder/sphere/cone)
category.description: Less common but real — MEP penetrations as cylinders.

design.primary_algorithm:
The category splits cleanly along a math-structure axis once you look at what an IfcCsgPrimitive3D actually IS. Four of the five subtypes are GEOMETRICALLY a prism (a closed swept-polygon-or-degenerate-polygon solid), and only IfcSphere is irreducibly a 3D solid. So the algorithm is a dispatch on cutter subtype, with every subtype routed to the right primitive operation rather than treating the category as monolithic.

**Step 1 — partition cutters during cut_openings::apply() and CrossProductCut::flush()**

In the cutter-partitioning loop already present at /home/edkjo/workspace/inbox/ifcfast/crates/core/src/mesh/cut_openings.rs:87-106, add a third bucket alongside `halfspace_planes` and `solid_cutters`:

```rust
let mut halfspace_planes: Vec<(Vec3, Vec3)> = Vec::new();
let mut prism_cutters: Vec<PrismCutter> = Vec::new();   // NEW
let mut general_solid_cutters: Vec<(Vec<f32>, Vec<u32>)> = Vec::new();
```

Recognize CSG-primitive cutters by their `source` tag — already emitted distinctly by mesh/mod.rs:1091-1100 as `csg_block`, `csg_cylinder`, `csg_cone`, `csg_sphere`, `csg_pyramid`. Walk the `|`-chain like `is_halfspace_cutter` does.

**Step 2 — reduce four of five subtypes to a parametric prism cutter, not a mesh**

Where ifcfast's CSG primitives currently round-trip through `csg_primitive::*` returning a tessellated `LocalMesh` and then through Manifold, we instead recover the analytic form. New module `crates/core/src/mesh/prism_cutter.rs`:

```rust
pub enum PrismCutter {
    /// Closed prism defined parametrically: a 2D profile + 3D placement + depth.
    /// Captures IfcExtrudedAreaSolid AND the prism-shaped IfcCsgPrimitive3D subtypes:
    ///   - IfcBlock           -> rectangle profile, depth = ZLength
    ///   - IfcRightCircularCylinder -> N-gon (24 segments) circle, depth = Height
    ///   - IfcRectangularPyramid    -> rectangle profile, taper to apex (special-cased)
    ///   - IfcRightCircularCone     -> N-gon circle, taper to apex (special-cased)
    Prism { profile: Polygon2D, placement: Mat4, depth: f32, taper: Taper },
    /// Genuine 3D solid (sphere; future: ellipsoid, NURBS). Keep as a mesh and
    /// hand to the general engine.
    Mesh(Vec<f32>, Vec<u32>),
}

pub enum Taper {
    None,           // cylinder, block
    ToApex(Vec3),   // cone, pyramid -> profile collapses linearly to an apex point
}
```

A constructor `from_csg_primitive(table, id)` re-parses the same fields `csg_primitive::block/cylinder/cone/pyramid` already read, but emits a `Prism` variant instead of a tessellated mesh. For `IfcSphere`, fall through to `Mesh` (re-tessellated via existing `csg_primitive::sphere`).

**Step 3 — solve "prism − prism" with a 2D-Boolean + extrude algorithm (the core trick)**

This is the load-bearing primitive the entire shape category collapses onto. Algorithm:

1. Snap the cutter into the host's extrusion frame. Let `H` be the host prism (the IfcExtrudedAreaSolid wall/slab/column) with profile `P_h` in plane π_h (origin `O_h`, basis `e1_h, e2_h`, sweep direction `d_h`, depth `Δ_h`). Let `C` be the cutter prism (profile `P_c`, plane π_c, direction `d_c`, depth `Δ_c`).
2. Test parallelism of sweep directions: `|d_h · d_c| > 1 - 1e-4`. THREE CASES.

**Case A — Coaxial sweep (host and cutter swept along the same axis, e.g. a vertical pipe through a vertical wall in elevation? — almost never; or a vertical cylinder through a horizontal slab? — never, axes differ by 90°). Rare in practice. Same algorithm as Case C, no shortcut.**

**Case B — Perpendicular sweep (the dominant real case: vertical wall, horizontal pipe penetration; or horizontal slab, vertical cylinder penetration). `d_h ⟂ d_c`.**

This is the case where 2D Boolean dominates. Project the cutter prism's silhouette onto the host's cross-section plane π_h:
   - Sample the cutter as a closed 3D mesh (cylinder = 24-gon × Δ_c, etc.)
   - For each vertex of the cutter mesh, project onto π_h: `(u, v) = (v - O_h) · (e1_h, e2_h)`
   - Compute the 2D convex/non-convex hull of projected points by edge-walk — for our four prisms the silhouette outline is closed-form: cylinder→2D rectangle Δ_c × 2R in (d_c, axis_perp_h) basis projected onto (e1_h, e2_h); block→2D rectangle (oriented); cone→2D trapezoid; pyramid→2D trapezoid. Each closed-form projection is ≤30 LOC per primitive.

Then **do a 2D polygon Boolean difference** `P_h_new = P_h \ P_silhouette` using the `i_overlay` crate (already a candidate for ifcfast — it backs the georust ecosystem and is pure-Rust, f64, OGC-valid). Specifically:
   - `i_overlay::core::overlay::Overlay::with_paths(&[p_h_outer], &[p_h_holes]).overlay_with_paths(&[p_silhouette], OverlayRule::Difference, FillRule::EvenOdd)`
   - Result: a polygon-with-holes representing the host's new cross-section.

Finally **re-extrude** the new profile to depth Δ_h using ifcfast's existing extrusion path (`mesh::extrusion::extrude` body, refactored to a `build_prism(profile, placement, depth)` helper). The output mesh is the host with the cutter cleanly removed — closed, manifold, outward-wound by construction.

This is what production tools secretly do for the "round hole in wall" case if they were rewritten today: web-ifc lifts the cutter to a mesh and runs fuzzy-bools because it generalizes to all cutter types; ifcopenshell-OCCT goes through generic BRep Boolean. We're claiming the principled fast path for the dominant geometric case.

**Case C — Generic (sweep directions not aligned, e.g. tilted cone or sphere)**. Fall back to the general 3D solid subtractor (Step 5 below).

**Step 4 — handle the taper variants**

- `IfcRightCircularCylinder`: Taper::None — pure prism, Case B works as-is.
- `IfcBlock`: Taper::None — pure prism, Case B works as-is.
- `IfcRectangularPyramid`: Taper::ToApex. Pyramid's silhouette in Case B is a TRAPEZOID (or triangle if axis perpendicular). The 2D-Boolean approach still works because the silhouette is a closed 2D polygon — we just compute it analytically (4 vertices: base corners projected, apex projected, hull walk).
- `IfcRightCircularCone`: Taper::ToApex with a circular base. Silhouette in Case B is a 2D trapezoid (rectangle morphing to a line at the apex side) or triangle. Project base 24-gon and apex, take 2D hull.

For non-perpendicular cones/pyramids (rare), fall through to Case C.

**Step 5 — IfcSphere and Case-C fallback: pure-Rust general mesh CSG via csgrs**

The audit notes csgrs (pure Rust, MIT, v0.20.1, 231 stars, the most mature pure-Rust 3D-mesh-CSG kernel; BSP-tree-based, port of csg.js lineage) handles arbitrary closed meshes. For:
- IfcSphere as cutter (essentially never happens in real files)
- Tilted cylinders/cones whose sweep axis doesn't align with host's sweep
- Future-proof general fallback

We add a thin feature-gated path `geom::csg_pure` that wraps csgrs the same way `geom::csg` wraps manifold-csg. Same API surface (`subtract_many`), same error type. Compile-time feature-flag `csg-pure` (default OFF until validated; once green, swap default).

For now: **keep manifold-csg as the Case-C fallback behind the `csg` feature for one release**, ship the Case-B fast path, and migrate Case-C to csgrs in a follow-up once we've validated csgrs handles building-element f32-precision and coplanar-face robustness. **THIS IS THE "MIXED" RECOMMENDATION.**

**Step 6 — composition with the existing pipeline**

The new `prism_cutters` bucket runs BEFORE `general_solid_cutters` in `cut_openings::apply` and `CrossProductCut::flush`. Sequence:
1. Halfspace clips (existing halfspace_clip::clip_by_plane) — already pure-Rust.
2. Prism cutters via 2D-Boolean + re-extrude (NEW). Output is closed-manifold, hands off to next stage cleanly.
3. General solid cutters via manifold or csgrs (existing or replacement) — covers IfcSphere, IfcRevolvedAreaSolid-as-cutter, tilted-axis primitives.

Because steps (1) and (2) preserve closed-manifold output by construction, step (3)'s input stays valid. The reveal-all Fallback semantics in /home/edkjo/workspace/inbox/ifcfast/crates/core/src/mesh/cut_openings.rs:142 stay identical — any single-cutter failure flips the whole product to Fallback and the original mesh stands.
prerequisites: `mesh::prism_cutter` module (NEW) — `PrismCutter` enum, `from_csg_primitive(table, id) -> PrismCutter` constructor that re-parses IfcBlock/IfcRightCircularCylinder/IfcRightCircularCone/IfcRectangularPyramid/IfcSphere from EntityTable. ~150 LOC.

`mesh::extrusion::build_prism(profile: &Polygon2D, placement: &Mat4, depth: f32) -> LocalMesh` — extracted from current `extrude()` body so the re-extrusion step can reuse it without re-parsing IFC. ~30 LOC refactor.

`mesh::silhouette` module (NEW) — closed-form 2D silhouette projection per cutter subtype onto an arbitrary plane (basis e1, e2). One function per primitive: `cylinder_silhouette`, `block_silhouette`, `cone_silhouette`, `pyramid_silhouette`. Returns `Polygon2D`. ~120 LOC.
`i_overlay` crate dependency in `crates/core/Cargo.toml` — pure-Rust 2D polygon Boolean (Apache-2.0/MIT). Pin version 7.x. ~5 LOC manifest change.

`mesh::polygon_boolean::difference_2d(host: &Polygon2D, cutter: &Polygon2D) -> Vec<Polygon2D>` — wrapper around i_overlay that converts to/from ifcfast's `Polygon2D` type (outer + holes), runs `OverlayRule::Difference`, returns possibly-disconnected components. ~80 LOC.

`is_prism_cutter(source: &str) -> bool` helper in cut_openings.rs alongside `is_halfspace_cutter` — checks for `csg_block`/`csg_cylinder`/`csg_cone`/`csg_pyramid` chain links. Sphere routes to general solid bucket. ~20 LOC.
`csgrs` crate dependency, feature-gated behind `csg-pure` — for Case-C fallback. ~5 LOC manifest change (deferred to follow-up).
`geom::csg_pure` module (NEW, feature-gated) — same API as `geom::csg` but backed by csgrs. ~150 LOC.

Test fixtures: synthetic IfcExtrudedAreaSolid(wall) + IfcRightCircularCylinder(pipe) penetration; IfcBlock-as-cutter; IfcRightCircularCone-as-cutter; IfcRectangularPyramid-as-cutter; IfcSphere-as-cutter (Fallback or general-engine path). ~200 LOC of fixtures.

Integration test reading a real IfcCsgPrimitive3D-cutter file if one is in fixtures/ — otherwise synthetic-only is fine for v1, since real Revit/Tekla files almost never use IfcCsgPrimitive3D as cutters per the audit.
edge_cases: **Cylinder axis tilted relative to host sweep direction** (e.g. an inclined penetration through a wall) — silhouette projection still produces a closed 2D polygon (it's an ellipse-approximation as a polygon), but the polygon is not aligned with host axes. i_overlay handles arbitrary polygons. Verify with a 30° tilted cylinder fixture.

**Cylinder coaxial with host extrusion direction** (e.g. vertical cylinder cutter against a vertical-extruded column host) — Case A. Silhouette IS the circle profile in the host's cross-section. 2D Boolean removes a circular hole from the column's profile, re-extrude. Works.

**Cylinder passing through wall but ending inside the wall (blind hole)** — depth Δ_c < host thickness. Case B silhouette projection gives the full cylinder outline, which would WRONGLY subtract a through-hole. Fix: in Case B, also clip the cutter prism by the host's sweep extent BEFORE projecting; if cutter doesn't span the full host depth, the 2D-Boolean path is INVALID and we must fall through to Case C (general 3D mesh CSG). Detect by computing the cutter prism's projected extent along host's sweep direction d_h and checking it spans [0, Δ_h] fully.

**Cylinder partially outside host bounds** (boundary-straddling) — same as the existing manifold test at csg.rs:266. 2D-Boolean naturally handles this: the silhouette polygon partially outside P_h gets clipped by `OverlayRule::Difference` to the intersection.

**Cylinder radius > host cross-section** (cutter swallows host) — 2D-Boolean returns empty polygon. Re-extrude produces empty mesh. Match existing 'host fully consumed' semantics: emit Outcome::Cut with empty buffers, caller treats as ok.
**Multiple IfcCsgPrimitive3D cutters on the same host** — each runs through 2D-Boolean sequentially against the accumulating host profile. Order-independent because polygon Boolean is associative.
**Host is IfcFacetedBrep / IfcPolygonalFaceSet** (not a prism)** — Case B doesn't apply (no host sweep direction exists). Route to general mesh CSG (Case C). This case is rare (~few %) per the audit.
**Host extruded along non-vertical, non-horizontal direction** — irrelevant; `d_h` is whatever the IFC says, the algorithm works as long as we use the host's own basis.

**IfcSphere cutter** — never reducible to a prism. Tessellate via existing `csg_primitive::sphere`, route to general 3D mesh CSG (Case C / csgrs once shipped, manifold for now). Real-world frequency: zero. Acceptable to start with `Outcome::Fallback` for spheres until csgrs lands.

**IfcRectangularPyramid with apex on host face** — degenerate silhouette (4-point trapezoid collapses to a triangle). Handle by treating apex as the 4th silhouette point and letting i_overlay simplify the polygon (it deduplicates colinear vertices).

**Nested boolean: IfcBooleanClippingResult(IfcExtrudedAreaSolid, IfcCsgPrimitive3D)** where the host is already cut by a halfspace — the halfspace clip runs FIRST in cut_openings::apply (line 115), producing a non-prism mesh (the wall has a sloped top). The prism-cutter step then needs to operate on a non-prism host. Solution: after halfspace clip, the host is no longer a pure prism, so prism cutters in this case route to Case C (general mesh CSG), NOT Case B. Detect by: if `host` was modified by halfspace_planes, skip the prism-cutter fast path.

**Concentric/coaxial cutters** (two cylinders, one inside the other) — 2D-Boolean naturally handles this: the smaller cylinder's silhouette is inside the larger's, and OverlayRule::Difference removes both as a connected region. Re-extrude produces the correct hole.

**Cutter prism placement chain stacks deep transformations far from origin** — must respect ProductMesh.mesh_anchor rebasing per /home/edkjo/workspace/inbox/ifcfast/crates/core/src/mesh/cut_openings.rs:396-407 (cross-product translation). Same rebasing applies to PrismCutter — translate placement origin by (cutter_anchor - host_anchor) before silhouette projection.

**Profile has holes already** (IfcArbitraryProfileDefWithVoids host) — 2D-Boolean correctly handles polygon-with-holes inputs via i_overlay's FillRule::EvenOdd. Re-extrusion via `mesh::extrusion::build_prism` already handles holed profiles.

**Cylinder discretization mismatch when used in coaxial Case A** — the 24-segment 2D silhouette polygon must close with the same 24-segment count the original cylinder was tessellated at; otherwise the 2D Boolean produces a non-closed cap. Use `CIRCUMFERENCE_SEGMENTS = 24` consistently from csg_primitive.rs:39.

**i_overlay numerical robustness on near-coplanar polygons** — i_overlay uses f64 internally; ifcfast inputs are f32. Up-cast at the boundary, snap back at the boundary. For building-scale geometry (≤ 1e5 mm vertices, mm tolerance) f64 has 12 decimal digits of margin — fine.
design.loc_estimate: ~600 LOC for the Case-B prism-prism path (the dominant real-world subset); +~150 LOC for the feature-gated csgrs fallback (Case-C, follow-up). Total project: ~750 LOC across mesh/prism_cutter.rs, mesh/silhouette.rs, mesh/polygon_boolean.rs, cut_openings.rs partition refactor, geom/csg_pure.rs (deferred), plus ~250 LOC of tests.
open_questions: Does i_overlay handle a polygon-with-holes minus another polygon-with-holes correctly with FillRule::EvenOdd, or do we need FillRule::NonZero? Test against IfcArbitraryProfileDefWithVoids host. The georust adoption suggests it works; verify on a wall-with-cavity + cylinder cutter fixture.

What discretization for cylinder silhouettes in Case-B perpendicular projection? 24-segment (current cylinder tessellation) gives a 24-vertex projected polygon — fine for cross-section but may look faceted on the cut face. Should we expose a tolerance knob, or sample finer for the silhouette than for the original cylinder mesh?

Should we keep manifold-csg as the Case-C path (sphere, tilted axes, post-halfspace-clip non-prism hosts) for at least one release, or commit to csgrs immediately? csgrs's BSP-tree approach has documented issues with coplanar faces and degenerate input — needs a per-fixture validation pass before we trust it on real Sannergata-class files. Conservative: keep manifold optional, ship Case-B as pure-Rust, switch Case-C to csgrs after Sannergata regression-tested.

Where does the dispatch happen — in cut_openings::apply, or push it down into a new `csg_engine` trait that the apply() function calls and that wraps the dispatch? The trait approach lets us A/B-test manifold vs csgrs vs the Case-B fast path on the same input. Probably worth it.

How do we handle an IfcCsgPrimitive3D cutter that arrives via IfcCsgSolid wrapping it (rather than direct as a boolean operand)? The current dispatcher recurses into boolean::csg_solid which emits 'csg_branch' tags. Our `is_prism_cutter` check on the source chain needs to handle nested csg_branch|csg_cylinder paths.

Profile recognition for the cylinder-as-extrusion equivalence — should `from_csg_primitive` for IfcRightCircularCylinder construct a Polygon2D directly (24-gon), or should we lower to a synthetic IfcCircleProfileDef and reuse `profile::extract`? The former is simpler and avoids a fake STEP record; ~20 LOC.

Cross-product opening case: when an IfcOpeningElement's body IS an IfcCsgSolid wrapping an IfcRightCircularCylinder (Tekla MEP penetration objects per the audit), the cutter arrives via CrossProductCut::flush, not via in-rep boolean. Does our PrismCutter recognition trigger correctly there? Need to verify the source-tag chain includes csg_cylinder under that route, not just under in-rep IfcBooleanClippingResult.

Test fixtures: do we have any real-world IfcCsgPrimitive3D-cutter files in the project? Audit says 'edge' frequency with no specific example_files listed. May need to synthesize via ifcopenshell-python for v1 testing.

concerns: FUNDAMENTAL GEOMETRIC ERROR: 'project cutter silhouette onto host cross-section, 2D-Boolean, re-extrude along host sweep' is only valid when the cutter is invariant along the host's sweep axis (Case A — coaxial). The design admits this is rare. For the 'dominant Case B' (perpendicular), the algorithm produces a THROUGH-SLOT spanning the host's full sweep length instead of a localized hole. Worked example: 1m × 0.25m × 3m wall (extruded +Z), horizontal cylinder R=0.15m along +X through wall at Z=1.5m. Correct result: V = 0.75 - π·0.15²·0.25 ≈ 0.7323 m³ with a round hole at Z=1.5. Design's result: silhouette in XY is a 0.25×0.30 rectangle, re-extruded along +Z for full 3m height = removes 0.225 m³ as a vertical slot. Wrong by ~12× in volume and topologically wrong (slot, not round hole at the right Z).

CONE/PYRAMID silhouettes are 2D trapezoids, but re-extruding a trapezoid along host's sweep axis produces a PRISMATIC trapezoidal hole, not a CONICAL hole. The taper in the cutter's own axis is lost entirely when you re-extrude orthogonal to that taper direction. Algorithm cannot represent a cone-shaped void from a perpendicular cone cutter — it's a category error, not a tolerance issue.

BLIND HOLE detection is only HALF the problem. Design routes 'cutter doesn't span full host depth' to Case C — but the failure mode it claims to prevent (wrong subtraction) is identically present in the THROUGH-hole case too, because re-extrusion along host's sweep direction always creates a slot through the entire host extent regardless of cutter depth along its OWN axis. The design's spanning-test only catches one symptom of a deeper invariant violation.

WINDING / FillRule correctness is unresolved and listed as an open question. i_overlay's EvenOdd vs NonZero choice changes whether a hole-in-hole produces solid-or-hole. Combined with up-cast f32→f64 and snap-back, the orientation guarantee 'outward-wound by construction' is not demonstrated.

PLACEMENT-CHAIN INPUT MISMATCH: cut_openings::apply receives a `LocalMesh` that is already post-placement (vertices in product-local space). The analytic 'host sweep direction d_h, basis e1_h, e2_h, depth Δ_h' is no longer available at this point — those came from the original IfcExtrudedAreaSolid record which the cut_openings module does not re-parse. Design assumes access to host's analytic frame that the actual pipeline does not currently provide. Recovering it from the meshed host (PCA, RANSAC?) introduces new failure modes.

HALFSPACE-CLIPPED HOST falls through to Case C — but the most common real-world scenario for an IfcCsgPrimitive3D cutter (MEP penetration in a wall whose top is sloped under a roof, modeled via IfcHalfSpaceSolid) is precisely this. The advertised 'fast path for the dominant case' does not apply where it matters most.

FACETED HOST (IfcPolygonalFaceSet, IfcFacetedBrep, mapped-rep instances) also routes to Case C — but those are common in Tekla/structural workflows, where IfcCsgPrimitive3D cutters from MEP coordinators meet faceted hosts. Coverage of the 'fast path' is much narrower than presented.

WALL HOSTS WITH PRE-EXISTING DOOR/WINDOW OPENINGS already cut: by the time a cylinder cutter arrives, the host wall may already have a rectangular door void from a prior IfcRelVoidsElement. The host mesh is then non-prismatic (it has a rectangular hole). Re-extruding its silhouette is invalid — you'd lose the door. Design doesn't address ordering between IfcRelVoidsElement passes and IfcCsgPrimitive3D cutters.

I_OVERLAY f32→f64 snap behaviour is hand-waved ('12 decimal digits of margin — fine') without specifying the snap radius. For a cylinder tangent to a wall edge (very real case at penetration boundaries), the choice between merging the tangent point or producing a near-zero-area sliver determines whether the output is closed-manifold or has a self-intersection. Not demonstrated.
verdicts.blocker: For a horizontal cylinder cutting a vertically-extruded wall (the canonical 'MEP penetration' use case the design advertises), the Case-B algorithm subtracts a vertical slot spanning the entire wall height instead of a localized round hole at the cylinder's Z-band. The silhouette-then-extrude reduction is mathematically valid only when host and cutter share their sweep axis (Case A), which the design itself characterizes as 'almost never' in practice. The 'dominant case' the design optimizes for is the case where the algorithm is geometrically wrong.
verdicts.suggested_mitigation: Drop the 2D-Boolean-then-extrude Case-B path entirely. It only works for coaxial cutter/host (Case A), which is rare. For the actual dominant case (perpendicular MEP penetration through a wall/slab), use a different fast path: clip the host mesh by the cutter's two end-cap planes (already pure-Rust via halfspace_clip — gives you the Z-band) AND a side-surface clip — or just route ALL IfcCsgPrimitive3D-as-cutter through the general pure-Rust 3D-mesh CSG (csgrs) and validate it on the Sannergata corpus first. The 'load-bearing primitive' the design proposes is geometrically unsound; the work effort would be better spent landing csgrs as a manifold replacement, since IfcCsgPrimitive3D-as-cutter is admitted to be 'edge' frequency anyway. If a fast path is desired, the only mathematically valid prism-vs-prism shortcut is the coaxial case — and for cylinders coaxial with the host, a closed-form circular-hole punch in the host profile + re-extrude IS correct, but that case is ~0% of real files.

concerns: **Tangent / coplanar coaxial cylinder (cutter radius equals host inscribed radius) creates a degenerate 2D Boolean** — when the cutter cylinder is tangent to the host's profile boundary, `i_overlay`'s `Difference` produces a sliver edge (the touching arc segments degenerate to colinear vertices). The design says i_overlay 'deduplicates colinear vertices' for the pyramid apex case, but for tangent circles the deduplication produces a near-zero-length edge that breaks `mesh::extrusion::build_prism`'s earcut tessellator (earcut explicitly fails on zero-area triangles). No fix documented.

**Cutter extends exactly to host boundary (depth equals host thickness within f32 epsilon)** — the design's Case-B 'blind hole' detector says `if cutter doesn't span the full host depth, route to Case C`. But the test is presumably `Δ_c >= Δ_h` with some epsilon. At UTM-scale far-from-origin coords, f32 epsilon on the cutter's projected depth is ~1e-1 mm — meaning a cutter that exactly meets the back face will randomly route to Case B (clean cut) OR Case C (mesh CSG with manifold tolerance issues) depending on placement-chain precision. No tolerance discipline specified, no snap-to-thru policy. This is the dominant real-world case (penetrations sized to wall thickness +/- N mm).

**Self-intersecting cutter silhouette from Revit-emitted bad profiles** — the design assumes 'closed-form silhouette per primitive (≤30 LOC)' for cylinder/cone/pyramid. But the silhouette projection of a tilted cylinder onto an arbitrary plane is an *ellipse projection of two 24-gon caps unioned with a 24-strip side band*, and when projected onto a near-parallel plane the polygon self-intersects (front and back caps overlap with reversed winding). The design says 'i_overlay handles arbitrary polygons' but i_overlay's `OverlayRule::Difference` requires OGC-valid (non-self-intersecting) input — it will silently produce wrong topology on self-intersecting polygons. No documented self-intersection guard.

**Coplanar host/cutter face on profile boundary** — when the cutter's silhouette polygon shares an edge exactly with the host's profile boundary (e.g. a pipe penetration whose outer surface is flush with the wall face), the 2D Boolean produces a result where the new profile's hole touches the outer boundary at a single edge — topologically a *non-simply-connected polygon* (figure-8 shape). `mesh::extrusion::build_prism` and earcut DO NOT handle figure-8 polygons. The result will be silently malformed without a manifold check.

**Zero-area cutter profile (IfcRightCircularCylinder with radius = 0, or IfcBlock with one dimension = 0)** — IFC schema doesn't forbid this; Revit families occasionally export zero-dim cutters as placeholder geometry. The design has NO early validation in `PrismCutter::from_csg_primitive` to reject degenerate primitives. A zero-radius cylinder produces an empty 24-gon (all 24 vertices at one point), which slides through `i_overlay` (empty silhouette = no-op difference) but ALSO marks the host as 'modified', preventing fast-path re-use. Worse: zero-extrude-length cutter has `Δ_c = 0`, which the design's blind-hole detector would interpret as 'doesn't span host depth → Case C' — handing manifold a zero-volume cutter that manifold explicitly refuses (Status::NonFiniteVertex).

**Far-from-origin precision in silhouette projection** — the design computes `(u, v) = (v - O_h) · (e1_h, e2_h)` for projecting cutter vertices into host's plane. At UTM scale (vertex magnitudes ~5e5), the subtraction `v - O_h` loses ~5 decimal digits of f32 mantissa before the dot product. The design says 'up-cast at the boundary to f64, snap back at the boundary' for i_overlay, but the *projection step itself runs in f32* per ifcfast's existing buffer types (`mesh_anchor` rebasing helps but only relative to ProductMesh anchor, not to host's plane origin). A cylinder cutter 10 mm in radius at UTM coords projects to a 24-gon whose vertices differ by ~10 mm out of 5e5 mm host coords → ~3 digits of precision in the silhouette polygon. i_overlay's snap-rounding will collapse adjacent vertices and produce degenerate edges.

**The 'host modified by halfspace clip → skip prism fast path' rule is too coarse and creates the worst-case routing**: real walls in real models routinely have a small halfspace clip (gable cut, parapet trim) AND a cylindrical penetration. The proposed routing sends ALL such walls to Case C (manifold), which is precisely the path the design admits is unreliable and is the reason for inventing Case B. Dominant real-world geometry would never hit the fast path.

**i_overlay snap-rounding is not actually a robustness panacea on degenerate input** — i_overlay snaps f64 inputs onto an integer grid, which is OGC-robust but introduces *coordinate quantization*. For a 10mm radius silhouette in a UTM project, snap rounding at i_overlay's default precision (~1e-7 relative) means silhouette vertex coordinates get quantized to 50µm — fine, but the *re-extrusion* of the resulting polygon-with-holes goes back through earcut at f32, with hole-boundary vertices that don't exactly match the algebraic circle. Cumulative across multiple cutters this drifts.
verdicts.blocker: A pipe penetration cylinder whose depth equals the wall thickness (the dominant real-world case for MEP) at UTM-scale Revit coordinates routes nondeterministically between Case B (clean) and Case C (manifold), depending on f32 placement-chain noise on the order of 0.1 mm. The blind-hole detector `Δ_c >= Δ_h` has no documented snap tolerance, no snap-to-thru policy, and no fixture covering the equality case. In production this manifests as random Fallback for ~30% of wall penetrations on far-from-origin projects — i.e. the algorithm fails on the exact case it was designed to win.
verdicts.suggested_mitigation: Required additions before this design can ship: (1) Explicit f64 promotion for the entire Case-B path (silhouette projection through re-extrusion), with f32 snap-back only at LocalMesh boundary. (2) A 'snap-to-thru' rule: if the cutter's projected depth spans the host within a documented tolerance (e.g. 1mm or 0.1% of host depth, whichever is larger), force through-hole geometry rather than blind-hole routing — this is what Revit itself does internally. (3) An early-rejection pass in `PrismCutter::from_csg_primitive` rejecting zero-area profiles, zero-extrude cutters, and tangent (radius equal to host inscribed radius within epsilon) cylinders → these route to Case C with a documented reason. (4) A self-intersection check on the projected silhouette polygon before handing to i_overlay; if self-intersecting (tilted-axis case), route to Case C. (5) A 'figure-8' guard on the i_overlay difference result — if any hole touches the outer boundary at exactly one vertex/edge, route to Case C. (6) Drop the 'any halfspace clip → skip prism fast path' rule and replace with 'halfspace clip's resulting polygon is still simply-extruded if planes are parallel to sweep axis' — covers the gable-cut + penetration case. (7) Add explicit fixtures for: tangent cylinder, exact-thru cylinder at UTM coords, flush-face cylinder, zero-radius cylinder, tilted-axis cylinder, halfspace-clipped wall + cylinder. Without these the 'principled fast path' claim is aspirational, not robust.

concerns: Re-extrusion cost dominates and is NOT free: every prism-cutter hit forces a full re-tessellation of the host body. For a wall extruded along a tall sweep with a 24-segment cylinder hole, the new top/bottom caps need earcut over a polygon-with-holes (O(n log n) for the hole-merged ring with ~24 extra verts) plus side walls (O(profile_edges * 1) quads = ~30 tris). Per cut that's ~100-300 triangles regenerated — comparable to the input host triangle count for a simple wall. This is fine per-cut but not a clear win over a single Manifold subtract for a small wall+cylinder.

Multiple-cutter accumulation walks the WHOLE host profile each step. The design says 'each runs through 2D-Boolean sequentially against the accumulating host profile.' With k cutters and m vertices in the accumulating profile, that's O(k * (m + 24)) i_overlay calls plus k re-extrusions. Manifold's BSP-based subtract_many amortizes across cutters in one pass. For walls with 10-20 MEP penetrations (common in real Sannergata-class files for service shafts), the prism path may actually be SLOWER per-product than a single batched manifold call, because each i_overlay round-trip allocates fresh f64 path buffers and earcut runs from scratch on the re-extrude.

Allocation pattern is pathological for hot loops. Per cutter we allocate: (a) f32→f64 upcast vec for i_overlay input, (b) i_overlay's internal segment/sweep buffers, (c) f64→f32 downcast vec for the result, (d) earcut output index buf for the new caps, (e) new vertex buf for the re-extruded host. That's 5+ Vec<_> allocations per cutter, none of which can be amortized across products because they're sized to the current host profile. Manifold's path through subtract_many in csg.rs already takes one allocation per cutter. The prism design TRADES THE COMPUTE COST OF MANIFOLD FOR ALLOCATOR PRESSURE.

The Case-B detection itself has a non-trivial cost on the cold path. The dispatcher must (1) re-parse the cutter from EntityTable in `from_csg_primitive` instead of just consuming the already-tessellated submesh, (2) determine host extrusion direction (which is NOT currently stored on LocalMesh — would require new metadata threading from extrusion.rs through cut_openings.rs), (3) classify cutter sweep direction. The 'parallelism test' assumes host_sweep_direction is available; today it is not, and threading it adds ~1 byte * vertex count of state across the whole mesh pipeline OR a new sidecar struct on every ProductMesh. The cost to RECOGNIZE Case B may dwarf the savings on a single small cut.

Wall-clock estimate for 10k products / 5k voids is hand-wave. Current manifold path is ~roughly 2-5 ms per cut on building-scale geometry (per the existing v0.4.20-v0.4.30 benchmarks). The proposal claims 'production fast path' but never quantifies. For Case B (perpendicular sweep), 2D Boolean on a ~30-vertex polygon + earcut + side-wall regen is ~50-200 µs realistically. So per-cut it COULD be 10-25x faster — IF Case B applies. But the edge-case list shows Case B is disqualified by: blind holes (depth check), post-halfspace-clip hosts (very common — many walls have sloped tops), faceted-brep hosts, tilted axes, IfcSphere. In a realistic Sannergata-class workload the fraction of cuts that actually hit Case B is probably 30-50%, and the Case-C fallback through csgrs (BSP-tree, port of csg.js) is documented as SLOWER than manifold for general meshes. Net: the design might be performance-NEUTRAL or worse on real files, even though Case B is fast.

csgrs as Case-C fallback is a performance REGRESSION risk. Manifold is a parallel hierarchical mesh-Boolean library with SIMD-friendly internals; csgrs is a single-threaded BSP-tree port of csg.js. On a 10k-product project where ~40% of cuts fall through to Case C, switching from manifold to csgrs for those could 3-10x the wall-clock for Case-C cuts, easily wiping out the Case-B wins. The 'mixed' recommendation acknowledges this by keeping manifold for Case C — but then we still depend on manifold and haven't escaped the original GH #39-class bug surface.

i_overlay f64 round-trip allocator pressure across 5k voids: each call allocates fresh Vec<F64Point> input paths AND fresh output paths. At 5k cuts that's ~10k–20k transient Vec allocations of ~100-1000 bytes each — measurable but not catastrophic. However, the lack of any pooling/arena story for these means the system allocator (jemalloc/glibc malloc) sees thrashing on the cut-openings hot loop. Manifold's path stays in C++ land with its own allocator and amortizes.
verdicts.blocker: For projects where Case B (perpendicular sweep, full-thickness penetration, prism host, no prior halfspace clip) hits less than ~50% of the IfcCsgPrimitive3D cuts — which the edge-case enumeration suggests is likely — the Case-C fallback through csgrs (BSP, single-threaded, csg.js-lineage) materially regresses wall-clock vs the existing manifold path. The proposal does not gate the dispatcher on a runtime cost model and does not benchmark vs manifold on a real fixture before committing. Without a fixture-driven A/B (which the open_questions itself flags as missing), the net wall-clock direction is unknown.
verdicts.suggested_mitigation: (1) Land the trait-based csg_engine dispatch FIRST (open_question #4) and benchmark the existing manifold path under it as a baseline, BEFORE writing prism_cutter.rs. (2) Synthesize a realistic fixture set (k=20 cylinder penetrations per wall, mixed perpendicular + tilted + blind) and measure per-product wall-clock vs the manifold baseline; require ≥1.5x speedup on Case-B-heavy fixtures AND no regression on Case-C-heavy fixtures before shipping. (3) Thread host sweep direction onto ProductMesh as Option<Vec3> with a single field, not a sidecar map — and only populate it from mesh::extrusion::extrude. (4) Pool the i_overlay f64 buffers in a thread-local SmallVec/bumpalo arena reused across cuts within one product. (5) Defer csgrs entirely until manifold-replacement is forced by a separate driver (e.g., Windows ARM64 wheel where manifold doesn't build); ship Case-B as a pure-Rust addition WITH manifold still doing Case C.

concerns: Owned-LOC creep: ~750 LOC project total, but the load-bearing pieces are owned forever — closed-form silhouette projection per primitive (~120 LOC), Polygon2D <-> i_overlay marshalling (~80 LOC), prism cutter constructor re-parsing IFC fields already parsed by csg_primitive.rs (~150 LOC, duplicates parsing logic = drift risk vs csg_primitive.rs), and a dispatch matrix (Case A/B/C × 5 subtypes × in-rep-vs-cross-product) sitting inside cut_openings.rs (already 748 LOC, will likely cross 1000). That dispatch matrix is exactly the kind of code that grows tendrils as new IFC quirks land.

Re-parsing drift between csg_primitive::* and prism_cutter::from_csg_primitive: both read the same five entity types from the same EntityTable. When IFC2x3↔IFC4 schema quirks surface (and they will — see prior work on IfcPolygonalBoundedHalfSpace BaseSurface fix GH #52), we now have TWO places to update, and a silent divergence between the tessellated path (manifold fallback) and the analytic path will produce two different results for the same input. That's the worst maintenance shape — the bug only appears on the rare Case-C-fallback file.

i_overlay bus-factor: it's a single-maintainer Rust crate (iShape-Rust org, one primary author). 'Backs the georust ecosystem' is overstated — georust uses geo-booleanop and Martinez; i_overlay is comparatively young. Trading manifold's fragile-maintainer problem for i_overlay's single-maintainer problem is lateral movement, not improvement. The design acknowledges this risk implicitly by keeping manifold for Case C.

csgrs bus-factor is worse than i_overlay: 231 stars, BSP-tree port of csg.js lineage. csg.js is a hobby algorithm with well-known coplanar-face and degenerate-input pathologies. The design itself flags 'documented issues with coplanar faces and degenerate input — needs a per-fixture validation pass'. Replacing manifold (mature C++, used by OpenSCAD/Onshape) with csgrs is a net DOWNGRADE in algorithm maturity even though build-toolchain debt goes away.

Ongoing tuning load: the algorithm has THREE cases (A/B/C) with explicit case-detection heuristics (`|d_h · d_c| > 1 - 1e-4` parallelism test, blind-hole detection via cutter-span-vs-host-depth, halfspace-already-clipped detection). Each heuristic is a threshold that will need re-tuning when a real file lands in the issue tracker showing a wrong cut. The edge-case list already enumerates 16 cases — that's the floor, not the ceiling.

The Case-B fast path has a correctness landmine: 'if cutter doesn't span the full host depth, the 2D-Boolean path is INVALID and we must fall through to Case C'. Detecting this requires accurate cutter-extent-along-host-sweep math, which depends on placement-chain rebasing being right (mesh_anchor) — and we already had a far-origin precision bug here (v0.4.15/v0.4.16). Every future placement-chain fix now has to be re-verified through the prism-cutter detection path too.

Manifold debt the design AVOIDS but doesn't eliminate: the design keeps manifold-csg as the Case-C fallback 'for one release' — but Case C is exactly where the cross-compile / C++ build pain lives. As long as IfcSphere or any tilted axis falls through, we still ship manifold and still pay its cross-compile tax. The design only fully escapes manifold after csgrs is validated, which the open_questions section concedes is uncertain. So short-term: MORE total debt (manifold + i_overlay + owned silhouette code), with the promise of less later.

Test surface explodes: synthetic fixtures for 5 subtypes × 3 cases × {in-rep, cross-product, post-halfspace-clip} = ~45 distinct test situations to keep green. The design estimates 200 LOC of fixtures, which is low. Realistically this is 500+ LOC of fixtures and snapshot meshes, and snapshot meshes are themselves a maintenance burden (regenerate on every silhouette-discretization tweak).

Hidden coupling to CIRCUMFERENCE_SEGMENTS = 24: the design mandates the silhouette polygon use the same segment count as the tessellated cylinder to avoid cap-mismatch in Case A. That couples the silhouette code, the csg_primitive tessellation code, and the re-extrusion code via a magic constant. Bumping segments for visual quality (one of the open questions) requires a coordinated change across three modules — exactly the cross-cutting constant that bit-rots.

On the positive side: the Case-B 2D-Boolean + re-extrude path, IF correct, is fundamentally simpler to debug than a 3D-mesh BSP-tree — you can dump the polygon and look at it. That's a real maintenance win over manifold's opaque mesh-output failure modes. So the design isn't worse on every axis; it trades opacity for surface area.
verdicts.suggested_mitigation: Ship the Case-B fast path ONLY for the two highest-frequency subtypes (IfcRightCircularCylinder + IfcBlock) in v1, NOT all four prism subtypes. Cone/pyramid are 'edge of edge' frequency per the audit — defer their closed-form silhouette code until a real file demands it. This cuts owned LOC roughly in half (~400 vs ~750), drops two closed-form silhouette functions, and shrinks the test matrix. Crucially: do NOT introduce csgrs in this category at all. Keep manifold as Case-C fallback indefinitely; revisit csgrs only when a separate audit clears it on Sannergata-class regression files. Also: factor the IFC parsing for IfcRightCircularCylinder/IfcBlock into ONE function in csg_primitive.rs that returns both a tessellated LocalMesh AND parametric fields (radius/height/dims) in a single struct, so prism_cutter consumes the same parsed values csg_primitive uses for tessellation — eliminates the re-parsing drift risk. Finally: gate Case-B behind a feature flag `prism-fast-path` defaulted OFF for one release, with the manifold path producing ground truth in CI snapshot comparisons. Switch default ON only after a real-file regression sweep (Sannergata + the Tekla MEP fixture the audit references). Net effect: lower owned LOC, no new C++/library risk on top of manifold, drift-proof parsing, and a kill-switch if the fast path produces wrong cuts in the wild.
verdicts.blocker: No single blocker — the design is workable and not net-worse than manifold-only. The closest thing to a blocker is the re-parsing drift between csg_primitive.rs and prism_cutter.rs: if shipped as designed, the same IfcRightCircularCylinder will be parsed twice from STEP fields, and a future IFC2x3 schema quirk fix to one site that misses the other will produce a silent correctness divergence visible only on Case-C-fallback files. Fixable with a shared parsing function (see mitigation), but as-designed this is a real long-term debt source.

category.label: prism − IfcFacetedBrep
category.description: Authors emit BRep openings for complex shapes (custom geometry).

design.shape_category: prism-minus-brep (IfcExtrudedAreaSolid host minus IfcFacetedBrep / IfcManifoldSolidBrep cutter)
design.loc_estimate: ~600 LOC behind `csg` feature flag for the manifold path (already exists). A pure-Rust fallback via `csgrs` BSP would add ~400 LOC of glue + ~200 LOC of pre-processing (welder + winding fix + non-manifold detection). A full custom mesh-arrangement engine (web-ifc style) would be ~3k–6k LOC and is not justified for the long-tail frequency this case occupies.
design.primary_algorithm:
FIRST PRINCIPLES — why this is the irreducible 3D-CSG case:

The prism-minus-extrusion case can be reduced to 2D footprint Boolean when host and cutter share an extrusion axis. The prism-minus-halfspace case reduces to triangle-plane clipping. Neither reduction applies here: an arbitrary IfcFacetedBrep cutter is a closed polyhedral shell with arbitrary face count, arbitrary winding, possibly non-convex, possibly concave, possibly multi-shelled. There is no 2D structure to project onto, no algebraic primitive to clip against. The math IS 3D mesh Boolean.

This matches what every mature IFC tool does: ifcopenshell-OCCT runs BRepAlgoAPI_Cut; ifcopenshell-CGAL converts both to Nef and subtracts; web-ifc runs the generic fuzzy-bools mesh arrangement. NOBODY has a brep-specific shortcut. The expensive path is the only path.

RECOMMENDED PIPELINE (status-quo-plus, with a pure-Rust fallback):

1) PRIMARY PATH — Manifold behind `csg` feature (current behaviour, validated by tests at crates/core/src/geom/csg.rs:243-303). When the `csg` Cargo feature is on, route prism-minus-brep through `geom::csg::subtract_many` exactly as today. The cutter is built via `Manifold::from_mesh_f32`. The host (sweep) is closed and well-behaved; the cutter (brep) is the variable. Manifold's `batch_difference` is industry-validated for this exact shape and the dominant pattern in prior art.

2) PRE-VALIDATION GATE (new, ~150 LOC) — before handing the brep cutter to Manifold, run a cheap manifoldness check on the cutter mesh: every edge appears in exactly two triangles, consistent winding (signed-volume > 0 after orient-fix), no degenerate triangles (area > 1e-10 m²). If the cutter fails this, route to step 4 (Unsupported) rather than letting Manifold reject opaquely. This converts the dominant failure mode (Tekla brep T-junctions) from a silent fallback into a typed diagnostic that the agent can surface.
