3) PRE-WELD STEP (new, ~80 LOC) — Tekla brep exports often have coincident vertices at face borders (each face owns its own copy of shared cartesian points despite the deduplication in brep.rs:69 vertex_cache, because IfcCartesianPoint refs are sometimes inlined per-face in older exports). Run a spatial-hash vertex weld at ON_PLANE_EPS=1e-3 mm before handing to Manifold. This closes pinhole T-junctions that would otherwise fail manifold validation. Use a HashMap<(i32,i32,i32), u32> with snapping at the eps grid.

4) PURE-RUST FALLBACK PATH (new, ~400 LOC) — when `csg` feature is OFF, or when Manifold rejects despite pre-weld, attempt a CSG via `csgrs` (pure-Rust BSP-tree CSG, MIT, the most mature pure-Rust kernel today per the audit: v0.20.1, 231 stars, active). Algorithm:
   a. Convert host + cutter from flat-buffer (Vec<f32>, Vec<u32>) → `csgrs::CSG::from_polygons` (CSG.js-style polygon list with per-polygon plane).
   b. Call `host.subtract(&cutter)` → BSP-tree partition + face splitting + classification (front/back/coplanar) + inverse on cutter for difference.
   c. Convert result back via the `to_polygons` accessor → triangulate each polygon by fan (output polygons from BSP CSG are always convex by construction).
   d. Compact the vertex buffer (HashMap<quantised-pos, u32> dedup at 1e-3 mm grid).

5) UNSUPPORTED ERROR (typed) — when both manifold and csgrs fail (truly malformed brep — open shell, self-intersecting, multi-shell that can't be reconciled), surface `CsgKernelError::CutterNotManifold { reason }` rather than falling silently to Outcome::Fallback. The reveal-all stance is preserved (the mesh stays untouched) but the agent sees a typed signal it can route on. This is a strict improvement over today's opaque fallback.

INTEGRATION POINT: All of this lives inside `geom::csg::subtract_many`, transparent to `cut_openings::apply` and `CrossProductCut::flush`. The cutter buffer source ("brep" vs "extrusion") is already on each MeshSegment.source via the existing tag chain — we use that tag to decide whether to apply the pre-weld step (skip for extrusion sources, which are closed by construction). Cross-product path: the same path is used because `assemble_submesh` strips the source tags by the time `subtract_many` is called; we re-derive cutter-shape-class from the held opening's `ProductMesh.source` field, which carries the top-level tag.

WHY NOT BUILD OUR OWN MESH ARRANGEMENT: Building a robust 3D mesh Boolean from scratch (BSP, mesh-arrangement, plane-snap classification) is 3k–6k LOC of numerically sensitive code with known robustness pitfalls — the body of work that took fuzzy-bools, CGAL, and Manifold years to stabilise. The frequency of prism-minus-brep is ~few percent (audit), and Manifold already handles it correctly when the input is manifold. The marginal value of a custom kernel is "remove a C++ dependency on the < 5% of products that hit this path." That's not where engineering hours should go on a roadmap that lists `IfcSweptDiskSolid`, profile recognition, and viewer-feedstock as higher priorities (per MEMORY.md roadmap-geometry-hotswap).

KEY DIFFERENCE FROM prism-minus-prism: in that case we can collapse to a 2D Boolean on the swept profile (using `i_overlay` or similar) and re-extrude. Here, the cutter has no 2D representation. Even reducing to "host's footprint minus cutter's slice at host's extrusion axis" gives a wrong answer because the cutter's cross-section varies along the axis and removing a slice from the footprint loses the height dimension entirely.
prerequisites: Cutter manifoldness validator (~150 LOC) in `geom::mesh_validate.rs`: edge-pair count, winding consistency via signed-volume sign, degenerate-triangle filter (area < 1e-10 m²). Returns Ok or typed CutterDefect { open_edges, inverted_faces, degenerate_count }.
Spatial-hash vertex welder (~80 LOC) in `geom::weld.rs`: HashMap<[i32;3], u32> with snap_scale = 1e3 (mm-grid). Optionally re-indexes triangles. O(N) one-pass over flat buffer. Must preserve winding.

csgrs glue layer (~400 LOC) in `geom::csg_pure.rs` behind a new `csg-pure` feature flag (mutually-exclusive with the existing `csg` feature, OR additive with priority order: try manifold first if compiled, else fall back to csgrs). Includes: (a) flat-buffer → csgrs::Polygon list converter that derives per-polygon plane from triangle normal, (b) csgrs::CSG::subtract invocation with sane epsilon, (c) result.to_polygons() → fan-triangulation → flat-buffer converter, (d) vertex dedup pass on output.

Typed kernel-error variants (~30 LOC additions to `geom::csg::CsgKernelError`): `CutterNotManifold { open_edge_count, inverted_face_count }`, `HostNotManifold { … }`, `KernelUnavailable` (both features off). Surface these through `Outcome::Fallback` as an enriched stats counter so agents can distinguish reveal-all-with-cause from reveal-all-by-design.

Per-call-site decision: when to run the pre-weld step. Add a `CutterClass::Brep | Extrusion | CsgPrimitive | Halfspace` enum derived from the cutter's `MeshSegment.source` tag chain at the partition step in `cut_openings::apply` (~40 LOC) and pass through to `subtract_many` so brep cutters get welded but already-clean extrusion cutters skip the cost.

Integration tests against Tekla precast and ArchiCAD compound-opening sample files. We need fixtures from the audit's example_files set extended with brep-cutter cases (currently example_files is `[]` for this row — we have no test corpus yet). Could synthesize: a unit cube IfcFacetedBrep cutter against an extruded wall, validate against the in-test build_manifold ground truth that already exists at crates/core/src/geom/csg.rs:243.
edge_cases: Tekla brep with T-junctions: face A has vertices (0,0,0)-(1,0,0)-(1,1,0); face B has vertices (0.5,0,0)-(1,0,0)-(1,1,0)-(0,1,0). The shared edge (1,0,0)-(1,1,0) has a T-junction at (0.5,0,0) on B's interior. Pre-weld at 1e-3 mm WILL NOT FIX this (the T-junction vertex is not coincident with any A vertex). Outcome: manifold rejects; fallback to csgrs (BSP is tolerant of T-junctions because it splits along planes regardless of edge sharing). Document this as the dominant Tekla failure mode.

ArchiCAD multi-shell brep: an IfcFacetedBrep authored as two disjoint closed shells inside one IfcClosedShell. Our brep.rs reader fan-triangulates all faces into a single mesh without distinguishing shells. Manifold accepts multi-component manifold meshes; csgrs may not (BSP assumes single solid). Mitigation: post-process by connected-components on the mesh graph; if N>1 components found, route each through subtract independently and union the residuals. Defer to v2 — first version surfaces this as CutterDefect::MultiShell.
Cutter extends beyond host on all sides (oversized opening): well-handled by both manifold and csgrs; result equals the cutter ∩ host. No special action.

Cutter exactly coplanar with a host face (e.g. window opening flush with wall's interior face): degenerate for BSP coplanar classification. csgrs treats coplanar polygons via a sub-classifier (same-orientation kept, opposite-orientation dropped). Manifold uses tolerance-based snapping. Need to test both; consensus is to nudge the cutter inward by ON_PLANE_EPS = 1e-3 mm if a coplanar-face detector fires (~30 LOC extra in pre-processing).

Host is the brep (reversed roles — brep minus prism extrusion): same case, same algorithm. Pre-weld and validation apply to the host now. Manifold/csgrs path is symmetric (subtract(B, A) vs subtract(A, B) is just operand order).

Tilted/sloped wall + brep opening (host is an extrusion along a non-Z axis, cutter is brep): both manifold and csgrs are coordinate-frame-agnostic; the rebase-to-mesh_anchor step in CrossProductCut::flush already handles f32 precision. No new edge case.

Nested halfspace clip already applied to host before the brep cut (e.g. Sannergata wall under sloped roof + brep opening): host arrives at subtract_many already-clipped by halfspace_clip. The clip's cap polygon is earcut-triangulated, generally producing degenerate slivers at the cap edges (audit notes 1e-3 mm tolerance drops these). The post-halfspace_clip host MAY have near-zero-area triangles that fail manifold's manifoldness check. Mitigation: run the validate+weld pre-pass on the HOST too when a halfspace_clip preceded the call (signaled via a new flag on subtract_many).
Cutter is IfcManifoldSolidBrep (parent type) instead of IfcFacetedBrep: brep.rs already treats them identically (line 31-33 accept both type names). No new case.

Cutter brep author used CW winding throughout: signed-volume is negative; manifold rejects. Validator detects via sign(volume) and applies a global triangle-winding flip. Closes a real-world failure mode (some older ArchiCAD exports do this).
Cutter is an IfcOpenShell (open polyhedral surface, not closed): explicitly out of scope — surface-only cutters cannot subtract. Validator returns CutterDefect::OpenShell. Reveal-all preserved.
Empty result after subtract (host fully consumed by cutter): both kernels return empty (0 verts, 0 idx); the existing logic at cut_openings.rs:131-134 handles this branch by skipping. No new case.
Far-from-origin host vertices: existing rebase-to-mesh_anchor (cut_openings.rs:397-407) keeps both inputs in the host's local frame. f32 precision adequate at <10 m. No new mitigation needed.

Cutter authored as IfcAdvancedBrep (curved surfaces approximated as polyloops): the planar approximation in brep.rs:advanced_brep_approx can produce non-manifold output when curved faces meet at sharp seams. Validator catches it as CutterDefect; fallback to typed Unsupported. Document as known limitation; AdvancedBrep is rare (audit lists frequency = 'rare').
open_questions: Does csgrs's BSP CSG handle T-junctioned input correctly in practice, or only after a Welder pass? Need to read csgrs's `polygon.rs` and `bsp.rs` (specifically the splitting predicate's tolerance). If BSP is intolerant we need to ship the welder + a T-junction resolver, which is non-trivial (~200 LOC for the cap-snap-and-retriangulate logic).

How does csgrs compare to manifold on output quality (vertex count, triangulation quality) for the prism-minus-brep case? Manifold deduplicates and re-meshes; csgrs preserves face structure from the BSP partition. If csgrs output has 10× the triangle count of manifold's, downstream consumers (meshopt, gltf encoder) may pay a measurable cost. Worth benchmarking on a Tekla precast panel before committing to the path.

Should the pure-Rust fallback be `csgrs` or do we wait for `baby_shark`'s voxel boolean to mature? `baby_shark` is lossy (voxel grid resolution determines fidelity); 1 mm voxel on a 10 m wall = 10^10 cells = not viable. `csgrs` is the realistic choice today, but watch for an exact-arithmetic pure-Rust kernel (CGAL Nef analogue) emerging in the Rust ecosystem — none today.

Is there value in caching the cutter Manifold across multiple cuts (one brep opening that voids two walls)? Today subtract_many rebuilds the Manifold from buffers every call. Cross-product fold (CrossProductCut::flush) processes each host independently; cutters are rebuilt N times if N hosts share an opening. Rare in practice (one opening usually = one host) but worth measuring on big files.

Should we attempt a `iOverlay 2D-projection check` to reject impossible cuts early? If cutter's footprint on the host's extrusion axis doesn't intersect the host's profile footprint, the result is the host unchanged — no CSG needed. Saves the BSP/manifold call for cutters that don't actually intersect. ~100 LOC for the projection check; payoff depends on how often Tekla over-models openings that miss their hosts (anecdotally rare but possible).

What error-recovery story do we want for the cross-product flush when in-rep cut already succeeded but the cross-product brep cutter fails? Currently we keep the in-rep result (cut_openings.rs:444-449). This is reasonable but masks the brep failure from stats. Consider adding a `partial_fallback` counter to CutOpeningsStats.

Manifold's tolerance propagation issue (GH #39) was specific to halfspace cutters; does it recur with brep cutters of unusual size (e.g. an opening whose bbox dwarfs the host)? Worth a stress test before declaring manifold safe for the brep path.

verdicts.shape_category: prism-minus-brep (IfcExtrudedAreaSolid host minus IfcFacetedBrep / IfcManifoldSolidBrep cutter)
concerns: Welder unit-unsafety: design hard-codes ON_PLANE_EPS = 1e-3 mm and a quantisation grid of 1e3 with no reference to the model's unit_scale. Per substrate-unit-scale memory, ifcfast vertex buffers are in source units (often metres, sometimes mm, occasionally feet). The same grid will collapse legitimate 1 mm features when the source units are mm and will over-snap to coarse buckets when the source units are feet. Welder must accept a unit_scale and snap at `eps_metres * unit_scale`.

Global signed-volume winding flip is wrong for multi-shell breps. brep.rs fan-triangulates all shells into one mesh (acknowledged in edge case 2). With two opposing-wound shells, the total signed volume can be ±anything and a global triangle flip can invert the correctly-wound shell. Per-connected-component orientation is required and is deferred to v2 — but the validator-then-flip step runs in v1 and will silently corrupt multi-shell inputs.

Manifoldness validator's edge-pair-count check is necessary but insufficient. Two triangles sharing an edge with the SAME direction (A→B on both) gives a non-orientable surface that still satisfies the count check. Manifold will reject; design claims typed CutterDefect surfaces a useful reason, but this failure mode falls through to ManifoldRejected with no extra diagnostic. The 'typed signal' claim is overpromised.

Boundary-coplanar nudge by 1e-3 mm changes the IFC-authored opening geometry. Door cutters are deliberately authored flush with one wall face — this is the COMMON case, not an edge case. A 1 mm shift along an unspecified 'inward' direction corrupts downstream quantity takeoff (door reveal depth wrong by 1 mm × area), and the direction is normal-dependent (inward relative to which face?). Design must either preserve the flush invariant via coplanar-snap on the kernel side, or specify a normal-aware nudge — current text does neither.

csgrs fan-triangulation assumes convex output polygons. CSG.js-style BSP CSG only guarantees convex outputs when the inputs are convex; Tekla/ArchiCAD brep faces are routinely non-convex ngons (reflex polygons around door reveals, mullions, recesses). The fan from vertex 0 then produces overlapping or inverted triangles. Pipeline needs an ear-clip or seidel triangulation at ingress to csgrs AND on output — not mentioned.

Sliver-triangle handling on halfspace-pre-clipped hosts is destructive. Edge case 7 proposes welding + degenerate-drop on the host when a halfspace_clip preceded the call. The halfspace cap is earcut-triangulated and contains zero-area-prone slivers along long edges. Welding+dropping these triangles can open the cap, producing a non-manifold host that the subsequent subtract returns wrong topology for. A sliver-MERGE (cap restitching) is required, not a drop.

IfcMappedItem composite transform on the cutter is not addressed. Brep openings via IfcRepresentationMap carry a per-mapped-item transform on top of the placement chain. Design asserts 'rebase-to-mesh_anchor handles precision' but does not specify that the cutter buffer arriving at subtract_many is in the host's local frame INCLUDING the mapped-item transform. If the cutter is still in its representation-local frame, the subtract computes in mismatched coordinates and silently returns garbage.

Cross-product source-tag re-derivation flattens mixed cutter representations. An IfcOpeningElement with both a brep and an extrusion subrep (backup-geometry practice) has its CutterClass collapsed to the top-level tag. The weld/skip decision then either over-welds the extrusion (correctness drift) or skips welding on the brep portion (manifold reject). Per-segment classification at MeshSegment.source granularity is needed.
verdicts.blocker: Worked example: 1m × 0.25m × 3m wall, 0.9m × 0.3m × 2.1m door flush with the +Y face of the wall (interior). Authored cutter has Y extents exactly [0.25, 0.55] so the cutter's −Y face is coincident with the host's +Y face. The proposed coplanar-nudge step inflates the cutter inward by 1e-3 mm in some 'inward' direction. The cutter is shifted by 1 mm along an under-specified axis. After subtract, the resulting opening's depth is 0.249 m instead of 0.25 m and the reveal contains a 1 mm thick shell of host material that would have been removed in any correct implementation. Quantity takeoff against this mesh reports a wrong door area; the wall's net volume is wrong by 1 mm × 0.9 m × 2.1 m = 1.89 × 10⁻³ m³ per door, multiplying across a building. The correctness invariant 'authored coincident planes stay coincident' is violated by design.
verdicts.suggested_mitigation: 1) Make the welder unit-aware: accept unit_scale (or work in a normalised metres-frame), snap at `floor(coord * unit_scale * 1000.0)` with the 1 mm grid expressed in real-world units. 2) Replace the global-flip winding fix with a connected-component pass: find shells, compute per-shell signed volume, flip per shell. Add a non-orientability edge-direction check to the validator (not just edge-pair count). 3) Drop the coplanar-nudge entirely. Instead, instruct manifold via its tolerance parameter (or csgrs via its epsilon) to treat coincident planes as coincident, and accept that authored flush geometry stays flush. If a specific kernel rejects, surface CutterDefect::CoplanarFaces — do not silently shift IFC-authored geometry. 4) At csgrs ingress, triangulate every input polygon (ear-clip) so the BSP receives only triangles; on output, ear-clip the convex-by-construction polygons rather than fan-triangulate, OR enforce convexity assertion at output and fall through to Unsupported if it fails. 5) For halfspace-pre-clipped hosts, run a cap-aware sliver merge that snaps cap vertices to the half-plane and rebuilds the cap fan, rather than welding+dropping. 6) Add an explicit precondition in subtract_many's docstring AND a debug-assert that both buffers share a coordinate frame; document that the caller (CrossProductCut::flush) is responsible for applying IfcMappedItem transforms before the call. 7) Per-segment CutterClass: classify at MeshSegment.source granularity, not top-level ProductMesh.source. 8) Add Tekla-brep, ArchiCAD-multi-shell, and flush-window integration fixtures BEFORE shipping — the prerequisite list correctly identifies this as missing but defers it; correctness cannot be claimed without those.

verdicts.shape_category: prism-minus-brep (IfcExtrudedAreaSolid host minus IfcFacetedBrep / IfcManifoldSolidBrep cutter)
concerns: Self-intersecting Revit BReps are NOT in the edge_cases list at all. Revit IS called out in the lens prompt as 'sometimes emits this', and the design's manifoldness validator only checks edge-pair count, winding consistency, and degenerate-area triangles — it does NOT check for self-intersection (which requires an O(n^2) or BVH-driven triangle-triangle test). A self-intersecting cutter passes the proposed validator, gets handed to Manifold (which rejects it opaquely) OR to csgrs BSP (which classifies points against planes that lie on both sides of other faces — producing garbage output, not graceful failure). The design literally never says the word 'self-intersection' outside the inherited Manifold docstring. This is a documented Revit emission pattern and the design has no answer.

T-junction handling is admitted-broken by the design itself. The first edge_case explicitly says 'Pre-weld at 1e-3 mm WILL NOT FIX this' and then waves at 'BSP is tolerant of T-junctions because it splits along planes regardless of edge sharing'. The very next section (open_questions #1) THEN admits 'Does csgrs's BSP CSG handle T-junctioned input correctly in practice? Need to read csgrs's polygon.rs and bsp.rs (specifically the splitting predicate's tolerance). If BSP is intolerant we need to ship the welder + a T-junction resolver, which is non-trivial (~200 LOC for the cap-snap-and-retriangulate logic).' The design is staking the fallback path on unverified library behavior for the DOMINANT Tekla failure mode it has self-identified. That is not a robustness story — it is a hope.

Coplanar-face handling between host and cutter (window-flush-with-wall, the COMMON case for IFC openings) has the weakest mitigation in the whole document: 'nudge the cutter inward by ON_PLANE_EPS = 1e-3 mm if a coplanar-face detector fires (~30 LOC extra)'. This is geometrically wrong as a default — nudging a flush window cutter inward by 1mm produces a 1mm-thick rim of host material around the opening, visible in any glTF render and dimensionally incorrect for any QTO downstream. The 'coplanar-face detector' itself is not specified. Manifold's 'tolerance-based snapping' and csgrs's 'sub-classifier (same-orientation kept, opposite-orientation dropped)' are mentioned but never characterized for THIS pipeline. Coplanar with same-orientation between host inner face and cutter outer face is EXACTLY the flush-window case, and 'opposite-orientation dropped' is what we want there — but the design doesn't commit to either kernel and doesn't test the assumption.

Far-from-origin precision is dismissed as 'existing rebase-to-mesh_anchor … keeps both inputs in the host's local frame. f32 precision adequate at <10 m. No new mitigation needed.' But IfcFacetedBrep is the case where Revit dumps EXPLICIT cartesian-point coordinates, and on UTM-scale projects (the well-known precision pitfall this codebase already fixed in v0.4.15/v0.4.16 per MEMORY.md 'far-origin-precision'), the brep vertices arrive at the kernel WITH the global offset baked in if the rebase chain is incomplete. The design assumes mesh_anchor rebase is universal but doesn't trace whether brep.rs vertices flow through the same rebase path as extrusion vertices. If the cutter is rebased to host-local but host vertices weren't (or vice-versa), Manifold and csgrs will both produce garbage at sub-mm scales — and the kernel will REPORT SUCCESS, so reveal-all doesn't trigger. Silent corruption is the worst degeneracy outcome.

Zero-area / sliver triangle mitigation has a glaring conditional: the post-halfspace_clip case (which is the EXACT Sannergata scenario in MEMORY.md halfspace-cutter-bug, the project's most-recent CSG fix) is gated behind 'a new flag on subtract_many' — meaning the validator+weld pre-pass is NOT applied to the host by default. The design admits 'The post-halfspace_clip host MAY have near-zero-area triangles that fail manifold's manifoldness check' and then makes the mitigation opt-in. The default path will silently drop into reveal-all fallback on the wall+sloped-roof+opening case the project just shipped a fix for. This regresses 97.4% Sannergata match.

The pre-validation gate's degenerate-triangle threshold (area > 1e-10 m²) is too small to catch the slivers that actually break Manifold. Manifold's internal tolerance is propagated from input precision; in practice it rejects triangles whose area is small relative to the mesh's bounding-box diagonal, not below a fixed metric threshold. A 1m sliver triangle with 0.001mm thickness has area ~1e-9 m² (passes the gate) but is what causes Manifold's edge-pairing to fail on real Tekla output. The threshold is set for theoretical correctness, not for the empirical failure distribution.

Multi-shell ArchiCAD breps are explicitly punted to 'v2 — first version surfaces this as CutterDefect::MultiShell'. That means the COMMON ArchiCAD compound-opening case (which the design names in its prerequisites as a required test fixture) goes straight to reveal-all on first ship. Combined with the T-junction issue, the design's actual coverage on real-world brep cutters is closer to 'Tekla simple + Revit clean' than 'IfcFacetedBrep generally'. The recommendation 'keep-manifold-optional' overstates the breadth of what works.

Zero-volume / inside-out cutter (cutter authored CW throughout): the validator detects 'via sign(volume) and applies a global triangle-winding flip'. But a cutter with MIXED winding (some faces CW, some CCW — a real ArchiCAD failure mode where authored faces and inherited-via-mapped-item faces disagree) has signed-volume near zero. The validator's sign-check passes, no flip is applied, Manifold rejects opaquely, csgrs misclassifies inside/outside. The design has no per-face winding-consistency check, only a global signed-volume sign.
verdicts.blocker: Revit / ArchiCAD self-intersecting BRep cutter passes the proposed manifoldness validator (which checks edge-pair count, signed-volume sign, and degenerate-area triangles — but NOT pairwise triangle-triangle intersection). Manifold rejects opaquely → fallback to csgrs BSP → csgrs partitions space against planes that interpenetrate other faces of the same mesh, producing classified-but-geometrically-incorrect inside/outside regions. Result: Boolean returns 'success' with corrupted topology. Reveal-all does NOT trigger because no kernel raised an error. Downstream consumers see a structurally valid glTF with wrong geometry — the worst possible degeneracy outcome. The lens prompt names this case verbatim ('Self-intersecting input (Revit sometimes emits this): graceful failure or garbage output?') and the design does not address it.
verdicts.suggested_mitigation: Three required additions before this design clears the robustness bar: (1) Add a triangle-triangle self-intersection check to the validator using a BVH over the cutter (and host, when post-halfspace_clip). Output CutterDefect::SelfIntersecting with the offending triangle pair count. This is ~250 LOC with `parry3d` (already in the broad-phase dep chain) and is the only honest way to convert self-intersection from silent corruption into a typed reveal-all signal. (2) Replace the 'nudge coplanar cutter by 1mm' coplanar mitigation with a proper coplanar-face classifier that runs BEFORE kernel dispatch: detect cutter faces coplanar with host faces using a plane-equation dot-product test (eps = 1e-5 on normalized normals, 1e-3 mm on plane offset), then route to a tailored coplanar handler (drop opposite-orientation pairs, keep same-orientation rim). This is the only way to get dimensionally-correct flush openings. (3) Remove the conditional gating of validate+weld on post-halfspace_clip hosts — run it unconditionally on the host whenever the source tag chain shows ANY prior CSG/clip operation. The 'new flag' design leaks the abstraction; the engine should self-detect. Until these three land, downgrade the recommendation to 'manifold-only behind csg feature, typed Unsupported otherwise' — the csgrs fallback as specified is not robust enough to be the no-feature default.

verdicts.shape_category: prism-minus-brep (IfcExtrudedAreaSolid host minus IfcFacetedBrep / IfcManifoldSolidBrep cutter)
concerns: Pre-weld spatial-hash pass adds O(V_cutter) hashing per cut on the happy path even when manifold would have accepted the input unmodified. For 5k brep openings the welder runs 5k times regardless of need; recommend gating the welder behind a cheap 'naive manifoldness probe' (count edges with HashMap<(u32,u32),u8>) so already-clean Tekla 2024+ exports skip both the weld and the rehash. Cost is borderline (sub-ms per opening) but it is non-zero allocation pressure (each call: HashMap<[i32;3],u32> + a Vec<u32> reindex map) and scales with the brep count.

csgrs BSP fallback has substantially worse asymptotic behaviour than manifold's mesh-arrangement. BSP triangle-plane splitting is O(T_host * T_cutter) in the worst case (each cutter polygon may split each host polygon, recursively). For a 200-triangle Tekla brep cutter against a 600-triangle halfspace-clipped wall, that is ~120k split operations vs manifold's roughly O((T_h+T_c) log (T_h+T_c)) arrangement. If 5% of 5k openings (250) fall back to csgrs, and each fallback is ~50-200 ms vs manifold's ~5-20 ms, that's 12-50 s added wall-clock on a project where the manifold path completes in single-digit seconds. The design acknowledges this only as an output-quality concern ('csgrs may produce 10× the triangle count'), not as an asymptotic-cost concern.

csgrs output is per-polygon plane-tagged with fan-triangulation downstream. The vertex-dedup pass at 1e-3 mm grid runs over the OUTPUT buffer post-triangulation, meaning every fan triangle's duplicated apex vertex gets re-hashed. For a wall with 50 cut faces this is fine; for the multi-shell ArchiCAD case noted in edge_cases where N components are subtracted independently and unioned, the dedup pass runs N+1 times with quadratic growth of intermediate buffers. No streaming/in-place option.

subtract_many today rebuilds the host Manifold from buffers on every call. The design preserves this but adds the welder allocation pattern (HashMap rebuild per cutter) without addressing the cross-product N-hosts-share-one-cutter case raised in open_questions. For Tekla precast (one opening voids one shell pair, common pattern) the cutter manifold is rebuilt twice. The design defers this to 'worth measuring on big files' rather than treating it as a known O(N_hosts) waste; on a 10k-product job with cross-product cuts the cutter rebuild dominates the cheap cases.

The pre-validation gate (~150 LOC: edge-pair count, signed-volume, degenerate-triangle filter) runs unconditionally before subtract_many on the brep path. Signed-volume is O(T) and trivial; edge-pair count requires a HashMap<(u32,u32),u8> sized to ~3*T entries — for a 5k-opening project averaging 100 tri/cutter this is 1.5M HashMap inserts and ~30 MB of transient allocation across the run. Not catastrophic but not free. Could be folded into the welder's single pass to amortise.

Manifold's batch_difference is leveraged today; the proposed design preserves this for the multi-cutter case but the csgrs fallback path does sequential subtract (cutter-by-cutter) per the design ('host.subtract(&cutter)' with no batch primitive mentioned). When the fallback fires on a wall with multiple brep openings the cost compounds: O(C * T_host * T_cutter_avg) where the host grows in triangle count after each split. This is the classic BSP-CSG worst case and the design does not flag it.

No upfront cheap-rejection (the iOverlay 2D-projection check mentioned in open_questions) is in the prerequisites list. For oversized brep cutters that don't actually intersect the host, both manifold and csgrs do full work before returning identity. On Tekla precast where the same brep template is replicated across many panels, false-positive cuts (cutter bbox overlaps host bbox but actual geometry misses) consume kernel time. Cheap AABB intersection is presumably already done in broad_phase but a host-extrusion-axis 2D footprint check is the next-cheapest reject.
verdicts.blocker: csgrs BSP fallback wall-clock degradation when manifold rejects: the design treats csgrs as 'a fallback' without quantifying that BSP-CSG is asymptotically worse than Manifold's mesh-arrangement. If 5% of brep cutters fail validation post-weld (T-junctions, multi-shell, AdvancedBrep approximation seams), each fallback costs 5-20× the manifold path. On a 5k-opening project that 5% = 250 cutters × ~100 ms = 25 s added, on top of the manifold happy-path which would have completed in ~10-20 s. The design needs a wall-clock budget per fallback and a hard cap (e.g., abort fallback at >500 ms per cut, surface as CutterDefect::FallbackTimeout) before recommending this as production-grade.
verdicts.suggested_mitigation: Three concrete changes to land the design under a defensible performance envelope: (1) Gate the welder + validator behind a cheap O(T) manifoldness probe (sign(volume) + edge-pair count in one pass), skipping both expensive steps for already-clean inputs — Tekla 2024+ and IFC exporters that emit well-formed brep should pay nothing. (2) Add a hard wall-clock budget to the csgrs fallback path (e.g., 200 ms per cut) with abort-to-Unsupported semantics; instrument it via a CutOpeningsStats.fallback_aborted counter so we can see the rate on real projects. (3) Cache the cutter Manifold (and weld result) by cutter-buffer-hash across the cross-product flush — one opening voiding N hosts pays the build cost once, not N times. Together these convert the design from 'works but may double wall-clock under adversarial input' to 'matches manifold's perf on the happy path, bounded degradation on the fallback path.' Without (2) in particular, a single pathological project (e.g., a Tekla model where every brep has T-junctions) could blow the cut-openings phase from seconds to minutes silently.

verdicts.shape_category: prism-minus-brep (IfcExtrudedAreaSolid host minus IfcFacetedBrep / IfcManifoldSolidBrep cutter)
concerns: Net new owned LOC roughly doubles the CSG surface from ~314 to ~1000+ (validator ~150, welder ~80, csgrs glue ~400, typed errors ~30, cutter-class plumbing ~40, plus coplanar nudge ~30 and tests). This is real debt that will need ongoing tuning for every new authoring quirk (Tekla T-junctions, ArchiCAD multi-shell, AdvancedBrep planar-approx, CW-winding olds). The design author admits 'document as known limitation' for multi-shell and AdvancedBrep — those will recur as issues.

Bus-factor: csgrs is named as 'v0.20.1, 231 stars, active' — that's a small project with a likely single-maintainer pattern; replacing one fragile upstream (manifold's C++ build, fragile maintainer per MEMORY.md) with TWO upstreams (manifold AND csgrs) increases not decreases total dependency surface. The design keeps manifold as the primary path, so the C++ build pain and cross-compile breakage that originally motivated a pure-Rust escape hatch are NOT eliminated — they're still there for the dominant path.

The pre-weld + validator + cutter-class plumbing is numerically-sensitive code (eps choice, snap grid, signed-volume sign convention, T-junction detection). This is precisely the category of code that accumulates one-off tuning commits forever — every new authoring tool the project encounters will need an eps tweak or a new defect variant. The open question 'Does csgrs's BSP handle T-junctioned input correctly in practice?' is unanswered and gates ~200 LOC of conditional T-junction-resolver work — that's debt that may double if the answer is bad.

Two CSG kernels with different output characteristics (manifold deduplicates + re-meshes; csgrs preserves BSP face structure that 'may have 10× the triangle count') means downstream stats, mesh_quality classifier, fingerprint columns (centroid/vertex_count/triangle_count in instances.parquet per MEMORY.md fingerprint-columns) become non-deterministic depending on which kernel ran. That ripples into substrate cache invalidation logic and clash-engine fingerprinting — a maintenance tax across the codebase, not just in geom/.

Feature-flag matrix grows: today there is `csg` (on/off). Design proposes `csg-pure` 'mutually-exclusive with the existing `csg` feature, OR additive with priority order'. The 'OR' is the giveaway — this is undecided. Whichever way it lands, CI matrix expands (today 4 platforms × 2 flag states already per MEMORY.md release-cross-compile; this becomes 4 × 4 or 4 × 3), and bug reports now need to specify which kernel ran.

Typed CsgKernelError variants leak through Outcome::Fallback into substrate stats counters that agents consume — that's an agent-visible contract change that triggers the AGENTS.md upkeep rule (per project CLAUDE.md). Adds maintenance coupling: every new variant must be reflected in AGENTS.md, and bumps _CACHE_SCHEMA_VERSION if it lands as a substrate column.
verdicts.suggested_mitigation: Ship the design in two phases to limit ongoing tuning surface: (1) the manifoldness validator + typed CutterDefect error variants ONLY (~180 LOC). That alone converts the dominant Tekla failure from 'silent fallback' to 'typed diagnostic agents can route on' — the highest-value, lowest-debt piece. Do NOT ship the csgrs pure-Rust fallback in v1; it adds 400+ LOC owned forever, doubles upstream bus-factor risk, and introduces a second kernel whose output characteristics differ from manifold (breaking determinism for fingerprint columns and substrate stats). Defer csgrs to a future v2 gated on: (a) audit of csgrs's T-junction tolerance answering the open question, (b) actual cross-compile pressure (linux-aarch64 deferred via GH #28 is the real driver — re-evaluate then), (c) benchmark confirming output triangle-count is within 2× of manifold's. Skip the spatial-hash welder until measured: most Tekla T-junctions are NOT coincident-vertex bugs (the design admits the welder WILL NOT FIX the dominant case), so its value is unclear. Keep the cutter-class plumbing (~40 LOC) since it's already justified by other tag-routing work. Net v1 cost: ~220 LOC owned, no new upstream, no new feature flag, no CI matrix growth — strictly lower long-term debt than the full design AND lower than the status-quo's opaque-fallback debt.

category.label: IfcFacetedBrep − prism
category.description: Brep host (uncommon for walls/slabs, common for foundations, transitions).

design.loc_estimate: ~2.5k LOC (new pure-Rust 3D mesh Boolean kernel ~2.0k + brep preflight ~250 + integration glue ~250). Alternative: ~150 LOC integration if we adopt csgrs as the dependency.
design.primary_algorithm:
FROM FIRST PRINCIPLES — WHY THIS IS 3D-CSG-SHAPED, NOT REDUCIBLE: A brep host is an arbitrary closed polyhedral shell — there is no canonical axis or 2D profile that captures its geometry. The prism-minus-prism shortcut (resolve to a 2D polygon Boolean on the common profile plane) does not apply: the cutter has a profile plane and extrusion axis, but the host does not. Any face of the host can intersect the cutter's side walls at an arbitrary angle. The resulting net solid has (a) host faces clipped to be outside the cutter prism, (b) cutter side-walls and caps that lie inside the host with reversed winding, (c) new boundary edges where each host face crosses each cutter face. This is exactly the shape that mesh-mesh booleans solve, and there is no closed-form analytic shortcut for the general brep case. Halfspace cuts are tractable because the cutter is a plane (a single linear predicate); prism-prism is tractable because both operands collapse to a 2D problem. Brep-prism collapses to neither.

ALGORITHM — PURE-RUST 3D MESH BOOLEAN VIA CSGRS-STYLE BSP, PRESCREENED BY EXTRUSION-AXIS FAST PATH:

Step 0 — PREFLIGHT BREP VALIDATION (new module mesh::brep_validate, ~250 LOC):
  Run before attempting any boolean. Check: (1) every edge is shared by exactly two faces (closed-manifold edge check via HashMap<(u32,u32), (face_id, face_id)>); (2) winding consistency (every shared edge has its two faces traversing it in opposite directions); (3) no self-intersection (cheap heuristic: face AABB overlap via Parry's BVH from the geom feature). If any check fails, return Outcome::UnsupportedBrep with the specific reason — non-manifold breps cannot be cut and the current Outcome::Fallback (reveal-all) at least surfaces a typed reason now. ~75% of Tekla precast breps pass this; the remaining ~25% have T-junctions or hanging edges that even manifold-csg rejects.

Step 1 — AABB FAST REJECT: Compute axis-aligned bounding boxes of host and cutter. If they don't overlap, return host unchanged. This is a 24-float comparison; near-free.

Step 2 — EXTRUSION-AXIS FAST PATH (optional, ~300 LOC, defers the heavy machinery): For the dominant Tekla precast case (panel host + rectangular door/window cutter), the cutter IS a prism with a known extrusion direction d and 2D profile P. If we can determine that the cutter fully traverses the host along d (cutter z-bounds extend beyond host z-bounds along d — a single dot-product check on host's AABB extreme vertices projected onto d), we can decompose:
  (a) "trim region" = ray-cast each host triangle's vertices along ±d to find the host's silhouette in the cutter's profile plane (geom::broad_phase BVH gives us the candidate triangles in O(log N));
  (b) classify each host triangle as INSIDE / OUTSIDE / CROSSING the cutter prism via signed distance to each of the cutter's 4–N side planes;
  (c) clip CROSSING triangles by each side plane using the existing halfspace_clip primitive (which already handles cap stitching and earcut triangulation) — but composing 4–N halfspaces requires an INTERSECTION semantic (keep triangle parts inside ALL side planes) rather than the existing UNION/sequential semantic. This is N halfspace clips of the *cutter shell*, not of the host. So: clip cutter side walls to host AABB, run halfspace_clip on the cutter once per host face that crosses it (using the host face's plane as the clipping plane), then invert the cutter winding and weld onto the host's clipped portion;
  (d) NOT recommended as the primary path because (c) needs new "clip-by-convex-prism" infrastructure and the corner cases (host face nearly parallel to cutter axis, cutter exiting through a curved host face) reintroduce all the robustness problems we'd hit anyway. RECOMMEND skipping this fast path in v1 — go straight to step 3 generic kernel.

Step 3 — GENERIC PURE-RUST 3D MESH BOOLEAN VIA CSGRS:
  Add csgrs (pure Rust, MIT, ~1k stars trajectory, version 0.20.1 current) as an optional dependency under a new `pure_csg` cargo feature parallel to today's `csg` (manifold). csgrs implements the CSG.js BSP-tree algorithm (Smith 2011, ported from JS by many tools), which is the canonical pure-Rust 3D mesh Boolean kernel:
    (a) Convert (vertices: &[f32], indices: &[u32]) → csgrs::CSG via from_polygons builder, splitting each triangle into a csgrs::Polygon with computed plane.
    (b) host_csg.difference(&cutter_csg) — internally builds two BSP trees, clips polygons of host against cutter's BSP (keeping outside), clips polygons of cutter against host's BSP (keeping inside, reversed), unions the two clipped sets.
    (c) Convert back via csgrs::CSG::polygons() iterator, fan-triangulating each polygon into our flat-buffer form.
  Wrap this behind geom::csg::subtract_many_pure(host_v, host_i, cutters) with the same signature as the manifold version, so cut_openings.rs (line 140 and line 422) calls one or the other based on cfg(feature = "pure_csg") vs cfg(feature = "csg") with pure_csg winning when both enabled.

Step 4 — FALLBACK DISCIPLINE: csgrs's BSP can fail (or produce non-manifold output) on (a) coplanar faces between host and cutter, (b) self-intersecting input, (c) f32 precision collapse far from origin. Our preflight (Step 0) catches (b). For (a) the csgrs BSP plane-classification uses a tolerance — we set EPS = 1e-4 mm in local-anchored coords (same as halfspace_clip's ON_PLANE_EPS scale). For (c) we already rebase to mesh_anchor before the call. On any csgrs failure, return Outcome::Fallback (reveal-all preserved); aggregate the count in CutOpeningsStats.fallback so the stats summary surfaces the rate.

Step 5 — NESTED HALFSPACE COMPOSITION: cut_openings::apply() already does halfspace clips BEFORE the solid-cutter boolean (line 115-126). A brep host with a halfspace clip first reduces to (clipped brep) which is still a closed mesh — halfspace_clip caps it with a planar polygon. The resulting mesh feeds straight into Step 3. No new composition logic needed.

WHY NOT KEEP MANIFOLD: Manifold's batch_difference works for the brep-prism case TODAY when the brep is closed-manifold (the GH #39 bug is specific to halfspace cutters, not brep cutters). The cost is the C++/cmake dep that's the open blocker on the default-on csg feature in the wheel. Going pure-Rust here aligns with the project's pure-Rust direction AND closes the cmake-on-Windows wheel-build risk. csgrs is less robust than Manifold on adversarial coplanar input, but the brep-prism case in real IFC files is benign — Tekla precast openings rarely share a face with the panel host.

WHY NOT TYPED-UNSUPPORTED-ERROR: Brep-prism is ~few% of cuts but it covers the dominant Tekla precast workflow and ArchiCAD complex walls — these are real-money products. Refusing them with Outcome::Unsupported regresses behavior available since the manifold-csg feature shipped.

WHY NOT MIXED: We considered keeping manifold for brep-prism specifically and pure-Rust everywhere else, but that perpetuates the C++ dep we're trying to remove. csgrs's robustness gap on this category is narrow enough (the failure modes are catchable) to fully replace.
prerequisites: mesh::brep_validate module (new, ~250 LOC) — closed-manifold edge check (every edge shared by 2 faces, opposite winding), self-intersection screen via Parry BVH AABB-overlap, returns BrepValidity::{Manifold, Open, NonManifoldEdges(count), SelfIntersecting} so the caller can decide between Outcome::Cut attempt and Outcome::UnsupportedBrep up front rather than catching csgrs panics.

geom::csg::subtract_many_pure(host_v, host_i, cutters) — new entry point (~250 LOC) that mirrors today's subtract_many signature but routes through csgrs::CSG::difference. Converts flat-buffer triangle list ↔ csgrs::Polygon set on both sides. Includes the same OutOfBoundsIndex / InvalidVertexBuffer guards as the manifold version. Wrapped behind a new `pure_csg` cargo feature.

cargo dependency: csgrs = { version = "0.20.1", optional = true, default-features = false } under [dependencies]; feature gate `pure_csg = ["mesh", "dep:csgrs"]` in [features]; the existing `csg` (manifold) feature stays but becomes mutually exclusive at the dispatch layer in cut_openings.rs (cfg-flagged use statement picks one).

cut_openings.rs dispatch update (~30 LOC) — both call sites (apply line 140 and CrossProductCut::flush line 422) become `#[cfg(feature = "pure_csg")] use crate::geom::csg::subtract_many_pure as subtract_many;` with a fallback `#[cfg(all(feature = "csg", not(feature = "pure_csg")))]` to manifold. No call-site code changes.

test fixtures: synthetic IfcFacetedBrep wall with a prism opening for unit tests in geom::csg::tests — adapt the existing box_at helper to emit a brep (irregular polyhedron, e.g. truncated pyramid) instead of a unit cube. ~50 LOC of test scaffolding.

Outcome::UnsupportedBrep variant added to cut_openings::Outcome enum + accumulator field in CutOpeningsStats.unsupported_brep — surfaces non-manifold brep input distinctly from generic Outcome::Fallback so the agent layer can flag authoring issues. ~15 LOC.

AGENTS.md update — document that brep-host cuts now go through csgrs (pure-Rust) when the `pure_csg` feature is active, and the new Outcome::UnsupportedBrep code-path. Per CLAUDE.md project rule, agent-facing surface changes require AGENTS.md update in the same change.
edge_cases: Non-manifold brep host (T-junctions, hanging edges, inconsistent winding) — common in Tekla precast exports. Currently silently falls through to Outcome::Fallback after csgrs panics or returns garbage. Mitigation: brep_validate preflight rejects these BEFORE the boolean attempt, returns Outcome::UnsupportedBrep, reveal-all preserved.

Open shell brep (IfcFaceBasedSurfaceModel masquerading as a solid) — has boundary edges (edges with only one adjacent face). Cannot be a cut host. brep_validate catches this; halfspace_clip would also fail because cap stitching assumes closed input. Treat as Outcome::UnsupportedBrep.

Cutter prism extends fully beyond the host on both sides — handled by csgrs BSP naturally; the cutter polygons outside the host get clipped away during the host.invert/clipTo(cutter)/cutter.clipTo(host) pipeline. Same behavior as today's manifold path on the boundary-straddling test.

Cutter coplanar with a brep face — pathological for BSP algorithms. csgrs's plane-classification tolerance (we set 1e-4 mm) should treat coplanar polygons as on-plane and assign them to the appropriate side. Documented failure mode if the tolerance is wrong: cap doubles or holes. Mitigation: if Outcome::Fallback fires at high rate on a specific file, log the cutter-host pair so we can capture the coplanar geometry for a regression test.

Brep with a curved face approximated as a poly-loop (IfcAdvancedBrep tagged advanced_brep_approx) — the planar fan from brep.rs lines 230-237 collapses curvature to a planar polygon. Cuts produce wrong volumes silently. brep_validate cannot detect this (the planar approximation IS topologically valid). Document as known approximation; don't try to fix in this category — IfcAdvancedBrep gets its own analysis row.

Brep host with multiple disconnected shells (uncommon but legal — e.g. a precast panel with a separately-shelled embedded fitting). brep_validate's edge-sharing check would pass per-shell; the boolean must run against the union, not just the outer shell. csgrs polygon-list construction handles this naturally; manifold-csg requires a single connected shell and would reject. This is a SLIGHT WIN for the pure-Rust path.

Cutter is in-rep (IfcBooleanClippingResult inline) rather than cross-product (IfcRelVoidsElement) — the apply() path handles this via segment partitioning at line 62. No special handling needed for brep host vs prism host; the host_segs/cutter_segs split is by source tag, not by entity type.

Brep host tilted 30° from vertical (sloped retaining wall, dormer panel) — local-anchored coords stay near origin so f32 precision is fine. The cutter's coordinate frame is independent (extrusion direction baked into its vertices already). csgrs's BSP is orientation-invariant. No issue.

Nested halfspace clip BEFORE the brep-prism boolean — apply() lines 115-126 already runs halfspace_clip on the host (whether the host is brep or extrusion) before subtract_many. A clipped brep is still a closed mesh (halfspace_clip caps the cut). Feeds into csgrs unchanged. Documented behavior.

Multiple cutters fold (3+ openings in one Tekla panel) — csgrs supports difference chaining but the cost is O(N) BSP rebuilds. We replicate manifold's batch_difference benefit by computing the *union* of the cutters first (single BSP build), then running host.difference(union). Worst case: cutters overlap each other (rare); union is well-defined and cheaper than serial difference. ~30 LOC inside subtract_many_pure.

Empty result (brep fully consumed by cutter) — return (Vec::new(), Vec::new()) just like today's path. Caller treats as Outcome::Cut with zero triangles. cut_openings.rs line 116 already has the host.1.is_empty() guard.
open_questions: csgrs robustness on real Tekla precast files — needs to be exercised against the actual sample corpus before committing. Suggest a benchmark pass on 5–10 brep-host files (whatever the existing tester sweep has) comparing csgrs vs manifold output volumes + topology stats. Acceptable if csgrs fallback rate ≤ manifold fallback rate + 10%.

csgrs BSP performance on large breps (Tekla panels with 500+ faces) — manifold's batch_difference does single-pass; csgrs may rebuild trees per cutter. If we hit O(N²) issues, the union-cutters-first optimization (described in edge cases) is mandatory rather than nice-to-have.

Should brep_validate live in mesh:: (alongside brep.rs as a sibling) or geom:: (alongside csg.rs as a consumer-side check)? Argument for mesh:: — it's about the tessellation output's well-formedness. Argument for geom:: — it's a precondition for the boolean engine. Lean mesh:: (the tessellator owns its output's invariants).

Do we deprecate the manifold (`csg`) feature entirely once `pure_csg` is the default, or keep both behind feature flags indefinitely? Implication for AGENTS.md and pyproject.toml. Probably keep both for one or two minor releases, then drop `csg` if pure_csg matches volume invariants on the tester sweep.

Coplanar tolerance picking — csgrs's PLANE_EPSILON default may not match our local-anchored coord scale. Need a tuning pass (sweep 1e-5 to 1e-2 mm) on a synthetic coplanar-cutter regression test. Default 1e-4 mm is a starting point.

Outcome::UnsupportedBrep — is this a new variant or a sub-tag inside Outcome::Fallback? Cleaner as a new variant for surface visibility, but breaks any consumer matching on the existing 3-variant enum exhaustively. Survey the call sites before committing (python wrapper, MCP tool, CLI stats printer). Likely safe — only stats accumulation and the python __repr__ touch the enum today.

concerns: Coplanar-face correctness is hand-waved with a fixed 1e-4 mm epsilon. The chosen algorithm (csgrs = CSG.js BSP port) is the canonical example of a boolean that is NOT robust on coplanar input — BSP plane-classification depends on polygon processing order, so the same brep+cutter pair can yield different topology across runs when input order changes. Tekla door cutters routinely share a face with the wall reveal; this is a COMMON case, not an edge case. The design lists coplanar as edge_case #4 but the proposed mitigation ('log the pair for a regression test') is reactive, not corrective — there is no algorithmic guarantee the output is correct, only a request to capture failures after the fact.

The fixed ON_PLANE epsilon does not scale with distance from the local anchor. CSG.js / csgrs stores each polygon's plane as (normal, w=dot(normal,point)) in single precision. For a brep face 30 m from the rebased anchor with unit normal, plane.w = 30000 mm. f32 relative precision at that magnitude is ~1.8e-3 mm — strictly worse than the proposed 1e-4 mm tolerance. The design asserts halfspace_clip 'already rebases to mesh_anchor' but the worked example never demonstrates the tolerance survives the rebase. For larger breps (Tekla precast panels can be 6 m long), the situation is worse at the far end. The tolerance has the wrong dimensional model.

The brep_validate winding-consistency check is incorrect as a precondition for the boolean. 'Every shared edge has its two faces traversing it in opposite directions' is a LOCAL consistency check — a whole-mesh inversion (every triangle flipped) passes trivially while producing a negative-volume brep. CSG difference of a negative-volume host with a positive-volume cutter computes host ∪ cutter, not host \ cutter. The correct precondition is signed volume > 0 (divergence theorem on the closed shell). Without it, half the IFC brep files in the wild (orientation flipped at the IfcClosedShell level) silently produce wrong results.

No volume-conservation post-condition. csgrs's documented failure mode on coplanar/near-coplanar input is silent garbage output, not panic. The design's 'fallback discipline' only catches csgrs Result::Err, but BSP collapses produce valid-looking Ok(mesh) with corrupt topology. A correct design requires a post-check 'result_volume ∈ [host_volume − sum(cutter_volume), host_volume]' (or a tighter bound via individual cutter overlap), and routes to Outcome::Fallback if violated. The design has neither this check nor any other invariant the result must satisfy.

Worked example trace shows a winding-correctness gap on boundary-straddling cutters. Host 1m×0.25m×3m wall, cutter 0.9m×0.3m×2.1m door at one face: cutter extends 0.025 m beyond the wall in Y on each side (over-sized cutter is the AUTHORING convention to ensure clean reveal). csgrs's clipTo/invert dance: host.clipTo(cutter) keeps host polygons outside the cutter; cutter.clipTo(host).invert() reverses the cutter polygons inside the host. But the cutter polygons OUTSIDE the host get clipped away during clipTo(host). Result: the cutter's side wall is split at y=0 and y=0.25 along the wall thickness; the inverted side-wall fragments must stitch to the new edges where the wall front/back faces were cut. CSG.js fan-triangulates each polygon independently, so the stitching across the wall thickness depends on coincident vertex deduplication that the design never specifies (csgrs does not collapse coincident verts by default).

Composition with halfspace_clip introduces a SYSTEMATIC coplanar failure. After halfspace_clip caps the host with an earcut fan on the cut plane, a subsequent solid cutter whose face is parallel to that cap plane (door cutter bottom = floor halfspace plane — extremely common: every door at floor level has this exact composition) presents csgrs with a polygon group that is coplanar by construction. This isn't a corner case the design's edge_case #4 acknowledges; it is the COMMON case the design's own apply() ordering creates. The cap fan's plane equation is computed independently per cap triangle and may differ by f32 round-off — csgrs's plane-coincidence detection will classify them inconsistently.

IFC winding/orientation semantics are unaddressed. IfcFace.Bound vertices are ordered by IfcOrientedEdge.Orientation × IfcEdge sense, and the containing IfcClosedShell defines outward as the side the face normal (per right-hand rule from the loop) points. IfcOpenShell and IfcFaceSurface variants exist that the design lumps together. brep.rs presumably already handles this at load time, but the design does not verify that the *post-load* mesh handed to csgrs has consistent outward winding relative to material — and csgrs has no internal check for this. A brep authored with consistent-but-inward winding (legal in some authoring tools after a mirror operation) sails through brep_validate and produces an inverted result silently.

Cutter unification via union-first as the dedup strategy is itself incorrect on the common touching-cutter case. Two openings sharing a mullion (twin doors, double window) have coplanar touching faces. csgrs::union on coplanar-touching meshes is exactly the BSP-coplanar failure mode the design says it will avoid — the design proposes solving an N-cutter problem by first running a strictly harder N-coplanar-face problem through the same fragile kernel. Manifold's batch_difference avoided this because Manifold uses exact predicates internally; csgrs does not.
verdicts.blocker: The brep_validate preflight as specified does not establish the precondition that csgrs::difference requires for correctness. Specifically: edge-pairing + opposite-traversal proves the mesh is a closed orientable 2-manifold, but does NOT prove the normals point outward (positive signed volume). An IFC IfcFacetedBrep authored with inward winding (legal — happens after mirror/symmetry operations in Tekla, ArchiCAD, occasionally Revit family import) passes the proposed brep_validate and then csgrs computes host ∪ cutter where the caller expected host \\ cutter. The output is a brep that includes the cutter as a positive volume bump, returned as Outcome::Cut (success). Worked example: a 1m×0.25m×3m wall with inverted winding + a 0.9m×0.3m×2.1m door cutter would yield ~0.75 + 0.4725 = ~1.2225 m³ of "wall" with the door extruded outward as a bump, marked as a successful cut. This is silent correctness corruption with no log, no fallback trigger, no surfacing to the agent layer — exactly the failure mode IFC-grade tooling cannot have.
verdicts.suggested_mitigation: Three mandatory additions before this design can be considered correct: (1) brep_validate must compute signed volume via the divergence theorem (sum over triangles of dot(v0, cross(v1-v0, v2-v0))/6) and reject negative-volume input as BrepValidity::InvertedWinding (with an option to auto-flip and retry, since recovery is a single triangle-index swap per face); (2) add a volume-conservation post-check after csgrs::difference: assert result_signed_volume ∈ [host_volume − sum_cutter_volumes − eps, host_volume + eps], and route violations to Outcome::Fallback with a typed reason (BSP_Coplanar_Collapse, Volume_Inversion, etc.) accumulated in CutOpeningsStats; (3) replace the fixed ON_PLANE_EPS = 1e-4 mm with a distance-from-anchor-scaled tolerance, e.g. eps = max(1e-4, host_aabb_diagonal × 1e-6) mm, computed once per call and passed into a forked csgrs that exposes PLANE_EPSILON as a parameter (csgrs's upstream default is not safe for ifcfast's coordinate scale). Without all three, "Outcome::Cut" on this category cannot be trusted by any downstream consumer.

concerns: Coplanar faces between host and cutter — the design's only mitigation is a hand-picked PLANE_EPSILON=1e-4 mm and post-hoc 'log and capture'. csgrs (CSG.js-port BSP) is exactly the class of kernel that is *known-fragile* on coplanar polygons; the failure modes named (cap doubles, holes) are precisely the silent-garbage outcomes the lens is asking about, with no algorithmic fix — only a regression-test feedback loop after users hit them. IFC recesses landing flush on a brep face (Tekla precast threshold details, ArchiCAD wall-end caps) make this a hot path, not a corner.

Self-intersection 'screen' via Parry BVH AABB-overlap of faces is not a self-intersection test. Any non-axis-aligned brep has overlapping face AABBs by construction (faces sharing an edge have overlapping AABBs). The screen will either pass everything (the real failures slip through to csgrs panic) or be tightened until it rejects legitimate breps. The design conflates 'a precondition was checked' with 'the precondition holds'.

Degenerate CUTTER is entirely unhandled. The proposal preflights the host but assumes the cutter is a well-formed prism. IfcExtrudedAreaSolid with Depth=0 (broken Revit families), collinear-point profiles, or zero-area profiles produce NaN face normals in csgrs's Polygon::new plane computation — BSP either infinite-loops, panics, or returns a tree that mis-classifies everything. There is no zero-volume/zero-area cutter check before the kernel call.

f32 round-trip across the csgrs boundary destroys exact coplanarity. csgrs computes plane normals from polygon vertices internally; even after mesh_anchor rebase, a host face and an exactly-coplanar cutter face computed from f32 vertices will disagree at ~1e-7 normal magnitude. BSP plane classification then sees 'spanning' instead of 'coplanar' and splits a face that should be on-plane — producing the sliver triangles and T-junctions that downstream halfspace_clip cannot recover from. mesh_anchor solves far-from-origin scale; it does not solve f32-quantization-induced non-coplanarity.

csgrs failure detection is unspecified. csgrs's difference() returns CSG, not Result — there is no error channel. The design says 'on any csgrs failure return Outcome::Fallback' but the only way to detect failure is either catch_unwind around a panicking BSP (slow, doesn't catch silent garbage) or a post-condition check on output topology (not proposed). Non-manifold csgrs output (a real possibility on the inputs the lens asks about) will pass through as Outcome::Cut and corrupt downstream meshes.

brep_validate's winding-consistency check is ill-defined on the very inputs it claims to gate. For a non-manifold edge with 3+ adjacent faces, 'opposite winding' has no defined meaning — the checker's behavior on the most common Tekla pathology (T-junction with 3 faces meeting at a hanging edge) is not specified. Either it returns NonManifoldEdges (covers some cases) or it returns a contradictory 'winding inconsistent' (rejects legitimate-but-irregular geometry).

Cutter side wall coplanar with a brep EDGE (not face) — extrusion slides exactly along a brep's vertical edge, common for windows aligned to a column. Produces zero-area BSP splits. Tighter than face-coplanarity and not enumerated in edge_cases. csgrs has no documented behavior here.

The 'cutter fully consumed by host but exiting through a curved (faceted) host face' degeneracy — the cutter's exit cap lands on multiple host faces at sub-EPS angles. Step 2's fast path explicitly punts on this; Step 3's generic BSP inherits the same precision problem with no extra mitigation.
verdicts.blocker: Real Tekla precast file: brep panel with a window opening whose top face is co-authored exactly on a horizontal brep face (a sill detail). f32 normals disagree at ~1e-7. csgrs BSP classifies the coplanar cap polygons as 'spanning' rather than 'coplanar-front', splits them into slivers, and emits a non-manifold output with duplicated cap triangles and ~3 micron T-junction gaps. Output passes all proposed post-checks (none exist), gets serialized to glTF, downstream clash engine reads it, vertex_count/triangle_count fingerprints are wrong, mesh_quality classifier flips from closed to degenerate silently. The Outcome::Fallback safety net never fires because there was no detectable failure. Manifold (the kernel being replaced) handles this case correctly because it operates in exact-predicate arithmetic; csgrs does not.
verdicts.suggested_mitigation: Three additions are required before this design survives the lens: (1) Cutter preflight symmetric to brep_validate — reject Depth < EPS, zero-area profiles, collinear-vertex polygons, and NaN/non-finite vertex coords up front, returning Outcome::UnsupportedCutter. (2) An exact-arithmetic coplanarity prepass: before handing host+cutter to csgrs, snap any cutter face whose plane is within 1e-5 of a host face's plane (in both normal and offset) to the host face's exact plane equation, then re-quantize cutter vertices to lie on it. This is the same trick OpenSCAD/CGAL kernels use to robustify f64-input BSPs; csgrs alone does not do it. (3) Output post-check: after csgrs.difference, run brep_validate on the result. If the output is non-manifold or has duplicated triangles, discard csgrs output and return Outcome::Fallback. Without all three, csgrs's well-documented coplanar-input fragility makes this category unsafe at the silent-garbage level. Alternative: keep manifold-csg specifically for IfcFacetedBrep − prism (mixed kernel) and accept the C++ dep here only; the design's argument against mixed ('perpetuates C++ dep') loses on the robustness axis the lens is judging.

concerns: BSP-tree boolean (csgrs/CSG.js port) is structurally 10-50x slower than Manifold on CAD-sized inputs: Manifold uses hierarchical CSG tree evaluation, multi-threaded BVH-pruned intersection, AVX2/NEON SIMD, and an arena allocator. csgrs is single-threaded recursive BSP with per-polygon heap allocations. For a 500-face Tekla precast panel with 5-8 openings this is the difference between ~10 ms and ~500 ms-1 s per host. There is no constant-factor knob to close this — the gap is algorithmic.

Extrapolating to the stated workload of 10k products / 5k IfcRelVoidsElements with the brep-prism category covering the dominant Tekla precast and ArchiCAD complex-wall workflow: if today's manifold path runs the cut-openings phase in ~30-60 s, the proposed pure-Rust csg phase will land in the 10-40 minute range. That moves cut-openings from 'second-tier cost' to the dominant phase of bundle generation, breaks the parallel-tessellation wins (MEMORY says ~2.4-2.6x at T=8 end-to-end), and is a clear regression on the path the user marks as default-on.

The 'union cutters first, then host.difference(union)' mitigation in edge_cases mis-models how Manifold's batch_difference works. Manifold doesn't serialize differences either — it builds a hierarchical CSG operation tree, lazily evaluates it, prunes via BVH, and produces a single SIMD-accelerated boolean pass. csgrs has no equivalent: union(c1..cN) inside csgrs is also N sequential BSP rebuilds. Computing the cutter-union first AT BEST replaces 'N host-vs-cutter BSP runs' with '(N-1) cutter-vs-cutter BSPs + 1 host-vs-union BSP'. Total work is comparable, not the order-of-magnitude win the design implies. Worse, the union's polygon count grows with each fold, so the final host.difference operates against an inflated operand.

Per-cut allocation pattern is pathological inside the rayon-parallel mesh pipeline. csgrs's recursive BSP build allocates a Node struct + Vec<Polygon> + Vec<Vertex> at every split, plus per-clip temporaries. A 500-face brep with 5 cutters produces O(50k) small heap allocations per host. With T=8 rayon workers all hitting the global allocator simultaneously, glibc malloc lock contention will throttle the parallel scaling that the rest of the pipeline relies on. Manifold uses contiguous flat arrays and an internal allocator — its allocation pattern is amortised and rayon-friendly.

The design explicitly skips Step 2 (extrusion-axis fast path) in v1, which removes the only mechanism that could have made the brep-prism path competitive for the dominant Tekla precast case. Combined with skipping the recommendation to keep manifold for this category, the design has no fallback for the slow case — every brep-prism cut hits the slow kernel unconditionally.

Parallelism interaction creates a long-tail wall-clock problem. The mesh tessellation pass parallelises across products, but a single 500-face brep with 8 openings can stall one rayon worker for several seconds in csgrs while the other 7 finish quickly. Manifold absorbs this by parallelising within a single boolean. ifcfast has no scheduler that can subdivide a single host's boolean across workers, so the slowest brep-prism cut sets the lower bound on the cut-openings phase wall-clock.

Step 1's AABB fast-reject buys ~0% on realistic workloads. IfcRelVoidsElement openings overlap their host BY DEFINITION (the void is contained in the host AABB). The fast-reject only triggers on misauthored or stale spatial relationships, which is the long-tail noise floor, not the common case.
verdicts.blocker: For a typical Tekla precast panel (brep host with ~500 faces, 5-8 prism cutters), per-host cut time on csgrs will run 10-50x slower than the current Manifold path. Scaled to a 5k-RelVoidsElement project, the cut-openings phase moves from tens of seconds to tens of minutes — and that's before counting allocator-contention degradation of the surrounding parallel mesh pipeline. There is no fast-path escape valve in the design as written (Step 2 is explicitly deferred), so every brep-prism cut pays the full BSP cost.
verdicts.suggested_mitigation: Do NOT replace Manifold for this category. Two viable paths: (1) Keep `csg` (manifold) as the engine for brep-prism specifically and route only the algorithmically-tractable categories (halfspace, prism-prism reducible to 2D) through pure-Rust kernels — the C++/cmake wheel-build pain is real but localised and a known-good pre-built manifold-csg crate already exists. (2) If pure-Rust is non-negotiable, defer this category to Outcome::Unsupported in v1 and ship the extrusion-axis fast path (Step 2) FIRST for the Tekla precast common case before touching the generic BSP. Either way, gate the change on a benchmark sweep against the existing tester corpus: measure cut-openings phase wall-clock on 5+ real brep-host files (Sannergata, Tekla precast samples) and require csgrs to be within 2x of Manifold before flipping the default. Also benchmark allocator contention at T=8 specifically — wall-clock per host is not enough; what matters is whether T=8 still scales to ~2.4x.

concerns: LOC estimate is misleading: the design headlines '~150 LOC integration' under the csgrs alternative but then enumerates seven prerequisites totaling ~500-700 LOC of NEW owned code (brep_validate ~250, subtract_many_pure ~250, dispatch wiring, Outcome::UnsupportedBrep plumbing, union-cutters-first optimization, test scaffolding, AGENTS.md). The headline understates owned-code growth by ~4x and that growth is permanent.

csgrs bus-factor risk is real and unevaluated. The design itself describes it as '~1k stars trajectory' (i.e. NOT yet 1k stars) at version 0.20.1 — pre-1.0 means minor-version breaking changes are normal Rust crate hygiene. No discussion of maintainer count, release cadence, issue-response time, or who else in the wild depends on it. Compare to manifold which has Google backing, Emmett Lalish as active lead, and is the de-facto reference 3D CSG kernel cited by every academic paper since 2022. The design's 'fragile maintainer' jab at manifold is unsupported.

The design mandates KEEPING BOTH the `csg` (manifold) and `pure_csg` (csgrs) features in the tree, gated by cfg flags at the cut_openings.rs dispatch site, 'for one or two minor releases'. This is the worst maintenance posture: two 3D CSG code paths, two sets of failure modes, two tolerance regimes, two stat-accumulator codepaths, twice the test surface. Every cut_openings change for that period must be reasoned about and tested on both backends. The 'open question' about whether to ever deprecate `csg` shows the exit strategy is unspecified.

Ongoing tuning is baked into the design as steady-state work, not one-time setup. The design explicitly calls for: a sweep of PLANE_EPSILON from 1e-5 to 1e-2 mm to find a tolerance, a benchmark pass against 5-10 brep-host files comparing csgrs vs manifold output, logging cutter-host pairs whenever Outcome::Fallback rate spikes on a file, and capturing coplanar geometry as regression tests on each new failure. Every new IFC authoring tool's quirks will trigger another tuning loop. Manifold absorbs this cost internally via robust predicates; we'd be absorbing it via human review.

CSG.js / Smith 2011 BSP (which csgrs implements) is a well-known FRAGILE algorithm — fine on toy inputs, brittle on real-world coplanar input. The design openly admits 'csgrs is less robust than Manifold on adversarial coplanar input' and hand-waves it with 'the brep-prism case in real IFC files is benign — Tekla precast openings rarely share a face with the panel host'. 'Rarely' is a maintenance commitment: every time a file DOES hit coplanar, we own the debugging. Wrong-volume silent outputs are dramatically more expensive to triage than wheel-build failures (which fail loud at CI time).

brep_validate (~250 LOC, new) is owned-forever code that duplicates topology validation that Manifold already does internally. We're moving validation cost out of a library and into our tree. The design also adds a new Outcome::UnsupportedBrep variant — an enum-breakage open question — and the design admits 'Survey the call sites before committing' is still TBD, meaning the ripple cost into the Python wrapper, MCP tool, and CLI stats printer is not budgeted.

The 'why not keep manifold' rationale is mis-framed: manifold's debt is the CMake-on-Windows wheel build and a C++ toolchain in CI. Both are one-time investments with known fixes — not ongoing tax. Trading a finite, well-understood build-system problem for an open-ended robustness/tuning treadmill on a pre-1.0 BSP library is a bad debt trade. The design also fails to consider the simpler maintenance option: fix the wheel build (single CI workstream) and KEEP manifold for the brep-prism path it already handles correctly today per the design's own admission ('Manifold's batch_difference works for the brep-prism case TODAY when the brep is closed-manifold').

Confidence is self-rated 'medium' with six unresolved open_questions, four of which are maintenance-cost-shaped (deprecation policy, tolerance tuning, perf scaling, enum back-compat). Shipping a design with those unresolved means the maintenance cost lands AFTER the code is in main, where it's harder to course-correct.
verdicts.blocker: Adopting csgrs as a second CSG backend while keeping manifold in tree for 'one or two minor releases' creates a double-maintenance window with no committed exit date and an unresolved deprecation policy. During that window every cut_openings change must be reasoned about, tested, and stat-instrumented across two 3D-CSG kernels with different robustness profiles, different tolerance regimes, and different failure modes. The design has no kill-switch criterion (no 'if csgrs fallback rate ≤ manifold's on the tester sweep by date X, drop manifold'), so the realistic outcome is permanent dual-path maintenance — strictly more debt than today's single-path manifold-only setup, even counting manifold's CMake/Windows wheel-build pain.
verdicts.suggested_mitigation: Reframe the decision as a build-system fix vs an algorithm swap, and pick the build-system fix for v1. Concretely: (a) keep manifold as the sole 3D-CSG backend for brep-prism — the design admits manifold handles this case correctly today; (b) invest the one-time CI/wheel-build work to make manifold build cleanly on Windows + macOS-Intel + linux-aarch64 (track as a single GH issue with a defined done state); (c) IF a wheel platform proves unfixable after a bounded attempt, THEN consider csgrs but ONLY as a full replacement (not a parallel path) with a committed deprecation of the `csg` feature in the same PR, a kill-switch metric (csgrs fallback rate ≤ manifold's on the existing tester sweep), and brep_validate folded into geom::csg::subtract_many_pure rather than a new module. The Outcome::UnsupportedBrep variant is a separable improvement worth doing regardless of backend — file it as its own issue. Above all, do NOT ship the dual-feature-gate posture; one CSG backend at a time in the tree.

category.label: Nested IfcBooleanClippingResult / IfcBooleanResult
category.description: Multi-level boolean trees where any operand can itself be a boolean.

design.primary_algorithm:
This category does not need its own CSG algorithm. The current design has already solved arbitrarily-deep nesting via a chain-tag-flattening trick at the extractor and a flat partition at the consumer. The right move is to retain that strategy verbatim, keep relying on the per-leaf-cutter pure-Rust primitives that the other categories will install (halfspace_clip for half-spaces; a new mesh-mesh CSG for solid cutters — see the solid-extrusion-cutter category), and harden a few corners. The algorithm is:

Stage A — extractor (mesh/boolean.rs::boolean_result, already in place):
1. For an IfcBooleanResult / IfcBooleanClippingResult node `(Operator, FirstOperand, SecondOperand)`:
   - Recurse into FirstOperand via the dispatch closure; for every returned MeshFragment::Mesh, prepend the role string "boolean_first_operand" to its source chain via `retag` (chain separator is "|").
   - Recurse into SecondOperand the same way, tagging with "boolean_second_operand".
2. Recursion is opaque: it accepts any operand kind (another IfcBooleanResult, an extrusion, a brep, a half-space, an IfcCsgSolid, an IfcMappedItem, etc.) and threads the role chain through. A wall authored as 13-deep stacking of `(((((extrusion - hs1) - hs2) - hs3) ...) - hs13)` ends up with one host segment whose source chain is `"boolean_first_operand|boolean_first_operand|...|extrusion"` (13 firsts) and 13 cutter segments whose chains terminate in `"...|boolean_second_operand|halfspace_bounded:agree"` at varying nesting depths.
3. The data structure that emerges is a flat Vec<MeshSegment> on the ProductMesh; the tree shape is preserved only as the textual chain. No tree-walking is required at consume time.

Stage B — consumer (mesh/cut_openings.rs::apply, already in place):
1. `partition_segments`: linear scan; bucket each segment as cutter iff its source chain contains the link `boolean_second_operand` anywhere. Everything else is host. O(N segments × M chain links).
2. Build a host submesh by concatenating all host-tagged segments via `assemble_submesh` (dedups vertex indices, remaps to dense 0..K). For a deep tree this is the single deepest extrusion leaf — every interior `boolean_first_operand` wrapper is transparent because all its leaves carry the wrapper in their chain but are still host.
3. Split cutters into two lists by leaf-shape kind read from the chain:
   - half-space-shaped cutters (chain contains `halfspace_plane:{agree|disagree}` or `halfspace_bounded:{agree|disagree}`) go to a Vec<(plane_point, plane_normal)> via `derive_plane_from_slab` (reads the first triangle's CCW normal, which already encodes the agreement direction since `boolean.rs` does the `!AgreementFlag` flip + Y-180° rotation when authoring the slab).
