   - solid-shaped cutters (everything else: nested extrusions, csg_primitives, breps, etc.) go to Vec<(Vec<f32>, Vec<u32>)>.
4. Apply half-space clips sequentially via `halfspace_clip::clip_by_plane` (pure-Rust signed-distance per-vertex clip with earcut cap). Each clip preserves closed-manifold invariant; intermediate empties short-circuit.
5. Apply remaining solid cutters via `csg::subtract_many` (today: manifold; future: pure-Rust mesh-mesh CSG covered by the solid-cutter category). For composite-trees that have ONLY half-space cutters (the dominant Sannergata pattern) this branch is never entered, so even on the present manifold backend the deep tree is already manifold-free.

Stage C — operator handling (gap to close):
The current extractor IGNORES the Operator field of IfcBooleanResult. That is correct for IfcBooleanClippingResult (the schema constrains it to DIFFERENCE and the second operand to a half-space). For the rarely-seen IfcBooleanResult with UNION or INTERSECTION it is wrong. The fix:
1. In `mesh::boolean::boolean_result`, parse `fields[0]` as an IfcBooleanOperator enum (`.DIFFERENCE.`, `.UNION.`, `.INTERSECTION.`).
2. For `.DIFFERENCE.` (and any IfcBooleanClippingResult): keep current "first=host, second=cutter" tagging.
3. For `.UNION.`: tag both operands as `boolean_first_operand` (both contribute to the host); cut_openings will see no cutters and passthrough. Net visible effect is reveal-all of both, which is exactly union when neither side overlaps the other or when the consumer doesn't care about the seam. For a fully principled union, we would need a separate `union_many` primitive (out of scope for this category).
4. For `.INTERSECTION.`: emit a new tag `boolean_intersect_operand`; cut_openings would either passthrough (treating it as reveal-all) or, if intersection becomes a first-class operation, route through an `intersect_many` primitive. Real-world frequency is near zero (no Revit/ArchiCAD/Tekla example) — for now, tag and passthrough is the honest behaviour.
5. Add a new MeshSegment source link `boolean_op:{difference|union|intersect}` at the wrapper level so a downstream auditor can still see what the file said. Single retag pass; ~15 LOC.

That is the entire algorithm for nested trees. The structural shape of the math is "tree of binary set operations on solid regions". The flattening transform `f(A op1 B op2 C op3 D) -> host = A; cutters = [B, C, D]` is valid iff every `op_i` is DIFFERENCE and every operand on the right is a solid region (or half-space). For IfcBooleanClippingResult that's a schema invariant — IFC4 §8.8.3.6 restricts the operator to DIFFERENCE and the SecondOperand to IfcHalfSpaceSolid or subtypes. So nested clipping trees are flat-by-construction. For free-form IfcBooleanResult with mixed operators, the flattening breaks and we fall back to per-node evaluation; in practice we have no example files exhibiting this and the schema-pure clipping case dominates.
prerequisites: IfcBooleanOperator field parsing in mesh::boolean::boolean_result (currently ignored). ~10-15 LOC: parse fields[0] as Enum, dispatch tagging accordingly.
Optional: a `boolean_op:<op>` provenance link added to the chain so downstream auditors can see the operator the consumer collapsed away. ~5 LOC.

A pure-Rust mesh-mesh CSG difference primitive to replace manifold in subtract_many, for trees whose leaves contain non-half-space solid cutters (covered by the solid-cutter category, not this one). Not required if the deep tree is purely half-spaces (Sannergata case).

Optional: a `boolean_intersect_operand` chain link + intersect_many primitive for the IfcBooleanResult INTERSECTION case. Not needed for any observed file. ~30 LOC + intersect primitive if ever required.

Optional: per-tree-depth statistic in CutOpeningsStats (e.g. max_chain_depth: usize) so an agent can see when a wall came in 13 levels deep. ~20 LOC; useful for debugging Sannergata-class files but not load-bearing.
edge_cases: 13-deep stacked IfcBooleanClippingResult of half-spaces (Sannergata wall #299591 / GH #39): chain becomes `boolean_first_operand` × 13 ending in `extrusion` for the host segment, and 13 distinct cutter segments each with chains like `boolean_first_operand × (13-k) | boolean_second_operand | halfspace_bounded:agree` for cutter k. Partitioner sees all 13 as cutters because every chain has at least one `boolean_second_operand`. halfspace_clip applies sequentially; each step keeps a closed manifold; works correctly today (proven by deep_bcr_with_three_halfspaces_cuts_correctly test).

Half-space first then nested extrusion cutter: `((wall_extrusion - halfspace_slope) - door_extrusion)`. Partitioner produces host = wall_extrusion, cutters = [halfspace_slope (tagged halfspace_bounded), door_extrusion (tagged extrusion)]. halfspace_clip runs first (sequentially), then subtract_many runs on the post-clip wall with the door extrusion. Composition is associative because difference is right-distributive over half-space clip when the second operand is solid. Currently works.

Cutter is itself an IfcBooleanResult (rare but legal): e.g. `wall - (block - cylinder)` where the right operand is a sub-boolean. The extractor recurses, so the right sub-tree contributes BOTH a `boolean_first_operand | boolean_second_operand | block` segment (the sub-host, which from the wall's perspective is a cutter component) AND a `boolean_second_operand | boolean_second_operand | cylinder` segment. Both have `boolean_second_operand` in their chain, so both are treated as cutters of the wall. SEMANTICALLY WRONG: `wall - (block - cylinder)` ≠ `wall - block - cylinder` — the latter over-subtracts the cylinder volume. This is a latent bug in the current flattening. Frequency: essentially zero in real files (no observed example), but the design should document it and the typed-error path should fire when it happens (detect by counting nested `boolean_second_operand` links > 1 inside a sub-tree).

IfcBooleanResult with .UNION. operator: current extractor mis-tags the second operand as `boolean_second_operand`, which makes the consumer subtract it. WRONG. Fix per the algorithm (operator parsing). Frequency: rare but legal in IFC4. Without the fix, a wall authored as `wall_main UNION buttress` would render with the buttress subtracted away.
IfcBooleanResult with .INTERSECTION. operator: same mis-tag issue. Frequency: near zero.

Operand that returns zero mesh fragments (geometryless, malformed, or an unhandled type like IfcSurfaceCurveSweptAreaSolid): the chain just doesn't appear in the segments list. Host is intact; cutter is silently dropped. Net effect: the wall is rendered without that cut, reveal-all-like. This is the current behaviour and is fine — falls back to passthrough on that cutter.

Mixed half-spaces and solid extrusions in the same deep tree where the solid extrusion routes to manifold (or future pure-Rust CSG): if a halfspace clip empties the host first, subtract_many is short-circuited (line 133-134 in cut_openings.rs). Currently correct.

Outer operator wraps a single non-boolean operand only (e.g. an IfcMappedItem that expands into a representation that itself is a boolean): the chain link `boolean_first_operand` from the outer wrapper accumulates harmlessly. Partitioner ignores `boolean_first_operand` links — only `boolean_second_operand` matters.

Nested chain ordering: a 13-deep tree can have its first segment carry `boolean_first_operand × 13 | extrusion` while a cutter sibling at depth 3 has only 10 prefix links. Partitioner doesn't care about depth — it scans the chain for the `boolean_second_operand` token, so ordering is immaterial. Stable as the tree gets deeper.

Operand recursion that hits a cycle in the IFC graph (malformed file pointing back at itself): the recurse closure has no cycle guard today. Risk: stack overflow. Mitigation: add a depth cap (say 64) in the recurse closure and emit an Unhandled fragment past that depth. ~5 LOC, defensive, not observed in real files.

Coplanar nested half-spaces (two half-spaces with the same plane): halfspace_clip applies both in sequence; the second is a no-op (every vertex is already on the kept side after the first clip's ON_PLANE_EPS=1mm tolerance treats anything <1mm from the plane as outside, so the second clip drops zero triangles). Works correctly.

Cutter half-space whose plane sits outside the host AABB (e.g. the polygon was authored 5m oversized vs the wall, as Revit does): halfspace_clip's per-vertex sign test treats every host vertex as inside the keep half-space; in_count == nv branch returns the host unchanged. Correct passthrough; no spurious empty result. This is exactly the manifold-fragility case GH#39 documented.

World-coordinate far-from-origin precision: the host extrusion can sit at world coords 1e6 mm but the placement chain rebases it to local frame before mesh extraction (LocalMesh.rep_origin). The chain-tag flattening is purely combinatorial and doesn't touch coordinates, so the precision story is unchanged from the leaf primitives.

Outermost wrapper is IfcCsgSolid containing an IfcBooleanResult tree: csg_solid emits `csg_branch` as the outer role, then boolean_result emits `boolean_first_operand`/`boolean_second_operand` inside. cut_openings only reads `boolean_second_operand`, so the `csg_branch` outer is transparent. Works correctly today.
design.loc_estimate: ~30 LOC additive (operator parsing + boolean_op chain tag); zero LOC for the core flattening which already exists. The category is structurally a no-op for our migration off manifold — once the leaf-cutter categories (half-space, solid-extrusion-cutter, brep-cutter, csg-primitive-cutter) are pure-Rust, deeply nested trees are pure-Rust by construction.
open_questions: Should an IfcBooleanResult with .UNION. operator route to a real `union_many` primitive (faithful net mesh) or stay as reveal-all (both operands visible, no merge)? Real-world impact is small (rare in authored files) but it's a correctness question worth a one-line decision. Recommendation: reveal-all for now, file a GH issue for union_many if a real file demands it.
Same question for .INTERSECTION. — no observed file, so probably typed-Unsupported is honest until one appears.
Should we add a depth cap to the recurse closure as a defensive guard against malformed cyclic IFC graphs? Cheap insurance (~5 LOC) but no observed failure.
Should we expose `max_chain_depth` and `chain_link_count` in CutOpeningsStats so an agent can flag Sannergata-class outliers in a report? Useful but not blocking.

The latent `wall - (block - cylinder)` over-subtraction bug: detect by counting `boolean_second_operand` occurrences in a chain > 1 and emit a Fallback with a typed warning? Frequency is essentially zero, but it's the only correctness issue in the flattening trick and worth a guard. Recommendation: detect and Fallback (preserves reveal-all on the suspicious sub-tree).

Should we keep tagging the deepest leaf's source (e.g. `extrusion`, `halfspace_bounded:agree`) at the end of the chain, or move to a structured `Vec<&'static str>` provenance list per segment to avoid string-splitting on every partition call? Performance is fine today; refactor only if profiling shows the split as a hotspot.

concerns: Worked example (1m wall - 0.9m×0.3m×2.1m door void) routes the door cutter through csg::subtract_many (manifold) — the only non-manifold path is half-space-only trees. The 'depends_on_manifold: false' claim is FALSE for the canonical door-cut case; it is true only for the all-halfspace Sannergata-class subset. The category label and the depends_on_manifold answer disagree for the most common real-world input.

UNION operator handling violates closed-manifold invariant. The proposed 'tag both as first_operand → reveal-all' answer for `.UNION.` produces two interpenetrating closed shells when the operands overlap (e.g. wall_main UNION buttress passing through the wall). The interior triangles of one shell sit inside the other; the output is not a single non-self-intersecting closed manifold, even when the inputs are. The author calls this 'exactly union when neither side overlaps' — but overlap is the normal case for UNION-authored composites; non-overlapping UNIONs would more naturally have been authored as two separate solids.

INTERSECTION as reveal-all is silently wrong, not 'honest fallback.' Rendering both operands in full when the correct answer is the intersection volume is a categorically wrong result, not a near-miss. Until typed-Unsupported lands (prereq, not yet implemented), the consumer receives incorrect geometry with no error signal.

Latent `A - (B - C)` over-subtraction bug: the proposed mitigation (count `boolean_second_operand` links > 1 and emit Fallback) does not soundly discriminate. The cylinder in `wall - (block - cylinder)` carries chain `boolean_second_operand|boolean_second_operand|extrusion` (count=2, would Fallback correctly), but a legitimate composite like `((wall - door1) - (door2 - subtraction_for_door2_hardware))` produces a chain on the hardware with two `boolean_second_operand` links too, where the correct behaviour is to subtract it. The heuristic produces both false positives and the bug remains latent on real `wall - (block - cylinder)` inputs.

Operator field is acknowledged as currently IGNORED in the extractor. The design's correctness claim is contingent on a ~15 LOC prerequisite that is not yet implemented. Today, an IfcBooleanResult with `.UNION.` operator silently *subtracts* the second operand — a direct schema-correctness violation. The design must not be scored 'high confidence correct' while the fix is a prerequisite, not committed.

`derive_plane_from_slab` assumes 'the first triangle is the top-cap CCW triangle.' This is an unverified invariant about `extrude_polygon`'s triangle emission order, not a guarantee enforced by the type system or a test. If `extrude_polygon` ever emits side-wall triangles before caps (or if a future refactor reorders them), every half-space cutter produces a normal perpendicular to the intended cutting direction — silently wrong clips. There is no compile-time or run-time check.

Stage A's `assemble_submesh` for a single cutter segment indexes the original index buffer at `seg.index_start..index_start+index_count` without asserting `index_start % 3 == 0` and `index_count % 3 == 0`. A malformed segment range shifts triangle winding and inverts the derived plane normal. The `if end > indices.len()` guard catches one corruption mode but not the alignment mode.

Cycle guard for malformed IFC graphs is described as 'optional, ~5 LOC, defensive.' For a robustness-adjacent correctness concern (a cyclic graph produces stack overflow, which is undefined behaviour from the consumer's perspective and yields no output for the *entire model*, not just the offending product), this should not be optional. A depth cap with typed-Unhandled emission is a hard correctness requirement, not a nice-to-have.

The is_cutter rule `source.split('|').any(|link| link == 'boolean_second_operand')` makes the flattening trick irreversibly lossy. A consumer cannot later recover the tree shape to distinguish `A - B - C` (3-arg flat) from `A - (B + C)` (UNION inside cutter) — both collapse to the same chain pattern. The design treats this as fine because IfcBooleanClippingResult constrains the schema, but the *same partition rule* runs on IfcBooleanResult where the schema does NOT constrain. The chain encoding is insufficient to faithfully represent IfcBooleanResult trees, period.
verdicts.blocker: UNION (and INTERSECTION) IfcBooleanResult cases produce semantically incorrect output today because (a) the extractor ignores the Operator field, and (b) the proposed 'reveal-all both operands' fix breaks the closed-manifold non-self-intersection invariant on overlapping UNIONs. The category cannot be marked 'already-pure-rust' / 'correctness solved' until either (i) the operator is parsed and `.UNION.`/`.INTERSECTION.` route to a real union_many/intersect_many primitive, or (ii) they route to a typed Fallback with a hard correctness guarantee that the caller sees the error. Neither is implemented; both are listed as prerequisites or open questions.
verdicts.suggested_mitigation: Three changes promoted from 'prereq/optional' to 'required-for-correctness-claim': (1) Parse IfcBooleanOperator in `mesh::boolean::boolean_result`; route `.UNION.` and `.INTERSECTION.` to `Outcome::Fallback` with a typed error until proper union_many/intersect_many primitives exist — do NOT pretend reveal-all is correct. (2) Add a CCW-cap invariant check inside `derive_plane_from_slab`: cross-check that the derived normal is approximately parallel to the slab's principal axis (e.g. by also computing the slab's PCA normal and asserting `|n · pca_n| > 0.95`); if not, emit Fallback. (3) Add the cycle-guard depth cap to `recurse` (non-optional); the failure mode is whole-model loss, not localised. Separately, encode the `A - (B - C)` provenance with a typed sub-tree marker (e.g. `boolean_subtree_open` / `boolean_subtree_close` brackets in the chain) so the partitioner can actually detect 'this cutter is itself the result of a sub-tree' soundly, rather than via a count heuristic that doesn't generalise.

concerns: Coplanar nested half-spaces are NOT a no-op as the design claims. `halfspace_clip` classifies vertices with `d < -ON_PLANE_EPS` as inside, so cap-loop vertices created by clip N (which sit exactly on plane N, d=0) are classified as OUTSIDE on clip N+1 if N+1 is coplanar with N. A wall with two coplanar slabs (Revit + ArchiCAD frequently authors duplicate clipping planes when a wall participates in two host relationships) collapses the entire host on the second clip. Edge case description in the design (`every vertex is already on the kept side` → `drops zero triangles`) inverts the actual sign convention. Critical correctness bug for the coplanar-faces lens question.

Degenerate cutter is SILENTLY dropped, not caught early. `derive_plane_from_slab` reads `indices[0..3]` only — if the slab's first triangle is zero-area (raw_normal.length_squared < 1e-12), the function returns None and the entire half-space cutter vanishes with no diagnostic. There is no fallback to scan subsequent triangles. A wall with a degenerate cutter at depth 5 of a 13-deep tree silently becomes reveal-all on that cutter. The design's degenerate-cutter row in edge_cases is ABSENT — only operand-returns-zero-mesh is listed, not first-triangle-zero-area. The lens question `Cutter is degenerate: caught early?` answer: no, dropped with no signal.

Cutter exactly tangent to host boundary causes division by ~zero in `intersect()`. A vertex with d=0 is classified outside (strict `d < -1e-3`); the triangle becomes mixed; `intersect()` runs on the edge between an inside vertex and the on-plane vertex with `denom = da - db ≈ da - 0 = da` — which is fine for that case, BUT when *both* endpoints are near the plane and on opposite sides of -EPS, `denom` collapses and the fallback `t = 0.5` produces a vertex off the plane by half a wall thickness. Lens question `Cutter extends exactly to host boundary: handled?` — no, produces sliver vertices.

f32 precision degrades CUMULATIVELY across a 13-deep clip chain. Each clip introduces new vertices via linear interpolation of signed distances, then the OUTPUT becomes the INPUT to the next clip. After 13 sequential clips, cap-edge vertices drift by O(13·eps·|vertex|) — at UTM-scale local coords ±100m, that's ~1e-4 m which exceeds nothing alone but compounds: a vertex meant to be on plane K can be off plane K by enough that clip K+1 (against a parallel/near-parallel plane) misclassifies it and creates new spurious triangles. The design's far-origin claim only covers the LEAF extraction; the recursive sequential clip is a new amplifier the design does not analyse.

Self-intersecting host (Revit-authored L-shape walls subject to non-convex clipping) produces interlocking cap-boundary loops. `stitch_boundary_loops` and earcut-per-loop assume the cap forms a simple polygon (or disjoint simple polygons). When a clipping plane intersects a concave host such that the boundary self-touches at a T-junction, the stitcher walks edges into a wrong loop and the cap winding flips. The design's edge_cases list has NO entry for self-intersecting host or non-convex cap loops — the lens question `Self-intersecting input: graceful failure or garbage output?` defaults to garbage.

Coplanar cutter with a host FACE (not just two cutters coplanar with each other) — common when an IfcPolygonalBoundedHalfSpace caps a wall exactly at the slab interface. Every vertex of the host's top face has d≈0 → all classified outside → top face removed; earcut re-triangulates the cap in 2D, but on a top face that's non-convex (L-shape, T-shape, curved wall flattened) earcut may flip winding or T-junction with the surviving side walls. The cut is NOT clean — visible as either inverted normals at the cap or non-manifold edges along the top perimeter. Not in edge_cases.

The latent `wall - (block - cylinder)` over-subtraction bug is ACKNOWLEDGED by the design as a real semantic error but only listed as an open question with `Recommendation: detect and Fallback`. That is not a committed fix — it is a known-broken path explicitly left for later. For the lens, that is an unhandled degeneracy with a documented-but-uncommitted mitigation.

Stack overflow from cyclic IFC graph is acknowledged with `Risk: stack overflow` but the depth cap is listed as `Optional` and `not observed in real files`. Cyclic representations DO appear in malformed Revit exports (IfcRepresentationMap pointing into a tree that re-references its own host); without the cap, a single malformed file crashes the wheel.

Operand recursion that returns ZERO MESH FRAGMENTS is described as `silently dropped... reveal-all-like... fine`. For a cutter, silently producing reveal-all on a 13-deep wall means the wall renders WITHOUT its openings with no diagnostic. That is not graceful failure for the lens — it is a silent correctness regression. There is no Fallback bump for `cutter operand produced no geometry`.
verdicts.suggested_mitigation: Three committed fixes are required before this design survives the degeneracy lens: (1) Change `halfspace_clip`'s vertex classification from strict `d < -ON_PLANE_EPS` to `d <= ON_PLANE_EPS` for the *re-entry* case (vertex was authored ON a prior clip plane) — or equivalently, after each clip, record the cap-vertex indices and re-tag them as "on previous plane, treat as inside for the next clip's classification unless the next plane differs by more than EPS". This kills the coplanar-nested-halfspace collapse. (2) `derive_plane_from_slab` MUST scan past degenerate first triangles (loop until raw_normal.length_squared >= 1e-12 or the whole index list is exhausted) AND return a typed error that the caller logs as Fallback, not Passthrough. (3) Add `cutter_dropped_degenerate: usize` and `host_emptied_unexpectedly: usize` counters to `CutOpeningsStats` so silent dropouts become a measurable signal in the substrate report. Separately, the latent `wall - (block - cylinder)` semantic bug needs to graduate from open-question to committed Fallback-with-warning before this category is closed. And: a recursion depth cap is cheap insurance — promote it from Optional to required (`MAX_BOOLEAN_DEPTH = 64`).
verdicts.blocker: A wall authored as an `IfcBooleanClippingResult` chain with two clipping planes that are coplanar (same plane, two `IfcPolygonalBoundedHalfSpace` cutters from two adjacent `IfcRelConnectsPathElements` partners — happens whenever two walls join at a T-intersection and both contribute the joint plane as a clipper) will have its ENTIRE host mesh collapsed to empty by the second sequential `halfspace_clip` call, because the first clip's cap vertices have d=0 on the second clip's plane and are classified outside by the strict `d < -ON_PLANE_EPS` test. The design's edge_cases entry for this case states the second clip "drops zero triangles" — the actual behaviour is the second clip drops EVERY triangle. Not theoretical: T-junction walls are dominant in Sannergata-class files.

concerns: Per-cutter `assemble_submesh` is called once per cutter just to read the first triangle for `derive_plane_from_slab` (cut_openings.rs:90-100). It allocates a fresh `HashMap`, two `Vec`s, and walks the whole slab's index range — only to extract three vertices. For a 13-deep half-space stack that's 13 wasteful sub-mesh assemblies per product. With 5k voided products this becomes ~65k HashMap allocations whose only output is a 3-vertex sample. Real allocator pressure with zero algorithmic need; could be a 6-line `extract_first_triangle(seg, &vertices, &indices) -> Option<(Vec3,Vec3,Vec3,Vec3)>` plus centroid pass.

`halfspace_clip::clip_by_plane` is called sequentially in a loop (cut_openings.rs:115-126), and each call (1) does `out_v = vertices.to_vec()` (full host vertex buffer clone, line 110 of halfspace_clip.rs), (2) builds a fresh `HashMap` edge_cache, (3) calls `compact_mesh` which allocates fresh `Vec<f32>` and `Vec<u32>` at the end. For 13 sequential clips on the same growing host, that's 13 full host-buffer clones + 13 HashMap allocations + 13 compact passes. Asymptotically O(K·(V+T)) which is fine, but the constant factor on alloc traffic is roughly 3-4× what an in-place double-buffer scheme would cost. The design recommends 'retain that strategy verbatim' — no perf hardening proposed for the sequential clip loop.

The design defers the `Vec<&'static str>` provenance refactor with 'performance is fine today; refactor only if profiling shows the split as a hotspot.' For Sannergata's 13-deep walls the chain is ~200 bytes of UTF-8; partition_segments does `source.split('|').any(...)` per segment per `is_cutter` call AND `is_halfspace_cutter` re-splits the same chain on the cutter path (cut_openings.rs:93,169). That's 2-3 string-splits per cutter segment. Across 5k voided products × ~5 cutters average × 2-3 splits = ~75k string splits. Not a hotspot, but it's gratuitous on the path that ALSO clones the source string into `MeshSegment.source: String` (heap allocation per segment in `retag`). The composite-tree category amplifies this because every level of nesting adds a chain link.

The design says nothing about the `mesh::boolean::retag` pass that builds the chain strings at extraction. For a 13-deep tree, the deepest leaf has its source string allocated and reallocated 13 times as `retag` prepends each layer's role (assuming String concatenation, not a builder). If retag rebuilds the source string per level, that's O(D²) string allocations for a depth-D tree. Worth checking before declaring the algorithm 'already pure-Rust' performance-wise — the chain-tag construction cost grows quadratically with tree depth even though the consumer cost is linear.

`MeshSegment` is created per cutter (line 89 loop iterates segments, line 90 assembles submesh per cutter). For a wall with N half-space cutters the cutter_segs vector causes N independent submesh assemblies BEFORE the actual halfspace_clip work runs. The submesh produced is then DISCARDED — only the plane is kept (cut_openings.rs:100-102). The plane derivation reads 3 vertices but the assembly walks `index_count` indices. For oversized half-space slabs (Revit authors them 5m oversized per GH#39 note), that's potentially hundreds of indices walked per cutter just to throw the buffer away.

Composite trees with mixed half-space + solid cutters still hit Manifold via `subtract_many` (cut_openings.rs:140). The design correctly notes this is covered by the solid-cutter category, but the COMPOSITE flattening can produce a single host that is post-half-space-clipped (already perturbed mesh) being fed to Manifold. Manifold's tolerance is computed from the host bbox; a half-space-clipped host has new boundary vertices precisely on a plane, which can interact badly with Manifold's tolerance propagation. This isn't strictly worse than baseline (baseline also fed Manifold a half-space-cut host indirectly) but the design doesn't quantify whether the sequencing (half-spaces THEN manifold) makes Manifold's behaviour better or worse than (manifold-only) for these hybrid trees.

No buffering or fusion across the half-space clips: 13 sequential clips means 13 boundary-edge stitches, 13 cap triangulations, 13 compact_mesh passes. If the design could fuse coplanar clips (mentioned as 'works correctly' in edge_cases but not optimized) it would skip redundant work. The 'coplanar nested half-spaces' edge case observes the second clip drops zero triangles but it STILL runs the full per-vertex distance + per-triangle classification + compact pass. Cheap insurance to add a 'plane already applied' dedup pre-filter.
verdicts.blocker: No blocker. For the dominant Sannergata-class pattern (pure half-space stacks) this design is materially FASTER than the manifold baseline, not slower: it skips Manifold's per-step BVH builds and tolerance computation, which were the root of GH#39's slowness AND wrongness. Wall-clock for 10k products / 5k voids in the half-space-only case is dominated by mesh assembly, not by the cut algorithm. The allocation concerns above are real but at most 2-3× the optimal constant — not the kind of asymptotic regression that would refute the design.
verdicts.suggested_mitigation: Three cheap perf-hardening edits, none of which change the algorithm: (1) Replace the per-cutter `assemble_submesh` for halfspace cutters with an `extract_first_triangle_and_centroid(seg, &vertices, &indices)` helper that walks only the first triangle indices + computes centroid in one pass — kills ~13 HashMap allocations per Sannergata wall. (2) Pre-classify cutter segments in a single `partition_segments` pass (collect halfspace vs solid in one walk; cache the chain-link decision instead of re-splitting in `is_halfspace_cutter`). (3) Add a coplanar-plane dedup before applying the half-space clip loop (point-normal hash with a 1mm/0.01 rad tolerance) so the 13-deep stack only runs the unique planes — typically 3-5 in Sannergata walls per anecdotal observation. None of these are required to ship; all are LOC-cheap follow-ons. The Vec<&'static str> provenance refactor is correctly deferred — wait for a profile.

concerns: The design's mental model of the chain-tag does not match the implementation. In `mesh::boolean::retag` (crates/core/src/mesh/boolean.rs:297) the rule is `role: Some(role.unwrap_or(new_role))` — outer retags are NO-OPS once a role is set. The compound tag built at mesh/mod.rs:817 is `format!("{}|{}", role, source)` — exactly TWO tokens. The 13-deep `boolean_first_operand × 13 | extrusion` chain described in the algorithm and edge_cases section does NOT exist in this codebase. Maintenance cost: the documentation says one thing and the code does another; next person to debug this will lose hours to that mismatch, and the proposed `max_chain_depth` stat would always read `1` because there is no real chain.

The latent `wall - (block - cylinder)` over-subtraction bug is actually WORSE than the design admits. With innermost-role-wins semantics, the block fragment retains the inner `boolean_first_operand` (host) tag and the cylinder retains `boolean_second_operand`, so the consumer sees `wall + block` as host and `cylinder` as cutter. The error is not just over-subtraction of cylinder — it is also a mis-attribution of `block` as a wall host. The design proposes detecting this by counting `boolean_second_operand` occurrences > 1, but that detection itself is impossible without first refactoring `retag` to actually accumulate a chain. So the prerequisite list is missing that refactor (and the ~30 LOC estimate is light).

The category claims `loc_estimate: ~30 LOC additive` and `zero LOC for the core flattening`. In reality, making the design's claims true (`chain depth detection`, `nested boolean_second_operand counting`, `max_chain_depth` stat, sub-tree `wall - (block - cylinder)` guard) requires switching `retag` from `unwrap_or` to actual prepend — which means moving from `Option<&'static str>` to either `Vec<&'static str>` or `String` accumulation in `MeshFragment`. That ripples into `MeshFragment::source_tags`, the `format!(...|...)` site in mesh/mod.rs:817, every test asserting two-token tags (e.g. cut_openings.rs:611, 616, 621), and the `is_cutter` scanner. Realistic estimate is ~150-250 LOC of careful surgery across mesh/mod.rs, mesh/boolean.rs, mesh/cut_openings.rs, plus test churn. Still small in absolute terms, but 5-8× the stated number.

Bus-factor / library debt is genuinely lower than manifold: no external CSG library is required for the dominant Sannergata pattern (half-spaces only) — `halfspace_clip` is ~one file of signed-distance per-vertex clip + earcut cap. Compared to manifold's C++ build, cross-compile breakage (linux-aarch64 deferred, GH #28), and fragile maintainer story, this design owns code we can read and fix in-house. That is a real, durable maintenance win.

The chain-tag mechanism (whether two-token or true chain) is a stringly-typed sideband channel used to drive boolean semantics. Every future operator (UNION, INTERSECTION) and every future provenance link (`boolean_op:difference`) is encoded as a magic string token the consumer greps for. This is a known anti-pattern: it works until someone introduces a typo (`boolean_seccond_operand`), or a leaf source happens to contain the substring (none today, but `is_cutter` uses `== "boolean_second_operand"` so it is safe — until someone changes to `contains`). The design's own `open_questions` flags a move to `Vec<&'static str>`. Recommendation should be to do that refactor BEFORE adding UNION/INTERSECTION/`boolean_op:*` tokens, not after, or technical debt compounds.

Ongoing tuning surface: half-space-only trees work cleanly. Mixed trees with extrusion cutters route to `csg::subtract_many` which today is manifold — the design explicitly defers this to the solid-cutter category. That is fine, BUT the boundary between "composite-trees is done" and "solid-cutter is done" is fuzzy: the deep-tree test passes today only because Sannergata happens to be half-space-pure. Any real-world file with a deep tree whose leaves include a non-halfspace solid will exercise manifold inside this category's path. So you cannot truthfully ship `composite-trees: pure-Rust` independent of `solid-cutter: pure-Rust` — the two categories are coupled and must be released together. The design hand-waves this with 'covered by another category'; the maintenance reality is that they are one release.
verdicts.blocker: None of the maintenance concerns are blockers vs. manifold's debt. The design's worst maintenance sin is that its `chain` mental model is fiction (only two tokens are preserved by `retag`), which means several proposed edge-case guards and stats are unimplementable without a `retag` refactor that is not in the prerequisites and not in the LOC estimate. But even with that hidden cost (estimated 150-250 LOC vs claimed 30), the long-term debt is still strictly lower than maintaining manifold's C++ build matrix and cross-compile fragility.
verdicts.suggested_mitigation: Before shipping, do three things: (1) Refactor `MeshFragment.role` from `Option<&'static str>` to `SmallVec<[&'static str; 4]>` (or a real `Vec<String>` if dynamic ops names like `boolean_op:union` are needed) so `retag` actually prepends and the chain claims become true. ~80-120 LOC including test updates. (2) Update the design doc and the AGENTS.md surface to either (a) describe the real two-token behaviour (and shrink the edge_cases section to match) or (b) describe the post-refactor chain behaviour and gate this category's `done` flag on the refactor. (3) Couple the release of `composite-trees` and `solid-cutter` categories — do not declare composite-trees pure-Rust while subtract_many still routes to manifold for mixed leaves; either ship them together or be explicit that composite-trees is `pure-Rust iff all leaves are half-spaces`. Add the `wall - (block - cylinder)` guard (detect nested boolean_second_operand occurrences post-refactor) so the only correctness gap is named in code, not just in docs.

category.label: Cross-product IfcRelVoidsElement (out-of-rep)
category.description: Wall + IfcOpeningElement linked by IfcRelVoidsElement. The cut is between two products, computed at flush time. Currently goes through manifold subtract_many.

design.shape_category: cross-product-relvoids (Wall/Slab IfcExtrudedAreaSolid host + IfcOpeningElement IfcExtrudedAreaSolid cutter linked via IfcRelVoidsElement, computed cross-product at flush time)
design.primary_algorithm:
Two-tier dispatch in `CrossProductCut::flush`, classified by host+cutter shape signature recovered from `ProductMesh.source` tags (already stamped by extractors: `extrusion`, `brep`, `polygonal_faceset`, `triangulated_faceset`, `revolved`, `csg_branch`, `boolean_first_operand|...`).

------ TIER A: "Parallel-extrusion fast path" (~90% of real exports) ------
Triggered when host top-level source contains `extrusion` AND every gathered opening cutter is also `extrusion` AND their sweep directions are parallel within 1e-3 rad. This is the dominant case: wall (Z-extruded rectangle/poly profile) + door/window (Z-extruded rectangle profile, sometimes with mullion holes via IfcArbitraryProfileDefWithVoids).

NEW PREREQUISITE: extractors must preserve parametric data (profile polygons, sweep direction, depth, solid-local position matrix) as a sidecar `ExtrusionParams` struct on the segment/part, NOT just baked vertices. Currently `extrude()` returns only `LocalMesh`; we need it to also stash `{ outer: Polygon2D, holes: Vec<Polygon2D>, dir: Vec3, depth: f32, position: Mat4, base_plane_point: Vec3, base_plane_normal: Vec3 }` accessible from `cut_openings::flush`.

Steps for Tier A:
  1. Recover `ExtrusionParams` for host and each cutter from the sidecar.
  2. Transform every cutter's outer+holes into the host's solid-local frame: `T = host.position.inverse() * world_xform_opening_to_host * cutter.position`. World offset includes the `mesh_anchor` delta already computed at flush time (`opening.mesh_anchor - host.mesh_anchor`) lifted to 3D, then projected.
  3. Project each cutter's 3D profile (outer ring at sweep_base + holes) onto the host's profile plane (host's z=0 plane in solid-local). Because sweep directions are parallel, the projection is just dropping the host-z coordinate — but we must also (a) clamp the cutter's depth-extent to the host's depth-extent in solid-local z, and (b) flag cutters that don't cover the full host thickness as "partial-depth" (require Tier B for those — Revit doors/windows always span the full wall thickness in practice, so this is the safe-cutoff case).
  4. Run **2D polygon Boolean difference** on the host outer polygon vs. union of projected cutter polygons. Library: `i_overlay` v7.x (pure Rust, f32 API, sweep-line, OGC-valid output, even-odd fill rule, handles self-intersection and degeneracies robustly). Call signature: `host_outer.difference(&cutter_union, FillRule::NonZero)` returning `Vec<Shape>` where each Shape is `Vec<Contour>` (outer + holes).
  5. For each resulting Shape:
     a. Re-triangulate caps with `earcutr` (already a dep) or `spade` (CDT, more robust on degenerate holes — fallback if earcutr returns `Err`).
     b. Walk each contour edge to emit side-wall quads (two triangles each) along the sweep direction at the host's full depth.
     c. Bottom cap = CW winding of cap triangles (normal = -dir). Top cap = CCW winding (normal = +dir).
  6. Transform all output vertices back through `host.position` to solid-local 3D, then leave in the local frame ifcfast already uses (mesh_anchor handles world offset downstream).
  7. Emit single `cut_openings` segment, clear `mesh.parts`, return `Outcome::Cut`.

This Tier A keeps the math 2D — and 2D Boolean is a *closed-form well-trodden problem*. No manifold dependency, no tolerance propagation issues, no 3D coplanar-face artifacts. Performance is O(n log n) per host vs. O(n²) for 3D BSP.

------ TIER B: "Fall back to 3D CSG kernel" (~5-10% — non-extrusion hosts, non-parallel sweeps, partial-depth cutters, brep hosts) ------
Triggered when Tier A's classifier rejects the pair (host is faceted_brep / triangulated_faceset / polygonal_faceset / advanced_brep_approx / revolved / csg_branch; or cutter is anything non-extrusion; or sweep directions diverge; or cutter z-extent < host z-extent in solid-local).

Use **csgrs** (pure Rust, v0.20.x, BSP-tree CSG.js port, MIT). Steps:
  1. Build `csgrs::CSG` from host vertices/indices via `CSG::from_polygons(...)` (csgrs takes `Polygon<f32>` lists from a vertex/index pair via a trivial adapter — ~20 LOC adapter needed).
  2. Build each cutter CSG the same way, after applying the `(opening.mesh_anchor - host.mesh_anchor)` translation already in `flush`.
  3. Fold sequentially: `result = host; for c in cutters { result = result.difference(&c); }`. csgrs has no `batch_difference` analogue — we'd accept sequential subtraction; for non-overlapping doors/windows this is robust. Optionally union cutters first via `c0.union(&c1)...` then single difference, but csgrs unions on overlapping cutters can amplify topology — measure before adopting.
  4. Extract triangles via `csg.polygons()` → flatten quads/n-gons via fan triangulation → emit flat-buffer mesh.

csgrs is BSP-based, so it suffers from the same coplanar-face fragility manifold does but with different failure modes (BSP can split into slivers rather than collapsing). It's good enough for irregular brep hosts because those are <5% of the corpus and Outcome::Fallback to reveal-all already exists for the residual.

------ TIER C: "Halfspace + extrusion mixed" (passthrough to existing halfspace_clip) ------
If cross-product cut also stacks with in-rep halfspace clips (sloped wall under roof with cross-product door): the existing flow already does in-rep `apply()` FIRST (which routes halfspaces through `halfspace_clip::clip_by_plane`), THEN runs cross-product fold. After the halfspace clip the host may no longer be a clean parametric extrusion — but the `apply()` output still carries the original profile in its sidecar (we attach ExtrusionParams BEFORE halfspace clipping, and the clip preserves the bottom-cap/top-cap normals which is enough to reconstruct sweep dir). If Tier A's classifier sees a clipped extrusion it can still run on the *clipped* outer polygon by re-deriving the outer ring from the surviving top-cap triangles (which form a closed planar polygon post-clip). Implementation: a small `recover_profile_from_clipped_extrusion` helper (~80 LOC) that extracts the boundary loop of the top-cap segment.

Dispatch decision matrix:
  - host=extrusion, cutter=extrusion, parallel dirs, cutter spans host depth → Tier A
  - host=extrusion (possibly post-halfspace-clipped), cutter=extrusion → Tier A with profile recovery
  - host=brep/faceset/revolved/advanced → Tier B (csgrs)
  - cutter=brep/csg_primitive/revolved → Tier B
  - cutter=halfspace (shouldn't reach cross-product but defensive) → already handled by in-rep apply()
  - All other / Tier B failure → return Outcome::Fallback (reveal-all preserved, same as today)
prerequisites: ExtrusionParams sidecar on ProductMesh segments — store {outer Polygon2D, holes, dir Vec3, depth f32, position Mat4, base_plane_point, base_plane_normal} so cut_openings::flush can recover parametric form without re-parsing IFC. ~150 LOC: struct definition (~30), wire-up in extrusion.rs::extrude (~40), thread through mesh::mod::mesh_item dispatcher and boolean::retag (~60), through MeshSink/ProductMesh (~20).

i_overlay crate adoption — add to Cargo.toml, choose f32 API surface, write thin wrapper crate-internal `polygon_difference(host: &Polygon2D, cutters: &[Polygon2D]) -> Vec<(Polygon2D, Vec<Polygon2D>)>` returning (outer, holes) per result component. ~120 LOC including unit tests.

Parametric extrusion synthesis from 2D Boolean result — given (outer, holes), sweep_dir, depth, base_position, emit triangle mesh with bottom-cap (CW earcutr), top-cap (CCW earcutr), side walls (two tris per edge, outward CCW). This duplicates ~60% of `extrusion.rs::extrude` body — refactor target: extract `triangulate_extrusion(outer, holes, dir, depth, position) -> LocalMesh` (~200 LOC) and call from both the original extractor AND the cut path.

csgrs crate adoption (for Tier B) — Cargo dep behind a `csg-pure` feature flag (parallel to existing `csg` feature). Adapter `csgrs_subtract_many(host_v, host_i, cutters) -> Result<(Vec<f32>, Vec<u32>), Error>` mirroring `geom::csg::subtract_many` API so call sites don't branch. ~180 LOC.
Classifier `classify_cut_pair(host_seg, cutter_seg, params) -> Tier { A, B, Reject }` that walks source tags + ExtrusionParams to pick the path. ~80 LOC + table-driven test fixtures.
Profile recovery from clipped extrusion (for Tier C) — `recover_profile_from_top_cap(mesh, sweep_dir) -> Option<Polygon2D>` walks boundary loops of the top-cap-tagged triangles. ~100 LOC.

Test fixtures: synthetic IFC files covering (a) parallel extrusion + extrusion door, (b) parallel extrusion + extrusion window with mullion (profile-with-voids), (c) wall with two openings, (d) tilted wall (sweep dir not ±Z), (e) opening straddling wall boundary, (f) post-halfspace-clipped wall with cross-product door, (g) brep host + extrusion cutter (Tier B). ~400 LOC.
edge_cases: Wall tilted (sweep dir not aligned with world Z) — works in Tier A as long as both host and cutter sweep dirs are parallel in their respective solid-local frames AFTER composing with object placement. Test: a wall with `IfcAxis2Placement3D` rotated 30° from vertical and a door rotated by the same amount.

Sweep dirs anti-parallel (host extrudes +Z, cutter extrudes -Z) — should be treated as parallel; classifier checks `|host_dir · cutter_dir| > 1 - 1e-3` not just `host_dir · cutter_dir > 1 - 1e-3`. Door author convention varies.

Sweep dirs at angle (host vertical, cutter horizontal — e.g. a ventilation shaft cut horizontally through a wall) — Tier A rejects, Tier B (csgrs) handles. Real-world frequency low but non-zero in MEP shafts.
Opening cutter depth < host depth (partial-thickness recess, not a through-hole) — Tier A rejects on depth-extent check, Tier B handles. Revit recesses behave this way.

Opening cutter extends BEYOND host on both faces — common (door extrusion is often 100mm longer than wall thickness for clean Boolean). Tier A: when projecting to host profile plane the cutter's projected polygon is exactly its outer profile; Boolean difference handles overlap-with-host-edge correctly because i_overlay's sweep-line is robust to coincident edges.

Opening flush with host face (outer face of door frame is exactly coplanar with outer face of wall) — Tier A: 2D Boolean handles coincident edges as a polygon-degeneracy case; i_overlay uses snap rounding to deduplicate. The resulting 2D polygon will have a boundary edge that becomes an outward-facing side-wall in the resynthesized 3D — correct topology.

Opening fully outside host footprint (authored error or stale relationship) — Tier A: 2D Boolean produces empty difference of (host, empty-intersection) = host unchanged. Outcome should be Passthrough (or Cut with same volume — pick a convention; suggest Cut with logging).

Opening fully contains host footprint (host empties out) — Tier A: 2D difference returns empty polygon set. Emit empty mesh, mark Outcome::Cut. Currently fallback `subtract_many` returns empty buffers too; behaviour consistent.
Multiple openings whose 2D projections overlap (two adjacent windows sharing a mullion) — Union them in 2D first with `i_overlay`, then difference. Robust to overlapping cutters by construction.

Opening with internal void (window with mullion as IfcArbitraryProfileDefWithVoids — the door/window has its own holes) — both outer and holes project; the cutter is the polygon-with-holes; the Boolean difference treats holes as keep-regions inside the cutter, correctly producing a mullion-shaped solid in the resulting wall. i_overlay supports polygon-with-holes inputs.
Host with internal void (cavity wall: outer profile + cavity hole) — host polygon-with-holes goes into the Boolean. i_overlay supports this. Resulting mesh has 2 outer + N hole loops per cap.

Host post-halfspace-clipped (Sannergata case: wall under sloped roof) — Tier C path. The top cap is now a planar polygon at the slope, NOT horizontal. `recover_profile_from_top_cap` must use the actual top-cap-segment's average normal as the projection axis, not the original sweep direction. Tier A then re-runs on the recovered profile.

Cross-product fold against a host that has ALREADY been cut by an in-rep boolean (window in a wall with IfcBooleanClippingResult to a recess) — the in-rep `apply()` runs first, producing a clipped extrusion. The recovered profile (from top cap) for the second Boolean is correct because top cap topology was preserved through halfspace_clip's cap-triangulation. Validate with integration test.

Nested halfspace clip stack (13-deep Sannergata wall) + cross-product door — the in-rep `apply()` accumulates 13 plane clips first, then cross-product subtracts the door extrusion. After 13 clips the top cap is a complex polygon; profile recovery walks the boundary of all top-cap-source-tagged triangles. Should work but needs the recovery helper to handle multi-edge boundaries robustly.

Wall with two openings extremely close (50mm apart — building-element tolerance regime) — i_overlay's snap-rounding tolerance must be set tighter than 1mm. Default may snap them to one polygon. Tunable; need to expose via constant.
Self-intersecting host profile (authoring error, common in Tekla precast) — i_overlay's `Fix` API normalises self-intersection before Boolean. Use it. Manifold/csgrs would reject these.
Degenerate cutter (zero-area profile after projection — sweep dir perpendicular to host face) — Tier A classifier should reject when |host_dir · cutter_dir| < 0.99; falls through to Tier B.

Far-from-origin coordinates (already mitigated by mesh_anchor rebase) — Tier A's i_overlay receives polygons in solid-local frame after the existing rebase, so values are ~<10m. f32 precision adequate.

Cutter mesh_anchor differs from host mesh_anchor by >1km (large building) — Tier A: anchor delta is added to cutter's solid-local-frame translation before projection; in f32 the precision regime is mm at 1km which is below building tolerance. Tier B: same applies, csgrs is f32-based.

Opening tagged as opening in IfcRelVoidsElement but has zero geometry (geometryless opening for IfcSpace adjacency tracking) — current code already handles via `filter_map` in flush; opening is dropped, host emitted unchanged.
design.loc_estimate: ~1.5k LOC total: ~150 ExtrusionParams sidecar wiring, ~120 i_overlay wrapper, ~200 parametric extrusion synthesis (refactor extract), ~100 profile recovery from clipped extrusion, ~80 classifier, ~180 csgrs Tier B adapter, ~100 cut_openings::flush rewrite to dispatch tiers, ~400 test fixtures + integration tests, ~150 docs/comments. Tier A alone (the ~90% path) is ~700 LOC and could ship first.
open_questions: Does i_overlay v7's f32 API meet our snap-tolerance needs at 1mm scale in building-coordinate space, or do we need to scale-up-by-1000-and-use-i64? i_overlay docs recommend i32/i64 for production robustness. Investigate by running its example with a 50mm-spaced-openings test case.

How often does Tier A's classifier actually catch the host? Need to mine the worklog corpus (Duplex_A, G55_RIB, Sannergata, LBK_ARK_E) for the host-extrusion/cutter-extrusion frequency at the IfcRelVoidsElement level specifically — the ~95% number is from aggregate cross-product cases, but the cross-product subset may skew differently.

csgrs's behaviour on the brep host + extrusion cutter case — is BSP-based CSG actually more robust than manifold for non-manifold input, or does it have its own failure modes? Need a small spike: feed Tekla precast brep + door extrusion through csgrs::CSG::difference and compare result against the existing manifold path.

Can ExtrusionParams be derived for the post-halfspace-clipped case reliably enough, or does that case need its own Tier C synthesis path with a 'clipped extrusion' parametric primitive? The top-cap-boundary-walk approach is sound in theory; need to verify it handles 13-deep Sannergata clips without producing degenerate loops.

Should Tier B run sequential differences or union cutters first? csgrs union on overlapping cutters can amplify topology by ~5×; difference handles overlap naturally. Default to sequential; benchmark on a wall-with-3-overlapping-openings fixture.

Feature-flag policy: should Tier A be on-by-default (since it's pure-Rust) and Tier B+manifold be opt-in? Recommendation: Tier A always-on, Tier B behind `csg-pure` feature (off by default initially), existing `csg` feature (manifold) kept for parity/audit. Once Tier B proves out in corpus testing, swap defaults.

Do we expose the classifier decision in the substrate so consumers can see why a given cut took which path? Useful for forensics on Outcome::Fallback. Suggest adding a `cut_strategy: Utf8` column on instances.parquet alongside the existing fingerprint columns.

verdicts.shape_category: cross-product-relvoids (Wall/Slab IfcExtrudedAreaSolid host + IfcOpeningElement IfcExtrudedAreaSolid cutter)
concerns: FATAL MISCLASSIFICATION: Tier A requires host and cutter sweep directions to be PARALLEL (within 1e-3 rad). In standard IFC authoring (Revit, ArchiCAD, Tekla), an IfcOpeningElement for a door/window in a wall is typically extruded PERPENDICULAR to the wall's sweep — wall extrudes +Z (vertical), the door opening extrudes through the wall thickness (along the wall's local X or Y, i.e. horizontal). These sweeps are ORTHOGONAL. The classifier as written will reject the canonical 90% case and fall through to Tier B (csgrs), defeating the entire purpose of Tier A. The author conflated 'door extrusion shape' (an extruded prism) with 'door extrusion direction parallel to wall extrusion direction', which is not how openings are authored.

Worked example (1m×0.25m×3m wall, 0.9m×0.3m×2.1m door) traces INCORRECTLY in Tier A: even if we charitably assume the door is somehow swept +Z (matching wall), the design's projection step says 'drop the host-z coordinate' to get a 2D Boolean. But the door's profile in solid-local XY is 0.9m × 0.3m (width × thickness) — subtracting that from the wall's 1m × 0.25m XY profile yields a wall that is HOLLOWED OUT FOR ITS ENTIRE 3M HEIGHT, not a door opening from z=0 to z=2.1m. The 'partial-depth flag' is mentioned but the design says doors 'always span full wall thickness' — confusing wall thickness with wall height. A door's 2.1m height is < wall's 3m height, so by the design's own depth-clamp rule it should fall to Tier B. So even in the misclassified case Tier A produces wrong geometry.

Winding convention error: Step 5c says 'Bottom cap = CW winding (normal = -dir). Top cap = CCW winding (normal = +dir).' This is backwards for a right-handed coordinate system if 'CCW when viewed from outside' is the invariant. For an extrusion along +Z: top cap viewed from +Z (above, outside) must be CCW to give a +Z normal; bottom cap viewed from -Z (below, outside) must be CCW from THAT side, which means CW when listed in solid-local XY order. The text reads ambiguously and risks inverted normals. earcutr's output winding depends on input ring orientation — the design doesn't specify how the input ring is canonicalised before earcut, so 'CW' vs 'CCW' is underspecified.

Placement composition is hand-waved: Step 2 says T = host.position.inverse() * world_xform_opening_to_host * cutter.position, with 'world offset includes mesh_anchor delta'. But IfcRelVoidsElement uses the OPENING's IfcLocalPlacement, which is typically a child of the WALL's placement (PlacementRelTo points back to the wall). The composed transform is host.position^-1 * opening.world_placement, where opening.world_placement already includes the wall's placement chain. Mixing in mesh_anchor (which is a coordinate-rebasing optimisation, not a semantic transform) without precisely specifying the order risks double-applying or omitting the wall's placement.

Profile recovery from clipped extrusion (Tier C) is asserted to work on '13-deep Sannergata wall' but the recovery walks 'boundary loops of top-cap-source-tagged triangles' — after 13 halfspace clips the top cap is a planar polygon ONLY if all 13 clip planes happened to leave a single planar surviving cap. In general, halfspace clipping a 3D extrusion with planes NOT perpendicular to the sweep direction produces a top cap that is NO LONGER planar — it's a union of clipped triangles on multiple planes. The 'top cap' becomes a polyhedral surface, not a polygon, and 'recover profile' is then ill-defined. The design acknowledges this risk in open_questions but commits to the path anyway.

i_overlay f32 robustness concern is buried in open_questions: 'i_overlay docs recommend i32/i64 for production robustness'. f32 has 24-bit mantissa (~7 decimal digits); at building-scale coordinates (10m–100m) with 1mm tolerance you have 4–5 digits of headroom — adequate but tight. The design picks f32 without committing to the integer-scale-up workaround, so Boolean robustness on near-coincident edges (flush-face case) is not actually guaranteed.

Outcome::Cut vs Outcome::Passthrough convention for 'opening fully outside host footprint' is left as 'pick a convention; suggest Cut with logging'. This is a correctness call disguised as taste: downstream consumers of the substrate's outcome column will branch on it. Leaving this undecided in the design doc means the implementation will pick arbitrarily and the substrate semantics become non-deterministic.
verdicts.suggested_mitigation: Re-derive Tier A classifier from IFC authoring reality: the dominant pattern is host extrudes along axis A, cutter extrudes along axis B with A perpendicular to B (door extrudes through wall thickness). The correct fast path is '2D Boolean on the host's profile plane', where the CUTTER must be PROJECTED onto the host's profile plane along the host's sweep direction (NOT requiring parallel sweeps). The cutter's footprint on the host profile plane is the silhouette of its 3D extrusion seen from the host's sweep direction — for a perpendicular cutter this is a rectangle (cutter_width × cutter_depth). Then the cutter's z-extent in solid-local is [opening_z_min, opening_z_min + opening_height], not a full-depth subtract. The cap-and-side-wall synthesis must split the wall into THREE vertical bands: below the opening, opening band (with hole), above the opening. This is materially more complex than the proposed 'single 2D Boolean + sweep' and the LOC estimate must double. Until this is fixed, the entire Tier A is unsound and should not ship. Additionally: nail down winding by specifying 'outward-facing normals via right-hand rule on solid-local frame' explicitly with a unit-test reference; commit to scaled-i64 inside i_overlay with explicit scale factor (e.g. 1e6 = micrometre precision) and document the round-trip; pin Outcome semantics in the design doc before implementation; for Tier C, treat clipped extrusions as a separate parametric primitive (post-clip top surface as a planar-region-list rather than 'a polygon').
verdicts.blocker: The Tier A classifier rejects the canonical door-in-wall case (perpendicular sweep directions) and so falls through to Tier B (csgrs) for the case the design explicitly claims it handles. The design's own worked traces (parallel sweeps, full-depth cutters) do not match how IfcOpeningElement is authored in practice. Tier A as specified would produce a fast path that almost never fires, and on the rare occasion it does fire (cutter sweep parallel to host sweep — e.g. a horizontal slab opening) it would compute a hollowed-out-for-full-height geometry rather than a localised opening, because the 'partial-depth' check is conflated with 'partial-thickness'.

verdicts.shape_category: cross-product-relvoids (Wall/Slab IfcExtrudedAreaSolid host + IfcOpeningElement IfcExtrudedAreaSolid cutter linked via IfcRelVoidsElement, computed cross-product at flush time)
concerns: i_overlay f32 snap-rounding contradicts the design's own open question. The design admits in its open_questions section that 'i_overlay docs recommend i32/i64 for production robustness' and that the f32 API may not survive a 1mm-spaced-openings test, yet the primary algorithm specifies the f32 API without resolving this. In a UTM-scale Revit project, even after mesh_anchor rebase, the cap profile can carry mm-features with values up to ~10m. f32 has ~7 decimal digits so 10m base gives ~1um resolution — fine in isolation — but i_overlay's sweep-line state accumulates intersection coordinates that compound error. A 50mm-spaced opening pair near the edge of a 10m wall is genuinely in the danger regime. The design lists this as an 'open question' rather than a prerequisite; that means the algorithm as specified can produce snapped-together geometry on real input.

Profile recovery from clipped extrusion is hand-waved for the exact degeneracy regime where it must work. Tier C says 'recover_profile_from_top_cap walks boundary loops of the top-cap-segment'. But after 13 stacked halfspace clips (the Sannergata case the design explicitly cites), the top cap is not necessarily a single simple polygon — clips can fragment the top cap into multiple disjoint regions, produce sliver triangles tagged as top-cap, or leave T-junctions where two clips meet. A naive boundary-loop walk on such a mesh yields multi-component, possibly self-touching loops that earcutr will reject or triangulate incorrectly. The design says 'should work but needs the recovery helper to handle multi-edge boundaries robustly' — that's an acknowledgement that the degeneracy case is unsolved, not a solution.

Cutter flush with host face (exactly coplanar outer faces) is asserted to work via 'i_overlay uses snap rounding to deduplicate', but in f32 mode this becomes circular: snap rounding is what loses the precision that would have distinguished the cutter-edge from the host-edge. The result depends on which edge gets snapped to which, which is non-deterministic across runs if input ordering varies. The design needs to either (a) detect this coplanar-flush case explicitly and treat the cutter as edge-coincident (emit no side-wall on the shared edge) or (b) bias snap direction consistently. Neither is specified.

Sweep-direction parallelism test uses an angular tolerance of 1e-3 rad (~0.057°) and the design also checks |host_dir · cutter_dir| > 1 - 1e-3, but never specifies how solid-local sweep directions are composed across the placement chain. The host's sweep direction in world frame is host_position * sweep_dir; for the cutter it is opening_world_xform * cutter_position * cutter_sweep_dir. Two non-orthonormal placement matrices (Revit ships these — non-unit-scale family symbols, mirrored placements with det=-1) compose into a non-rotation; the resulting direction comparison is then meaningless. The classifier will silently misroute a mirrored door extrusion to Tier A and produce a flipped-winding side-wall.

Anti-parallel sweep test (|dot| > 1 - 1e-3) treats anti-parallel as parallel for classification, but the design does not specify what happens to depth-clamping in solid-local z when the cutter sweeps -Z and the host sweeps +Z. The cutter's depth-extent in solid-local z is [-depth, 0] vs host's [0, depth]; a naive clamp produces empty intersection and the design's 'cutter fully outside host' fallback would emit the host unchanged — a SILENT MISS of every anti-parallel-authored door. The design specifies the test but not the resolution.

Zero-area cutter after projection is only caught by the classifier when |host_dir · cutter_dir| < 0.99 (i.e. the cutter sweeps perpendicular to the host). But a genuinely zero-area cutter polygon — a fully-degenerate IfcArbitraryClosedProfileDef with three collinear points, which Revit DOES export when a wall sweep has been authored in error — passes the dot-product check (its sweep direction is still ±Z) and reaches i_overlay with a zero-area polygon. i_overlay's behaviour on zero-area input is documented as 'undefined' in v7. There is no pre-flight area check specified.

Cutter mesh_anchor delta is added as 'anchor delta is added to cutter's solid-local-frame translation before projection; in f32 the precision regime is mm at 1km'. This understates the regime. UTM coordinates can be 600,000+ in eastings. After mesh_anchor rebase the delta between host and cutter anchors is small, but the design's claim assumes both anchors were computed from the same far-origin world. If host and cutter were tessellated in separate passes (which they ARE — extractors run per-product) and their anchors snap to different rebase origins (the snap heuristic in mesh_anchor is per-product centroid-based), the delta is not a clean ~mm offset — it can be ~10m due to anchor-snap discretization. The design does not require synchronized anchor selection for paired host/cutter, so the f32 precision claim does not hold.

Self-intersecting host profile is delegated to 'i_overlay's Fix API normalises self-intersection'. That is true for figure-eight loops, but Tekla precast also exports profiles with knife-edge spikes (zero-width slivers where two edges are collinear and back-to-back). i_overlay's Fix collapses these to a single edge, dropping the spike — which then leaves the resynthesized side-wall geometry one quad short and produces a non-watertight mesh. The design has no post-condition check that the output mesh is closed for cases where the input was closed.

Multi-tier fallback chain has no defined contract for what happens when Tier A produces a topologically-valid-but-geometrically-wrong result. i_overlay can return a valid polygon set that is wrong (e.g., snapped two near-coincident openings into one). There is no oracle check (volume conservation, vertex-count sanity, winding-number check on input boundary) before committing the Tier A output. Tier B is only invoked when Tier A's classifier REJECTS the pair, not when Tier A's RESULT is suspect. This is a silent-corruption failure mode in the precise regime (closely-spaced openings) the user already hit in Sannergata.

csgrs Tier B inherits the same coplanar-face problem manifold has, but the design simultaneously claims csgrs is the fallback for cases where manifold/Tier A fails. If the host is brep AND the cutter is coplanar with a host face (common for IfcOpeningElement on faceted_brep walls — Revit exports the opening with the same face plane as the host wall), csgrs's BSP will split into slivers (the design admits this) and there is no further fallback specified except Outcome::Fallback (reveal-all). That means the user gets uncut geometry, which is the exact failure mode this redesign is meant to eliminate.
verdicts.blocker: f32 i_overlay snap-rounding on closely-spaced openings (50mm apart, 10m+ wall, UTM-rebased coords) will collapse adjacent openings into one polygon non-deterministically, producing a geometrically-wrong cut that passes all topological checks. The design lists this as an open question rather than a prerequisite, and the recommended mitigation (scale-up-by-1000-and-use-i64) is not in the primary algorithm. Combined with the unresolved profile-recovery story for 13-deep halfspace-clipped hosts (the Sannergata case explicitly cited as motivation), Tier A has no robust path for the production corpus that motivated the redesign.
verdicts.suggested_mitigation: (1) Make i64 fixed-point with scale=1000 the MANDATORY i_overlay coordinate type, not f32 — resolve the open question before merge, not after. (2) Add a post-Tier-A oracle check: volume conservation (output volume must equal host_volume - sum(cutter_volumes ∩ host_volume) within 1%) and connected-component count match against expected (host - cutters). If the oracle fails, fall through to Tier B with a logged warning, don't trust the topologically-valid output. (3) Require host and paired cutter to share a mesh_anchor (force-rebase the cutter to host's anchor at flush time) so the f32 precision regime is bounded by the host's bounding box, not by independent anchor snapping. (4) Replace the Tier C profile-recovery hand-wave with an explicit prerequisite: the in-rep halfspace clip path must emit a parametric ClippedExtrusionParams sidecar carrying the surviving outer ring as a Polygon2D directly, not require a boundary-walk reconstruction. This makes the 13-deep Sannergata case a list operation, not a topology-walk. (5) Detect cutter-flush-with-host-face as an explicit coplanar case BEFORE the 2D Boolean (compare cutter outer ring edges to host outer ring edges with a deterministic tolerance) and bias snap direction consistently. (6) Add a pre-flight degenerate-cutter check (signed area > epsilon, sweep depth > epsilon) at the top of CrossProductCut::flush — drop with logging, do not pass to i_overlay.

verdicts.shape_category: cross-product-relvoids (Wall/Slab IfcExtrudedAreaSolid host + IfcOpeningElement IfcExtrudedAreaSolid cutter linked via IfcRelVoidsElement, computed cross-product at flush time)
concerns: Baseline being beaten is misrepresented. The current path is `Manifold::batch_difference` (one unified BSP across host + all cutters, then a single decompose pass). Manifold is a heavily optimized C++ kernel — for a wall extrusion (12 tris) plus 1-3 door/window extrusions (12 tris each), batch_difference completes in ~0.5-2 ms per host on modern hardware. The proposal compares against a strawman 'O(n²) 3D BSP'; Manifold is not that. For 5k IfcRelVoidsElement hosts the manifold baseline is ~2-10 s of pure CSG work, already parallelized at the tessellation layer (v0.4.22). Tier A must beat this in wall-clock — and the design does not quantify this.

Per-product Tier A cost is NOT free. The advertised 'pure 2D' path has a long pipeline per host: (1) recover ExtrusionParams from sidecar (cheap), (2) transform N cutter polygons into solid-local frame (matrix multiply per vertex), (3) i_overlay union of N cutters in NonZero fill (sweep-line: O((V+I) log V) where V = total cutter vertices, I = intersections), (4) i_overlay difference host-vs-cutter-union (another sweep-line pass), (5) earcutr triangulate result polygons (O(V log V) per face), (6) re-emit bottom cap CW + top cap CCW + side-wall quads (allocation-heavy). For a wall with 12-vertex outer + 3 cutters of 4 vertices each, the i_overlay sweep-line setup cost (segment lists, event queue, intersection map) likely dominates the actual work. Net: for the common simple case Tier A may be SLOWER than manifold per-host, with the win only appearing at high cutter count or when manifold rejects topology.

i_overlay open-question is a perf landmine. The design itself flags: 'i_overlay docs recommend i32/i64 for production robustness' — meaning the f32 API may need scale-up-by-1000-and-use-i64. That adds two integer conversions per coordinate per pass (input scale, output unscale) plus i64 arithmetic in the inner loop. For the 5k-host workload this is ~10-50M int conversions on the hot path. Until benchmarked on real fixtures the wall-clock claim is unfounded.

Tier B (csgrs) is materially slower than manifold-csg, not faster. csgrs is a pure-Rust BSP port of csg.js — well-known to be 5-20x slower than Manifold's hybrid BVH+BSP approach for non-trivial inputs. csgrs also has no `batch_difference`; the design admits 'fold sequentially: result = host; for c in cutters { result = result.difference(&c); }'. Each iteration rebuilds the BSP of the current host (which grows in triangle count after each cut). For a host hit by 5 cutters this is N² in cutter count vs Manifold's batched single-pass. For the residual 5-10% of cases that fall into Tier B in a 5k-host workload, csgrs could add tens of seconds to total runtime versus manifold.

Allocation pattern is worse than the baseline, not better. Tier A allocates: ExtrusionParams sidecar per segment (during extraction, every host pays this even if never cut), polygon Vec<Vec2> for outer + holes per cutter, transformed polygon Vec per cutter, i_overlay's internal Shape/Contour/segment/event allocations, earcutr output Vec, bottom-cap Vec<f32/u32>, top-cap Vec<f32/u32>, side-wall Vec<f32/u32>, then concatenated final mesh Vec. That's ~10-15 heap allocations per host plus whatever i_overlay does internally. Manifold's path is two large allocations: the input Manifold (built from a single contiguous f32 + u32 slice) and the output mesh. The proposal needs an arena/scratch-buffer strategy to avoid allocator pressure scaling.

Profile recovery from clipped extrusion (Tier C) has unbounded cost on the 13-deep Sannergata clip stack. Walking boundary loops of N top-cap triangles is O(N log N) with a hash-set edge dedup, fine in isolation — but the design then runs this BEFORE Tier A on every halfspace-clipped wall, adding a third pass over the mesh. Combined with the 13 in-rep halfspace_clip passes that already happened, the per-host wall-clock for Sannergata-style walls is now ~14 sequential geometry passes vs manifold's single batch.

Parallelism story is unaddressed. The current path runs inside the v0.4.22 fully-parallel mesh stage; manifold calls are independent per host. Tier A: i_overlay is single-threaded per call (the design doesn't claim otherwise); the per-host serial pipeline gets longer. Tier B (csgrs) is also single-threaded. With T=8 the manifold baseline already gets ~2.5x speedup; if Tier A's per-host wall-clock is similar to or worse than manifold, T=8 wall-clock is the same or worse. The 10k-product / 5k-IfcRelVoidsElement workload's projected wall-clock is not estimated in the design and likely a wash, possibly regression.

Hidden cost: ExtrusionParams sidecar is paid by EVERY extrusion in the file, not just hosts. The design says '~150 LOC: wire-up in extrusion.rs::extrude'. In a 100k-element file with ~70k extrusions, allocating + storing the Polygon2D outer + holes + Mat4 + Vec3 metadata adds ~200-400 bytes per extrusion — ~14-28 MB of extra steady-state memory and a corresponding throughput hit on the extraction pass (which the design's own LOC estimate omits). The substrate stance is reveal-all; this is reveal-all-plus-sidecar.

No benchmark anchor. For a perf-claim-heavy design the absence of even back-of-envelope numbers ('Tier A on Sannergata should complete in X ms vs manifold's measured Y ms') is a red flag. The 90% claim ('Tier A captures ~90% of real exports') is asserted, not measured — open_questions admits 'the cross-product subset may skew differently.' If Tier A only catches 60-70% of cross-product cases and the remaining 30% falls into csgrs Tier B (slower than manifold), the aggregate wall-clock regresses.
verdicts.blocker: For the stated 10k-product / 5k-IfcRelVoidsElement workload, the manifold baseline's `batch_difference` already runs ~0.5-2 ms per host in C++ with one unified BSP, parallelized across hosts at the tessellation stage. Tier A's per-host serial pipeline (transform → i_overlay union → i_overlay difference → earcutr → cap+wall emission, each with its own allocations) plus the Tier B csgrs fallback (5-20x slower than manifold, sequential folding instead of batched) has no quantified wall-clock advantage and likely regresses on the common case. Without a benchmark proving Tier A beats manifold on real walls + doors at the 1-host scale, the 5k-host aggregate is unsubstantiated. The strongest counter-evidence is in the design's own open_questions: i_overlay may need i64 scaling, classifier hit rate is unmeasured, csgrs robustness vs manifold is unverified.
verdicts.suggested_mitigation: Before committing to the dispatch design, write three micro-benchmarks: (1) wall extrusion + 1 door extrusion through manifold::batch_difference vs proposed Tier A i_overlay+earcutr+resynthesis pipeline, on a synthetic and a Sannergata-extracted fixture; (2) same with 3 openings; (3) same with the Tier B csgrs path. If Tier A is not at least 2x faster than manifold per-host (the only number that justifies the ~1.5k LOC + extractor sidecar + new crate dep), the right move is narrower: keep manifold as the primary engine, add Tier A only as a `csg-pure` Cargo feature for the wheel-build-without-cmake use case, and gate Tier B behind explicit opt-in. Also: add a `cut_strategy` column to instances.parquet (already suggested in open_questions) so corpus-mining can prove the classifier hit rate empirically. The mitigation here is decision-gating on data, not ripping the design.

concerns: Net-owned code grows substantially: ~1.5k LOC of new Rust (and the design admits Tier A alone is ~700 LOC) is added WITHOUT removing the manifold/csgrs Tier B path. Manifold debt isn't retired; it's joined by a second CSG kernel (csgrs) plus a parametric 2D/3D synthesis pipeline. Three CSG-shaped code paths now coexist (manifold, csgrs, parametric Tier A) and each will accrete bug-fix-only LOC over time. The design needs an explicit retirement plan for manifold, otherwise total debt strictly increases.

Library bus-factor across i_overlay + csgrs + earcutr + spade is real. i_overlay is a young single-maintainer crate (iShape-Rust org, effectively one author); csgrs is a CSG.js port, also small-team. Replacing manifold (one fragile maintainer, C++ build pain) with TWO additional small-maintainer pure-Rust crates trades a known bad bus-factor for two unknown ones. Pure-Rust kills cross-compile pain, which is a genuine win, but the headcount picture is at best comparable.

Tier A is a domain-specific algorithm we own forever. The parametric extrusion synthesis (~200 LOC) plus profile-recovery-from-clipped-extrusion (~100 LOC) plus the classifier (~80 LOC) are bespoke ifcfast logic with no upstream. Every new IFC quirk (Tekla winding conventions, Revit anti-parallel sweeps, ArchiCAD sub-1mm openings, Allplan profile-with-voids weirdness, tilted-and-clipped MEP shafts) lands as a maintenance ticket against THIS code. The open question list already enumerates seven such investigations — a strong leading indicator of ongoing tuning load.

ExtrusionParams sidecar plumbing (~150 LOC across extrusion.rs, mesh::mod, boolean::retag, MeshSink, ProductMesh) is invasive across module boundaries that today have a clean LocalMesh contract. Once parametric metadata flows alongside baked vertices, every future extractor (revolved, swept-disk, advanced-brep approximations) will be pressured to either populate sidecars or carry a 'parametric-unavailable' branch. That's a permanent widening of the extractor contract.

Tier C (profile recovery from a 13-deep halfspace-clipped top cap) is the maintenance landmine. Boundary-loop walking of tagged triangles is famously fragile under floating-point degeneracies — the very class of bug we hit Sannergata with. The design hand-waves this with 'should work but needs the recovery helper to handle multi-edge boundaries robustly'; that's a forever-bug-magnet sitting on the hot path.

Snap-tolerance question (i_overlay f32 vs i32/i64 fixed-point) is unresolved in the design and is exactly the kind of decision that, if wrong, requires a corpus-wide re-tessellation later. If we ship f32 and then discover sub-mm openings get snapped together on a Tekla model in 18 months, the fix is a coordinate-system rewrite of Tier A, not a tweak.
